speeches.speech: In Troy, there lies the scene. From isles of Greece The princes orgulous, their high blood chafed, Have to the port of Athens sent their ships, Fraught with the ministers and instruments Of cruel war: sixty and nine, that wore Their crownets regal, from the Athenian bay Put forth toward Phrygia; and their vow is made To ransack Troy, within whose strong immures The ravish'd Helen, Menelaus' queen, With wanton Paris sleeps; and that's the quarrel. To Tenedos they come; And the deep-drawing barks do there disgorge Their warlike fraughtage: now on Dardan plains The fresh and yet unbruised Greeks do pitch Their brave pavilions: Priam's six-gated city, Dardan, and Tymbria, Helias, Chetas, Troien, And Antenorides, with massy staples And corresponsive and fulfilling bolts, Sperr up the sons of Troy. Now expectation, tickling skittish spirits, On one and other side, Trojan and Greek, Sets all on hazard: and hither am I come A prologue arm'd, but not in confidence Of author's pen or actor's voice, but suited In like conditions as our argument, To tell you, fair beholders, that our play Leaps o'er the vaunt and firstlings of those broils, Beginning in the middle, starting thence away To what may be digested in a play. Like or find fault; do as your pleasures are: Now good or bad, 'tis but the chance of war.
lines: In Troy, there lies the scene. From isles of Greece
The princes orgulous, their high blood chafed,
Have to the port of Athens sent their ships,
Fraught with the ministers and instruments
Of cruel war: sixty and nine, that wore
Their crownets regal, from the Athenian bay
Put forth toward Phrygia; and their vow is made
To ransack Troy, within whose strong immures
The ravish'd Helen, Menelaus' queen,
With wanton Paris sleeps; and that's the quarrel.
To Tenedos they come;
And the deep-drawing barks do there disgorge
Their warlike fraughtage: now on Dardan plains
The fresh and yet unbruised Greeks do pitch
Their brave pavilions: Priam's six-gated city,
Dardan, and Tymbria, Helias, Chetas, Troien,
And Antenorides, with massy staples
And corresponsive and fulfilling bolts,
Sperr up the sons of Troy.
Now expectation, tickling skittish spirits,
On one and other side, Trojan and Greek,
Sets all on hazard: and hither am I come
A prologue arm'd, but not in confidence
Of author's pen or actor's voice, but suited
In like conditions as our argument,
To tell you, fair beholders, that our play
Leaps o'er the vaunt and firstlings of those broils,
Beginning in the middle, starting thence away
To what may be digested in a play.
Like or find fault; do as your pleasures are:
Now good or bad, 'tis but the chance of war.

speeches.speech: Call here my varlet; I'll unarm again: Why should I war without the walls of Troy, That find such cruel battle here within? Each Trojan that is master of his heart, Let him to field; Troilus, alas! hath none.
lines: Call here my varlet; I'll unarm again:
Why should I war without the walls of Troy,
That find such cruel battle here within?
Each Trojan that is master of his heart,
Let him to field; Troilus, alas! hath none.

speeches.speech: Will this gear ne'er be mended?
lines: Will this gear ne'er be mended?

speeches.speech: The Greeks are strong and skilful to their strength, Fierce to their skill and to their fierceness valiant; But I am weaker than a woman's tear, Tamer than sleep, fonder than ignorance, Less valiant than the virgin in the night And skilless as unpractised infancy.
lines: The Greeks are strong and skilful to their strength,
Fierce to their skill and to their fierceness valiant;
But I am weaker than a woman's tear,
Tamer than sleep, fonder than ignorance,
Less valiant than the virgin in the night
And skilless as unpractised infancy.

speeches.speech: Well, I have told you enough of this: for my part, I'll not meddle nor make no further. He that will have a cake out of the wheat must needs tarry the grinding.
lines: Well, I have told you enough of this: for my part,
I'll not meddle nor make no further. He that will
have a cake out of the wheat must needs tarry the grinding.

speeches.speech: Have I not tarried?
lines: Have I not tarried?

speeches.speech: Ay, the grinding; but you must tarry the bolting.
lines: Ay, the grinding; but you must tarry
the bolting.

speeches.speech: Have I not tarried?
lines: Have I not tarried?

speeches.speech: Ay, the bolting, but you must tarry the leavening.
lines: Ay, the bolting, but you must tarry the leavening.

speeches.speech: Still have I tarried.
lines: Still have I tarried.

speeches.speech: Ay, to the leavening; but here's yet in the word 'hereafter' the kneading, the making of the cake, the heating of the oven and the baking; nay, you must stay the cooling too, or you may chance to burn your lips.
lines: Ay, to the leavening; but here's yet in the word
'hereafter' the kneading, the making of the cake, the
heating of the oven and the baking; nay, you must
stay the cooling too, or you may chance to burn your lips.

speeches.speech: Patience herself, what goddess e'er she be, Doth lesser blench at sufferance than I do. At Priam's royal table do I sit; And when fair Cressid comes into my thoughts,-- So, traitor! 'When she comes!' When is she thence?
lines: Patience herself, what goddess e'er she be,
Doth lesser blench at sufferance than I do.
At Priam's royal table do I sit;
And when fair Cressid comes into my thoughts,--
So, traitor! 'When she comes!' When is she thence?

speeches.speech: Well, she looked yesternight fairer than ever I saw her look, or any woman else.
lines: Well, she looked yesternight fairer than ever I saw
her look, or any woman else.

speeches.speech: I was about to tell thee:--when my heart, As wedged with a sigh, would rive in twain, Lest Hector or my father should perceive me, I have, as when the sun doth light a storm, Buried this sigh in wrinkle of a smile: But sorrow, that is couch'd in seeming gladness, Is like that mirth fate turns to sudden sadness.
lines: I was about to tell thee:--when my heart,
As wedged with a sigh, would rive in twain,
Lest Hector or my father should perceive me,
I have, as when the sun doth light a storm,
Buried this sigh in wrinkle of a smile:
But sorrow, that is couch'd in seeming gladness,
Is like that mirth fate turns to sudden sadness.

speeches.speech: An her hair were not somewhat darker than Helen's-- well, go to--there were no more comparison between the women: but, for my part, she is my kinswoman; I would not, as they term it, praise her: but I would somebody had heard her talk yesterday, as I did. I will not dispraise your sister Cassandra's wit, but--
lines: An her hair were not somewhat darker than Helen's--
well, go to--there were no more comparison between
the women: but, for my part, she is my kinswoman; I
would not, as they term it, praise her: but I would
somebody had heard her talk yesterday, as I did. I
will not dispraise your sister Cassandra's wit, but--

speeches.speech: O Pandarus! I tell thee, Pandarus,-- When I do tell thee, there my hopes lie drown'd, Reply not in how many fathoms deep They lie indrench'd. I tell thee I am mad In Cressid's love: thou answer'st 'she is fair;' Pour'st in the open ulcer of my heart Her eyes, her hair, her cheek, her gait, her voice, Handlest in thy discourse, O, that her hand, In whose comparison all whites are ink, Writing their own reproach, to whose soft seizure The cygnet's down is harsh and spirit of sense Hard as the palm of ploughman: this thou tell'st me, As true thou tell'st me, when I say I love her; But, saying thus, instead of oil and balm, Thou lay'st in every gash that love hath given me The knife that made it.
lines: O Pandarus! I tell thee, Pandarus,--
When I do tell thee, there my hopes lie drown'd,
Reply not in how many fathoms deep
They lie indrench'd. I tell thee I am mad
In Cressid's love: thou answer'st 'she is fair;'
Pour'st in the open ulcer of my heart
Her eyes, her hair, her cheek, her gait, her voice,
Handlest in thy discourse, O, that her hand,
In whose comparison all whites are ink,
Writing their own reproach, to whose soft seizure
The cygnet's down is harsh and spirit of sense
Hard as the palm of ploughman: this thou tell'st me,
As true thou tell'st me, when I say I love her;
But, saying thus, instead of oil and balm,
Thou lay'st in every gash that love hath given me
The knife that made it.

speeches.speech: I speak no more than truth.
lines: I speak no more than truth.

speeches.speech: Thou dost not speak so much.
lines: Thou dost not speak so much.

speeches.speech: Faith, I'll not meddle in't. Let her be as she is: if she be fair, 'tis the better for her; an she be not, she has the mends in her own hands.
lines: Faith, I'll not meddle in't. Let her be as she is:
if she be fair, 'tis the better for her; an she be
not, she has the mends in her own hands.

speeches.speech: Good Pandarus, how now, Pandarus!
lines: Good Pandarus, how now, Pandarus!

speeches.speech: I have had my labour for my travail; ill-thought on of her and ill-thought on of you; gone between and between, but small thanks for my labour.
lines: I have had my labour for my travail; ill-thought on of
her and ill-thought on of you; gone between and
between, but small thanks for my labour.

speeches.speech: What, art thou angry, Pandarus? what, with me?
lines: What, art thou angry, Pandarus? what, with me?

speeches.speech: Because she's kin to me, therefore she's not so fair as Helen: an she were not kin to me, she would be as fair on Friday as Helen is on Sunday. But what care I? I care not an she were a black-a-moor; 'tis all one to me.
lines: Because she's kin to me, therefore she's not so fair
as Helen: an she were not kin to me, she would be as
fair on Friday as Helen is on Sunday. But what care
I? I care not an she were a black-a-moor; 'tis all one to me.

speeches.speech: Say I she is not fair?
lines: Say I she is not fair?

speeches.speech: I do not care whether you do or no. She's a fool to stay behind her father; let her to the Greeks; and so I'll tell her the next time I see her: for my part, I'll meddle nor make no more i' the matter.
lines: I do not care whether you do or no. She's a fool to
stay behind her father; let her to the Greeks; and so
I'll tell her the next time I see her: for my part,
I'll meddle nor make no more i' the matter.

speeches.speech: Pandarus,--
lines: Pandarus,--

speeches.speech: Not I.
lines: Not I.

speeches.speech: Sweet Pandarus,--
lines: Sweet Pandarus,--

speeches.speech: Pray you, speak no more to me: I will leave all as I found it, and there an end.
lines: Pray you, speak no more to me: I will leave all as I
found it, and there an end.

speeches.speech: Peace, you ungracious clamours! peace, rude sounds! Fools on both sides! Helen must needs be fair, When with your blood you daily paint her thus. I cannot fight upon this argument; It is too starved a subject for my sword. But Pandarus,--O gods, how do you plague me! I cannot come to Cressid but by Pandar; And he's as tetchy to be woo'd to woo. As she is stubborn-chaste against all suit. Tell me, Apollo, for thy Daphne's love, What Cressid is, what Pandar, and what we? Her bed is India; there she lies, a pearl: Between our Ilium and where she resides, Let it be call'd the wild and wandering flood, Ourself the merchant, and this sailing Pandar Our doubtful hope, our convoy and our bark.
lines: Peace, you ungracious clamours! peace, rude sounds!
Fools on both sides! Helen must needs be fair,
When with your blood you daily paint her thus.
I cannot fight upon this argument;
It is too starved a subject for my sword.
But Pandarus,--O gods, how do you plague me!
I cannot come to Cressid but by Pandar;
And he's as tetchy to be woo'd to woo.
As she is stubborn-chaste against all suit.
Tell me, Apollo, for thy Daphne's love,
What Cressid is, what Pandar, and what we?
Her bed is India; there she lies, a pearl:
Between our Ilium and where she resides,
Let it be call'd the wild and wandering flood,
Ourself the merchant, and this sailing Pandar
Our doubtful hope, our convoy and our bark.

speeches.speech: How now, Prince Troilus! wherefore not afield?
lines: How now, Prince Troilus! wherefore not afield?

speeches.speech: Because not there: this woman's answer sorts, For womanish it is to be from thence. What news, AEneas, from the field to-day?
lines: Because not there: this woman's answer sorts,
For womanish it is to be from thence.
What news, AEneas, from the field to-day?

speeches.speech: That Paris is returned home and hurt.
lines: That Paris is returned home and hurt.

speeches.speech: By whom, AEneas?
lines: By whom, AEneas?

speeches.speech: Troilus, by Menelaus.
lines: Troilus, by Menelaus.

speeches.speech: Let Paris bleed; 'tis but a scar to scorn; Paris is gored with Menelaus' horn.
lines: Let Paris bleed; 'tis but a scar to scorn;
Paris is gored with Menelaus' horn.

speeches.speech: Hark, what good sport is out of town to-day!
lines: Hark, what good sport is out of town to-day!

speeches.speech: Better at home, if 'would I might' were 'may.' But to the sport abroad: are you bound thither?
lines: Better at home, if 'would I might' were 'may.'
But to the sport abroad: are you bound thither?

speeches.speech: In all swift haste.
lines: In all swift haste.

speeches.speech: Come, go we then together.
lines: Come, go we then together.

speeches.speech: Who were those went by?
lines: Who were those went by?

speeches.speech: Queen Hecuba and Helen.
lines: Queen Hecuba and Helen.

speeches.speech: And whither go they?
lines: And whither go they?

speeches.speech: Up to the eastern tower, Whose height commands as subject all the vale, To see the battle. Hector, whose patience Is, as a virtue, fix'd, to-day was moved: He chid Andromache and struck his armourer, And, like as there were husbandry in war, Before the sun rose he was harness'd light, And to the field goes he; where every flower Did, as a prophet, weep what it foresaw In Hector's wrath.
lines: Up to the eastern tower,
Whose height commands as subject all the vale,
To see the battle. Hector, whose patience
Is, as a virtue, fix'd, to-day was moved:
He chid Andromache and struck his armourer,
And, like as there were husbandry in war,
Before the sun rose he was harness'd light,
And to the field goes he; where every flower
Did, as a prophet, weep what it foresaw
In Hector's wrath.

speeches.speech: What was his cause of anger?
lines: What was his cause of anger?

speeches.speech: The noise goes, this: there is among the Greeks A lord of Trojan blood, nephew to Hector; They call him Ajax.
lines: The noise goes, this: there is among the Greeks
A lord of Trojan blood, nephew to Hector;
They call him Ajax.

speeches.speech: Good; and what of him?
lines: Good; and what of him?

speeches.speech: They say he is a very man per se, And stands alone.
lines: They say he is a very man per se,
And stands alone.

speeches.speech: So do all men, unless they are drunk, sick, or have no legs.
lines: So do all men, unless they are drunk, sick, or have no legs.

speeches.speech: This man, lady, hath robbed many beasts of their particular additions; he is as valiant as the lion, churlish as the bear, slow as the elephant: a man into whom nature hath so crowded humours that his valour is crushed into folly, his folly sauced with discretion: there is no man hath a virtue that he hath not a glimpse of, nor any man an attaint but he carries some stain of it: he is melancholy without cause, and merry against the hair: he hath the joints of every thing, but everything so out of joint that he is a gouty Briareus, many hands and no use, or purblind Argus, all eyes and no sight.
lines: This man, lady, hath robbed many beasts of their
particular additions; he is as valiant as the lion,
churlish as the bear, slow as the elephant: a man
into whom nature hath so crowded humours that his
valour is crushed into folly, his folly sauced with
discretion: there is no man hath a virtue that he
hath not a glimpse of, nor any man an attaint but he
carries some stain of it: he is melancholy without
cause, and merry against the hair: he hath the
joints of every thing, but everything so out of joint
that he is a gouty Briareus, many hands and no use,
or purblind Argus, all eyes and no sight.

speeches.speech: But how should this man, that makes me smile, make Hector angry?
lines: But how should this man, that makes
me smile, make Hector angry?

speeches.speech: They say he yesterday coped Hector in the battle and struck him down, the disdain and shame whereof hath ever since kept Hector fasting and waking.
lines: They say he yesterday coped Hector in the battle and
struck him down, the disdain and shame whereof hath
ever since kept Hector fasting and waking.

speeches.speech: Who comes here?
lines: Who comes here?

speeches.speech: Madam, your uncle Pandarus.
lines: Madam, your uncle Pandarus.

speeches.speech: Hector's a gallant man.
lines: Hector's a gallant man.

speeches.speech: As may be in the world, lady.
lines: As may be in the world, lady.

speeches.speech: What's that? what's that?
lines: What's that? what's that?

speeches.speech: Good morrow, uncle Pandarus.
lines: Good morrow, uncle Pandarus.

speeches.speech: Good morrow, cousin Cressid: what do you talk of? Good morrow, Alexander. How do you, cousin? When were you at Ilium?
lines: Good morrow, cousin Cressid: what do you talk of?
Good morrow, Alexander. How do you, cousin? When
were you at Ilium?

speeches.speech: This morning, uncle.
lines: This morning, uncle.

speeches.speech: What were you talking of when I came? Was Hector armed and gone ere ye came to Ilium? Helen was not up, was she?
lines: What were you talking of when I came? Was Hector
armed and gone ere ye came to Ilium? Helen was not
up, was she?

speeches.speech: Hector was gone, but Helen was not up.
lines: Hector was gone, but Helen was not up.

speeches.speech: Even so: Hector was stirring early.
lines: Even so: Hector was stirring early.

speeches.speech: That were we talking of, and of his anger.
lines: That were we talking of, and of his anger.

speeches.speech: Was he angry?
lines: Was he angry?

speeches.speech: So he says here.
lines: So he says here.

speeches.speech: True, he was so: I know the cause too: he'll lay about him to-day, I can tell them that: and there's Troilus will not come far behind him: let them take heed of Troilus, I can tell them that too.
lines: True, he was so: I know the cause too: he'll lay
about him to-day, I can tell them that: and there's
Troilus will not come far behind him: let them take
heed of Troilus, I can tell them that too.

speeches.speech: What, is he angry too?
lines: What, is he angry too?

speeches.speech: Who, Troilus? Troilus is the better man of the two.
lines: Who, Troilus? Troilus is the better man of the two.

speeches.speech: O Jupiter! there's no comparison.
lines: O Jupiter! there's no comparison.

speeches.speech: What, not between Troilus and Hector? Do you know a man if you see him?
lines: What, not between Troilus and Hector? Do you know a
man if you see him?

speeches.speech: Ay, if I ever saw him before and knew him.
lines: Ay, if I ever saw him before and knew him.

speeches.speech: Well, I say Troilus is Troilus.
lines: Well, I say Troilus is Troilus.

speeches.speech: Then you say as I say; for, I am sure, he is not Hector.
lines: Then you say as I say; for, I am sure, he is not Hector.

speeches.speech: No, nor Hector is not Troilus in some degrees.
lines: No, nor Hector is not Troilus in some degrees.

speeches.speech: 'Tis just to each of them; he is himself.
lines: 'Tis just to each of them; he is himself.

speeches.speech: Himself! Alas, poor Troilus! I would he were.
lines: Himself! Alas, poor Troilus! I would he were.

speeches.speech: So he is.
lines: So he is.

speeches.speech: Condition, I had gone barefoot to India.
lines: Condition, I had gone barefoot to India.

speeches.speech: He is not Hector.
lines: He is not Hector.

speeches.speech: Himself! no, he's not himself: would a' were himself! Well, the gods are above; time must friend or end: well, Troilus, well: I would my heart were in her body. No, Hector is not a better man than Troilus.
lines: Himself! no, he's not himself: would a' were
himself! Well, the gods are above; time must friend
or end: well, Troilus, well: I would my heart were
in her body. No, Hector is not a better man than Troilus.

speeches.speech: Excuse me.
lines: Excuse me.

speeches.speech: He is elder.
lines: He is elder.

speeches.speech: Pardon me, pardon me.
lines: Pardon me, pardon me.

speeches.speech: Th' other's not come to't; you shall tell me another tale, when th' other's come to't. Hector shall not have his wit this year.
lines: Th' other's not come to't; you shall tell me another
tale, when th' other's come to't. Hector shall not
have his wit this year.

speeches.speech: He shall not need it, if he have his own.
lines: He shall not need it, if he have his own.

speeches.speech: Nor his qualities.
lines: Nor his qualities.

speeches.speech: No matter.
lines: No matter.

speeches.speech: Nor his beauty.
lines: Nor his beauty.

speeches.speech: 'Twould not become him; his own's better.
lines: 'Twould not become him; his own's better.

speeches.speech: You have no judgment, niece: Helen herself swore th' other day, that Troilus, for a brown favour--for so 'tis, I must confess,-- not brown neither,--
lines: You have no judgment, niece: Helen
herself swore th' other day, that Troilus, for
a brown favour--for so 'tis, I must confess,--
not brown neither,--

speeches.speech: No, but brown.
lines: No, but brown.

speeches.speech: 'Faith, to say truth, brown and not brown.
lines: 'Faith, to say truth, brown and not brown.

speeches.speech: To say the truth, true and not true.
lines: To say the truth, true and not true.

speeches.speech: She praised his complexion above Paris.
lines: She praised his complexion above Paris.

speeches.speech: Why, Paris hath colour enough.
lines: Why, Paris hath colour enough.

speeches.speech: So he has.
lines: So he has.

speeches.speech: Then Troilus should have too much: if she praised him above, his complexion is higher than his; he having colour enough, and the other higher, is too flaming a praise for a good complexion. I had as lief Helen's golden tongue had commended Troilus for a copper nose.
lines: Then Troilus should have too much: if she praised
him above, his complexion is higher than his; he
having colour enough, and the other higher, is too
flaming a praise for a good complexion. I had as
lief Helen's golden tongue had commended Troilus for
a copper nose.

speeches.speech: I swear to you. I think Helen loves him better than Paris.
lines: I swear to you. I think Helen loves him better than Paris.

speeches.speech: Then she's a merry Greek indeed.
lines: Then she's a merry Greek indeed.

speeches.speech: Nay, I am sure she does. She came to him th' other day into the compassed window,--and, you know, he has not past three or four hairs on his chin,--
lines: Nay, I am sure she does. She came to him th' other
day into the compassed window,--and, you know, he
has not past three or four hairs on his chin,--

speeches.speech: Indeed, a tapster's arithmetic may soon bring his particulars therein to a total.
lines: Indeed, a tapster's arithmetic may soon bring his
particulars therein to a total.

speeches.speech: Why, he is very young: and yet will he, within three pound, lift as much as his brother Hector.
lines: Why, he is very young: and yet will he, within
three pound, lift as much as his brother Hector.

speeches.speech: Is he so young a man and so old a lifter?
lines: Is he so young a man and so old a lifter?

speeches.speech: But to prove to you that Helen loves him: she came and puts me her white hand to his cloven chin--
lines: But to prove to you that Helen loves him: she came
and puts me her white hand to his cloven chin--

speeches.speech: Juno have mercy! how came it cloven?
lines: Juno have mercy! how came it cloven?

speeches.speech: Why, you know 'tis dimpled: I think his smiling becomes him better than any man in all Phrygia.
lines: Why, you know 'tis dimpled: I think his smiling
becomes him better than any man in all Phrygia.

speeches.speech: O, he smiles valiantly.
lines: O, he smiles valiantly.

speeches.speech: Does he not?
lines: Does he not?

speeches.speech: O yes, an 'twere a cloud in autumn.
lines: O yes, an 'twere a cloud in autumn.

speeches.speech: Why, go to, then: but to prove to you that Helen loves Troilus,--
lines: Why, go to, then: but to prove to you that Helen
loves Troilus,--

speeches.speech: Troilus will stand to the proof, if you'll prove it so.
lines: Troilus will stand to the proof, if you'll
prove it so.

speeches.speech: Troilus! why, he esteems her no more than I esteem an addle egg.
lines: Troilus! why, he esteems her no more than I esteem
an addle egg.

speeches.speech: If you love an addle egg as well as you love an idle head, you would eat chickens i' the shell.
lines: If you love an addle egg as well as you love an idle
head, you would eat chickens i' the shell.

speeches.speech: I cannot choose but laugh, to think how she tickled his chin: indeed, she has a marvellous white hand, I must needs confess,--
lines: I cannot choose but laugh, to think how she tickled
his chin: indeed, she has a marvellous white hand, I
must needs confess,--

speeches.speech: Without the rack.
lines: Without the rack.

speeches.speech: And she takes upon her to spy a white hair on his chin.
lines: And she takes upon her to spy a white hair on his chin.

speeches.speech: Alas, poor chin! many a wart is richer.
lines: Alas, poor chin! many a wart is richer.

speeches.speech: But there was such laughing! Queen Hecuba laughed that her eyes ran o'er.
lines: But there was such laughing! Queen Hecuba laughed
that her eyes ran o'er.

speeches.speech: With mill-stones.
lines: With mill-stones.

speeches.speech: And Cassandra laughed.
lines: And Cassandra laughed.

speeches.speech: But there was more temperate fire under the pot of her eyes: did her eyes run o'er too?
lines: But there was more temperate fire under the pot of
her eyes: did her eyes run o'er too?

speeches.speech: And Hector laughed.
lines: And Hector laughed.

speeches.speech: At what was all this laughing?
lines: At what was all this laughing?

speeches.speech: Marry, at the white hair that Helen spied on Troilus' chin.
lines: Marry, at the white hair that Helen spied on Troilus' chin.

speeches.speech: An't had been a green hair, I should have laughed too.
lines: An't had been a green hair, I should have laughed
too.

speeches.speech: They laughed not so much at the hair as at his pretty answer.
lines: They laughed not so much at the hair as at his pretty answer.

speeches.speech: What was his answer?
lines: What was his answer?

speeches.speech: Quoth she, 'Here's but two and fifty hairs on your chin, and one of them is white.
lines: Quoth she, 'Here's but two and fifty hairs on your
chin, and one of them is white.

speeches.speech: This is her question.
lines: This is her question.

speeches.speech: That's true; make no question of that. 'Two and fifty hairs' quoth he, 'and one white: that white hair is my father, and all the rest are his sons.' 'Jupiter!' quoth she, 'which of these hairs is Paris, my husband? 'The forked one,' quoth he, 'pluck't out, and give it him.' But there was such laughing! and Helen so blushed, an Paris so chafed, and all the rest so laughed, that it passed.
lines: That's true; make no question of that. 'Two and
fifty hairs' quoth he, 'and one white: that white
hair is my father, and all the rest are his sons.'
'Jupiter!' quoth she, 'which of these hairs is Paris,
my husband? 'The forked one,' quoth he, 'pluck't
out, and give it him.' But there was such laughing!
and Helen so blushed, an Paris so chafed, and all the
rest so laughed, that it passed.

speeches.speech: So let it now; for it has been while going by.
lines: So let it now; for it has been while going by.

speeches.speech: Well, cousin. I told you a thing yesterday; think on't.
lines: Well, cousin. I told you a thing yesterday; think on't.

speeches.speech: So I do.
lines: So I do.

speeches.speech: I'll be sworn 'tis true; he will weep you, an 'twere a man born in April.
lines: I'll be sworn 'tis true; he will weep you, an 'twere
a man born in April.

speeches.speech: And I'll spring up in his tears, an 'twere a nettle against May.
lines: And I'll spring up in his tears, an 'twere a nettle
against May.

speeches.speech: Hark! they are coming from the field: shall we stand up here, and see them as they pass toward Ilium? good niece, do, sweet niece Cressida.
lines: Hark! they are coming from the field: shall we
stand up here, and see them as they pass toward
Ilium? good niece, do, sweet niece Cressida.

speeches.speech: At your pleasure.
lines: At your pleasure.

speeches.speech: Here, here, here's an excellent place; here we may see most bravely: I'll tell you them all by their names as they pass by; but mark Troilus above the rest.
lines: Here, here, here's an excellent place; here we may
see most bravely: I'll tell you them all by their
names as they pass by; but mark Troilus above the rest.

speeches.speech: Speak not so loud.
lines: Speak not so loud.

speeches.speech: That's AEneas: is not that a brave man? he's one of the flowers of Troy, I can tell you: but mark Troilus; you shall see anon.
lines: That's AEneas: is not that a brave man? he's one of
the flowers of Troy, I can tell you: but mark
Troilus; you shall see anon.

speeches.speech: Who's that?
lines: Who's that?

speeches.speech: That's Antenor: he has a shrewd wit, I can tell you; and he's a man good enough, he's one o' the soundest judgments in whosoever, and a proper man of person. When comes Troilus? I'll show you Troilus anon: if he see me, you shall see him nod at me.
lines: That's Antenor: he has a shrewd wit, I can tell you;
and he's a man good enough, he's one o' the soundest
judgments in whosoever, and a proper man of person.
When comes Troilus? I'll show you Troilus anon: if
he see me, you shall see him nod at me.

speeches.speech: Will he give you the nod?
lines: Will he give you the nod?

speeches.speech: You shall see.
lines: You shall see.

speeches.speech: If he do, the rich shall have more.
lines: If he do, the rich shall have more.

speeches.speech: That's Hector, that, that, look you, that; there's a fellow! Go thy way, Hector! There's a brave man, niece. O brave Hector! Look how he looks! there's a countenance! is't not a brave man?
lines: That's Hector, that, that, look you, that; there's a
fellow! Go thy way, Hector! There's a brave man,
niece. O brave Hector! Look how he looks! there's
a countenance! is't not a brave man?

speeches.speech: O, a brave man!
lines: O, a brave man!

speeches.speech: Is a' not? it does a man's heart good. Look you what hacks are on his helmet! look you yonder, do you see? look you there: there's no jesting; there's laying on, take't off who will, as they say: there be hacks!
lines: Is a' not? it does a man's heart good. Look you
what hacks are on his helmet! look you yonder, do
you see? look you there: there's no jesting;
there's laying on, take't off who will, as they say:
there be hacks!

speeches.speech: Be those with swords?
lines: Be those with swords?

speeches.speech: Swords! any thing, he cares not; an the devil come to him, it's all one: by God's lid, it does one's heart good. Yonder comes Paris, yonder comes Paris. [PARIS passes] Look ye yonder, niece; is't not a gallant man too, is't not? Why, this is brave now. Who said he came hurt home to-day? he's not hurt: why, this will do Helen's heart good now, ha! Would I could see Troilus now! You shall see Troilus anon.
lines: Swords! any thing, he cares not; an the devil come
to him, it's all one: by God's lid, it does one's
heart good. Yonder comes Paris, yonder comes Paris.
[PARIS passes]
Look ye yonder, niece; is't not a gallant man too,
is't not? Why, this is brave now. Who said he came
hurt home to-day? he's not hurt: why, this will do
Helen's heart good now, ha! Would I could see
Troilus now! You shall see Troilus anon.

speeches.speech: Who's that?
lines: Who's that?

speeches.speech: That's Helenus. I marvel where Troilus is. That's Helenus. I think he went not forth to-day. That's Helenus.
lines: That's Helenus. I marvel where Troilus is. That's
Helenus. I think he went not forth to-day. That's Helenus.

speeches.speech: Can Helenus fight, uncle?
lines: Can Helenus fight, uncle?

speeches.speech: Helenus? no. Yes, he'll fight indifferent well. I marvel where Troilus is. Hark! do you not hear the people cry 'Troilus'? Helenus is a priest.
lines: Helenus? no. Yes, he'll fight indifferent well. I
marvel where Troilus is. Hark! do you not hear the
people cry 'Troilus'? Helenus is a priest.

speeches.speech: What sneaking fellow comes yonder?
lines: What sneaking fellow comes yonder?

speeches.speech: Where? yonder? that's Deiphobus. 'Tis Troilus! there's a man, niece! Hem! Brave Troilus! the prince of chivalry!
lines: Where? yonder? that's Deiphobus. 'Tis Troilus!
there's a man, niece! Hem! Brave Troilus! the
prince of chivalry!

speeches.speech: Peace, for shame, peace!
lines: Peace, for shame, peace!

speeches.speech: Mark him; note him. O brave Troilus! Look well upon him, niece: look you how his sword is bloodied, and his helm more hacked than Hector's, and how he looks, and how he goes! O admirable youth! he ne'er saw three and twenty. Go thy way, Troilus, go thy way! Had I a sister were a grace, or a daughter a goddess, he should take his choice. O admirable man! Paris? Paris is dirt to him; and, I warrant, Helen, to change, would give an eye to boot.
lines: Mark him; note him. O brave Troilus! Look well upon
him, niece: look you how his sword is bloodied, and
his helm more hacked than Hector's, and how he looks,
and how he goes! O admirable youth! he ne'er saw
three and twenty. Go thy way, Troilus, go thy way!
Had I a sister were a grace, or a daughter a goddess,
he should take his choice. O admirable man! Paris?
Paris is dirt to him; and, I warrant, Helen, to
change, would give an eye to boot.

speeches.speech: Here come more.
lines: Here come more.

speeches.speech: Asses, fools, dolts! chaff and bran, chaff and bran! porridge after meat! I could live and die i' the eyes of Troilus. Ne'er look, ne'er look: the eagles are gone: crows and daws, crows and daws! I had rather be such a man as Troilus than Agamemnon and all Greece.
lines: Asses, fools, dolts! chaff and bran, chaff and bran!
porridge after meat! I could live and die i' the
eyes of Troilus. Ne'er look, ne'er look: the eagles
are gone: crows and daws, crows and daws! I had
rather be such a man as Troilus than Agamemnon and
all Greece.

speeches.speech: There is among the Greeks Achilles, a better man than Troilus.
lines: There is among the Greeks Achilles, a better man than Troilus.

speeches.speech: Achilles! a drayman, a porter, a very camel.
lines: Achilles! a drayman, a porter, a very camel.

speeches.speech: Well, well.
lines: Well, well.

speeches.speech: 'Well, well!' why, have you any discretion? have you any eyes? Do you know what a man is? Is not birth, beauty, good shape, discourse, manhood, learning, gentleness, virtue, youth, liberality, and such like, the spice and salt that season a man?
lines: 'Well, well!' why, have you any discretion? have
you any eyes? Do you know what a man is? Is not
birth, beauty, good shape, discourse, manhood,
learning, gentleness, virtue, youth, liberality,
and such like, the spice and salt that season a man?

speeches.speech: Ay, a minced man: and then to be baked with no date in the pie, for then the man's date's out.
lines: Ay, a minced man: and then to be baked with no date
in the pie, for then the man's date's out.

speeches.speech: You are such a woman! one knows not at what ward you lie.
lines: You are such a woman! one knows not at what ward you
lie.

speeches.speech: Upon my back, to defend my belly; upon my wit, to defend my wiles; upon my secrecy, to defend mine honesty; my mask, to defend my beauty; and you, to defend all these: and at all these wards I lie, at a thousand watches.
lines: Upon my back, to defend my belly; upon my wit, to
defend my wiles; upon my secrecy, to defend mine
honesty; my mask, to defend my beauty; and you, to
defend all these: and at all these wards I lie, at a
thousand watches.

speeches.speech: Say one of your watches.
lines: Say one of your watches.

speeches.speech: Nay, I'll watch you for that; and that's one of the chiefest of them too: if I cannot ward what I would not have hit, I can watch you for telling how I took the blow; unless it swell past hiding, and then it's past watching.
lines: Nay, I'll watch you for that; and that's one of the
chiefest of them too: if I cannot ward what I would
not have hit, I can watch you for telling how I took
the blow; unless it swell past hiding, and then it's
past watching.

speeches.speech: You are such another!
lines: You are such another!

speeches.speech: Sir, my lord would instantly speak with you.
lines: Sir, my lord would instantly speak with you.

speeches.speech: Where?
lines: Where?

speeches.speech: At your own house; there he unarms him.
lines: At your own house; there he unarms him.

speeches.speech: Good boy, tell him I come. [Exit boy] I doubt he be hurt. Fare ye well, good niece.
lines: Good boy, tell him I come.
[Exit boy]
I doubt he be hurt. Fare ye well, good niece.

speeches.speech: Adieu, uncle.
lines: Adieu, uncle.

speeches.speech: I'll be with you, niece, by and by.
lines: I'll be with you, niece, by and by.

speeches.speech: To bring, uncle?
lines: To bring, uncle?

speeches.speech: Ay, a token from Troilus.
lines: Ay, a token from Troilus.

speeches.speech: By the same token, you are a bawd. [Exit PANDARUS] Words, vows, gifts, tears, and love's full sacrifice, He offers in another's enterprise; But more in Troilus thousand fold I see Than in the glass of Pandar's praise may be; Yet hold I off. Women are angels, wooing: Things won are done; joy's soul lies in the doing. That she beloved knows nought that knows not this: Men prize the thing ungain'd more than it is: That she was never yet that ever knew Love got so sweet as when desire did sue. Therefore this maxim out of love I teach: Achievement is command; ungain'd, beseech: Then though my heart's content firm love doth bear, Nothing of that shall from mine eyes appear.
lines: By the same token, you are a bawd.
[Exit PANDARUS]
Words, vows, gifts, tears, and love's full sacrifice,
He offers in another's enterprise;
But more in Troilus thousand fold I see
Than in the glass of Pandar's praise may be;
Yet hold I off. Women are angels, wooing:
Things won are done; joy's soul lies in the doing.
That she beloved knows nought that knows not this:
Men prize the thing ungain'd more than it is:
That she was never yet that ever knew
Love got so sweet as when desire did sue.
Therefore this maxim out of love I teach:
Achievement is command; ungain'd, beseech:
Then though my heart's content firm love doth bear,
Nothing of that shall from mine eyes appear.

speeches.speech: Princes, What grief hath set the jaundice on your cheeks? The ample proposition that hope makes In all designs begun on earth below Fails in the promised largeness: cheques and disasters Grow in the veins of actions highest rear'd, As knots, by the conflux of meeting sap, Infect the sound pine and divert his grain Tortive and errant from his course of growth. Nor, princes, is it matter new to us That we come short of our suppose so far That after seven years' siege yet Troy walls stand; Sith every action that hath gone before, Whereof we have record, trial did draw Bias and thwart, not answering the aim, And that unbodied figure of the thought That gave't surmised shape. Why then, you princes, Do you with cheeks abash'd behold our works, And call them shames? which are indeed nought else But the protractive trials of great Jove To find persistive constancy in men: The fineness of which metal is not found In fortune's love; for then the bold and coward, The wise and fool, the artist and unread, The hard and soft seem all affined and kin: But, in the wind and tempest of her frown, Distinction, with a broad and powerful fan, Puffing at all, winnows the light away; And what hath mass or matter, by itself Lies rich in virtue and unmingled.
lines: Princes,
What grief hath set the jaundice on your cheeks?
The ample proposition that hope makes
In all designs begun on earth below
Fails in the promised largeness: cheques and disasters
Grow in the veins of actions highest rear'd,
As knots, by the conflux of meeting sap,
Infect the sound pine and divert his grain
Tortive and errant from his course of growth.
Nor, princes, is it matter new to us
That we come short of our suppose so far
That after seven years' siege yet Troy walls stand;
Sith every action that hath gone before,
Whereof we have record, trial did draw
Bias and thwart, not answering the aim,
And that unbodied figure of the thought
That gave't surmised shape. Why then, you princes,
Do you with cheeks abash'd behold our works,
And call them shames? which are indeed nought else
But the protractive trials of great Jove
To find persistive constancy in men:
The fineness of which metal is not found
In fortune's love; for then the bold and coward,
The wise and fool, the artist and unread,
The hard and soft seem all affined and kin:
But, in the wind and tempest of her frown,
Distinction, with a broad and powerful fan,
Puffing at all, winnows the light away;
And what hath mass or matter, by itself
Lies rich in virtue and unmingled.

speeches.speech: With due observance of thy godlike seat, Great Agamemnon, Nestor shall apply Thy latest words. In the reproof of chance Lies the true proof of men: the sea being smooth, How many shallow bauble boats dare sail Upon her patient breast, making their way With those of nobler bulk! But let the ruffian Boreas once enrage The gentle Thetis, and anon behold The strong-ribb'd bark through liquid mountains cut, Bounding between the two moist elements, Like Perseus' horse: where's then the saucy boat Whose weak untimber'd sides but even now Co-rivall'd greatness? Either to harbour fled, Or made a toast for Neptune. Even so Doth valour's show and valour's worth divide In storms of fortune; for in her ray and brightness The herd hath more annoyance by the breeze Than by the tiger; but when the splitting wind Makes flexible the knees of knotted oaks, And flies fled under shade, why, then the thing of courage As roused with rage with rage doth sympathize, And with an accent tuned in selfsame key Retorts to chiding fortune.
lines: With due observance of thy godlike seat,
Great Agamemnon, Nestor shall apply
Thy latest words. In the reproof of chance
Lies the true proof of men: the sea being smooth,
How many shallow bauble boats dare sail
Upon her patient breast, making their way
With those of nobler bulk!
But let the ruffian Boreas once enrage
The gentle Thetis, and anon behold
The strong-ribb'd bark through liquid mountains cut,
Bounding between the two moist elements,
Like Perseus' horse: where's then the saucy boat
Whose weak untimber'd sides but even now
Co-rivall'd greatness? Either to harbour fled,
Or made a toast for Neptune. Even so
Doth valour's show and valour's worth divide
In storms of fortune; for in her ray and brightness
The herd hath more annoyance by the breeze
Than by the tiger; but when the splitting wind
Makes flexible the knees of knotted oaks,
And flies fled under shade, why, then the thing of courage
As roused with rage with rage doth sympathize,
And with an accent tuned in selfsame key
Retorts to chiding fortune.

speeches.speech: Agamemnon, Thou great commander, nerve and bone of Greece, Heart of our numbers, soul and only spirit. In whom the tempers and the minds of all Should be shut up, hear what Ulysses speaks. Besides the applause and approbation To which, [To AGAMEMNON] most mighty for thy place and sway, [To NESTOR] And thou most reverend for thy stretch'd-out life I give to both your speeches, which were such As Agamemnon and the hand of Greece Should hold up high in brass, and such again As venerable Nestor, hatch'd in silver, Should with a bond of air, strong as the axle-tree On which heaven rides, knit all the Greekish ears To his experienced tongue, yet let it please both, Thou great, and wise, to hear Ulysses speak.
lines: Agamemnon,
Thou great commander, nerve and bone of Greece,
Heart of our numbers, soul and only spirit.
In whom the tempers and the minds of all
Should be shut up, hear what Ulysses speaks.
Besides the applause and approbation To which,
[To AGAMEMNON]
most mighty for thy place and sway,
[To NESTOR]
And thou most reverend for thy stretch'd-out life
I give to both your speeches, which were such
As Agamemnon and the hand of Greece
Should hold up high in brass, and such again
As venerable Nestor, hatch'd in silver,
Should with a bond of air, strong as the axle-tree
On which heaven rides, knit all the Greekish ears
To his experienced tongue, yet let it please both,
Thou great, and wise, to hear Ulysses speak.

speeches.speech: Speak, prince of Ithaca; and be't of less expect That matter needless, of importless burden, Divide thy lips, than we are confident, When rank Thersites opes his mastic jaws, We shall hear music, wit and oracle.
lines: Speak, prince of Ithaca; and be't of less expect
That matter needless, of importless burden,
Divide thy lips, than we are confident,
When rank Thersites opes his mastic jaws,
We shall hear music, wit and oracle.

speeches.speech: Troy, yet upon his basis, had been down, And the great Hector's sword had lack'd a master, But for these instances. The specialty of rule hath been neglected: And, look, how many Grecian tents do stand Hollow upon this plain, so many hollow factions. When that the general is not like the hive To whom the foragers shall all repair, What honey is expected? Degree being vizarded, The unworthiest shows as fairly in the mask. The heavens themselves, the planets and this centre Observe degree, priority and place, Insisture, course, proportion, season, form, Office and custom, in all line of order; And therefore is the glorious planet Sol In noble eminence enthroned and sphered Amidst the other; whose medicinable eye Corrects the ill aspects of planets evil, And posts, like the commandment of a king, Sans cheque to good and bad: but when the planets In evil mixture to disorder wander, What plagues and what portents! what mutiny! What raging of the sea! shaking of earth! Commotion in the winds! frights, changes, horrors, Divert and crack, rend and deracinate The unity and married calm of states Quite from their fixure! O, when degree is shaked, Which is the ladder to all high designs, Then enterprise is sick! How could communities, Degrees in schools and brotherhoods in cities, Peaceful commerce from dividable shores, The primogenitive and due of birth, Prerogative of age, crowns, sceptres, laurels, But by degree, stand in authentic place? Take but degree away, untune that string, And, hark, what discord follows! each thing meets In mere oppugnancy: the bounded waters Should lift their bosoms higher than the shores And make a sop of all this solid globe: Strength should be lord of imbecility, And the rude son should strike his father dead: Force should be right; or rather, right and wrong, Between whose endless jar justice resides, Should lose their names, and so should justice too. Then every thing includes itself in power, Power into will, will into appetite; And appetite, an universal wolf, So doubly seconded with will and power, Must make perforce an universal prey, And last eat up himself. Great Agamemnon, This chaos, when degree is suffocate, Follows the choking. And this neglection of degree it is That by a pace goes backward, with a purpose It hath to climb. The general's disdain'd By him one step below, he by the next, That next by him beneath; so every step, Exampled by the first pace that is sick Of his superior, grows to an envious fever Of pale and bloodless emulation: And 'tis this fever that keeps Troy on foot, Not her own sinews. To end a tale of length, Troy in our weakness stands, not in her strength.
lines: Troy, yet upon his basis, had been down,
And the great Hector's sword had lack'd a master,
But for these instances.
The specialty of rule hath been neglected:
And, look, how many Grecian tents do stand
Hollow upon this plain, so many hollow factions.
When that the general is not like the hive
To whom the foragers shall all repair,
What honey is expected? Degree being vizarded,
The unworthiest shows as fairly in the mask.
The heavens themselves, the planets and this centre
Observe degree, priority and place,
Insisture, course, proportion, season, form,
Office and custom, in all line of order;
And therefore is the glorious planet Sol
In noble eminence enthroned and sphered
Amidst the other; whose medicinable eye
Corrects the ill aspects of planets evil,
And posts, like the commandment of a king,
Sans cheque to good and bad: but when the planets
In evil mixture to disorder wander,
What plagues and what portents! what mutiny!
What raging of the sea! shaking of earth!
Commotion in the winds! frights, changes, horrors,
Divert and crack, rend and deracinate
The unity and married calm of states
Quite from their fixure! O, when degree is shaked,
Which is the ladder to all high designs,
Then enterprise is sick! How could communities,
Degrees in schools and brotherhoods in cities,
Peaceful commerce from dividable shores,
The primogenitive and due of birth,
Prerogative of age, crowns, sceptres, laurels,
But by degree, stand in authentic place?
Take but degree away, untune that string,
And, hark, what discord follows! each thing meets
In mere oppugnancy: the bounded waters
Should lift their bosoms higher than the shores
And make a sop of all this solid globe:
Strength should be lord of imbecility,
And the rude son should strike his father dead:
Force should be right; or rather, right and wrong,
Between whose endless jar justice resides,
Should lose their names, and so should justice too.
Then every thing includes itself in power,
Power into will, will into appetite;
And appetite, an universal wolf,
So doubly seconded with will and power,
Must make perforce an universal prey,
And last eat up himself. Great Agamemnon,
This chaos, when degree is suffocate,
Follows the choking.
And this neglection of degree it is
That by a pace goes backward, with a purpose
It hath to climb. The general's disdain'd
By him one step below, he by the next,
That next by him beneath; so every step,
Exampled by the first pace that is sick
Of his superior, grows to an envious fever
Of pale and bloodless emulation:
And 'tis this fever that keeps Troy on foot,
Not her own sinews. To end a tale of length,
Troy in our weakness stands, not in her strength.

speeches.speech: Most wisely hath Ulysses here discover'd The fever whereof all our power is sick.
lines: Most wisely hath Ulysses here discover'd
The fever whereof all our power is sick.

speeches.speech: The nature of the sickness found, Ulysses, What is the remedy?
lines: The nature of the sickness found, Ulysses,
What is the remedy?

speeches.speech: The great Achilles, whom opinion crowns The sinew and the forehand of our host, Having his ear full of his airy fame, Grows dainty of his worth, and in his tent Lies mocking our designs: with him Patroclus Upon a lazy bed the livelong day Breaks scurril jests; And with ridiculous and awkward action, Which, slanderer, he imitation calls, He pageants us. Sometime, great Agamemnon, Thy topless deputation he puts on, And, like a strutting player, whose conceit Lies in his hamstring, and doth think it rich To hear the wooden dialogue and sound 'Twixt his stretch'd footing and the scaffoldage,-- Such to-be-pitied and o'er-wrested seeming He acts thy greatness in: and when he speaks, 'Tis like a chime a-mending; with terms unsquared, Which, from the tongue of roaring Typhon dropp'd Would seem hyperboles. At this fusty stuff The large Achilles, on his press'd bed lolling, From his deep chest laughs out a loud applause; Cries 'Excellent! 'tis Agamemnon just. Now play me Nestor; hem, and stroke thy beard, As he being drest to some oration.' That's done, as near as the extremest ends Of parallels, as like as Vulcan and his wife: Yet god Achilles still cries 'Excellent! 'Tis Nestor right. Now play him me, Patroclus, Arming to answer in a night alarm.' And then, forsooth, the faint defects of age Must be the scene of mirth; to cough and spit, And, with a palsy-fumbling on his gorget, Shake in and out the rivet: and at this sport Sir Valour dies; cries 'O, enough, Patroclus; Or give me ribs of steel! I shall split all In pleasure of my spleen.' And in this fashion, All our abilities, gifts, natures, shapes, Severals and generals of grace exact, Achievements, plots, orders, preventions, Excitements to the field, or speech for truce, Success or loss, what is or is not, serves As stuff for these two to make paradoxes.
lines: The great Achilles, whom opinion crowns
The sinew and the forehand of our host,
Having his ear full of his airy fame,
Grows dainty of his worth, and in his tent
Lies mocking our designs: with him Patroclus
Upon a lazy bed the livelong day
Breaks scurril jests;
And with ridiculous and awkward action,
Which, slanderer, he imitation calls,
He pageants us. Sometime, great Agamemnon,
Thy topless deputation he puts on,
And, like a strutting player, whose conceit
Lies in his hamstring, and doth think it rich
To hear the wooden dialogue and sound
'Twixt his stretch'd footing and the scaffoldage,--
Such to-be-pitied and o'er-wrested seeming
He acts thy greatness in: and when he speaks,
'Tis like a chime a-mending; with terms unsquared,
Which, from the tongue of roaring Typhon dropp'd
Would seem hyperboles. At this fusty stuff
The large Achilles, on his press'd bed lolling,
From his deep chest laughs out a loud applause;
Cries 'Excellent! 'tis Agamemnon just.
Now play me Nestor; hem, and stroke thy beard,
As he being drest to some oration.'
That's done, as near as the extremest ends
Of parallels, as like as Vulcan and his wife:
Yet god Achilles still cries 'Excellent!
'Tis Nestor right. Now play him me, Patroclus,
Arming to answer in a night alarm.'
And then, forsooth, the faint defects of age
Must be the scene of mirth; to cough and spit,
And, with a palsy-fumbling on his gorget,
Shake in and out the rivet: and at this sport
Sir Valour dies; cries 'O, enough, Patroclus;
Or give me ribs of steel! I shall split all
In pleasure of my spleen.' And in this fashion,
All our abilities, gifts, natures, shapes,
Severals and generals of grace exact,
Achievements, plots, orders, preventions,
Excitements to the field, or speech for truce,
Success or loss, what is or is not, serves
As stuff for these two to make paradoxes.

speeches.speech: And in the imitation of these twain-- Who, as Ulysses says, opinion crowns With an imperial voice--many are infect. Ajax is grown self-will'd, and bears his head In such a rein, in full as proud a place As broad Achilles; keeps his tent like him; Makes factious feasts; rails on our state of war, Bold as an oracle, and sets Thersites, A slave whose gall coins slanders like a mint, To match us in comparisons with dirt, To weaken and discredit our exposure, How rank soever rounded in with danger.
lines: And in the imitation of these twain--
Who, as Ulysses says, opinion crowns
With an imperial voice--many are infect.
Ajax is grown self-will'd, and bears his head
In such a rein, in full as proud a place
As broad Achilles; keeps his tent like him;
Makes factious feasts; rails on our state of war,
Bold as an oracle, and sets Thersites,
A slave whose gall coins slanders like a mint,
To match us in comparisons with dirt,
To weaken and discredit our exposure,
How rank soever rounded in with danger.

speeches.speech: They tax our policy, and call it cowardice, Count wisdom as no member of the war, Forestall prescience, and esteem no act But that of hand: the still and mental parts, That do contrive how many hands shall strike, When fitness calls them on, and know by measure Of their observant toil the enemies' weight,-- Why, this hath not a finger's dignity: They call this bed-work, mappery, closet-war; So that the ram that batters down the wall, For the great swing and rudeness of his poise, They place before his hand that made the engine, Or those that with the fineness of their souls By reason guide his execution.
lines: They tax our policy, and call it cowardice,
Count wisdom as no member of the war,
Forestall prescience, and esteem no act
But that of hand: the still and mental parts,
That do contrive how many hands shall strike,
When fitness calls them on, and know by measure
Of their observant toil the enemies' weight,--
Why, this hath not a finger's dignity:
They call this bed-work, mappery, closet-war;
So that the ram that batters down the wall,
For the great swing and rudeness of his poise,
They place before his hand that made the engine,
Or those that with the fineness of their souls
By reason guide his execution.

speeches.speech: Let this be granted, and Achilles' horse Makes many Thetis' sons.
lines: Let this be granted, and Achilles' horse
Makes many Thetis' sons.

speeches.speech: What trumpet? look, Menelaus.
lines: What trumpet? look, Menelaus.

speeches.speech: From Troy.
lines: From Troy.

speeches.speech: What would you 'fore our tent?
lines: What would you 'fore our tent?

speeches.speech: Is this great Agamemnon's tent, I pray you?
lines: Is this great Agamemnon's tent, I pray you?

speeches.speech: Even this.
lines: Even this.

speeches.speech: May one, that is a herald and a prince, Do a fair message to his kingly ears?
lines: May one, that is a herald and a prince,
Do a fair message to his kingly ears?

speeches.speech: With surety stronger than Achilles' arm 'Fore all the Greekish heads, which with one voice Call Agamemnon head and general.
lines: With surety stronger than Achilles' arm
'Fore all the Greekish heads, which with one voice
Call Agamemnon head and general.

speeches.speech: Fair leave and large security. How may A stranger to those most imperial looks Know them from eyes of other mortals?
lines: Fair leave and large security. How may
A stranger to those most imperial looks
Know them from eyes of other mortals?

speeches.speech: How!
lines: How!

speeches.speech: Ay; I ask, that I might waken reverence, And bid the cheek be ready with a blush Modest as morning when she coldly eyes The youthful Phoebus: Which is that god in office, guiding men? Which is the high and mighty Agamemnon?
lines: Ay;
I ask, that I might waken reverence,
And bid the cheek be ready with a blush
Modest as morning when she coldly eyes
The youthful Phoebus:
Which is that god in office, guiding men?
Which is the high and mighty Agamemnon?

speeches.speech: This Trojan scorns us; or the men of Troy Are ceremonious courtiers.
lines: This Trojan scorns us; or the men of Troy
Are ceremonious courtiers.

speeches.speech: Courtiers as free, as debonair, unarm'd, As bending angels; that's their fame in peace: But when they would seem soldiers, they have galls, Good arms, strong joints, true swords; and, Jove's accord, Nothing so full of heart. But peace, AEneas, Peace, Trojan; lay thy finger on thy lips! The worthiness of praise distains his worth, If that the praised himself bring the praise forth: But what the repining enemy commends, That breath fame blows; that praise, sole sure, transcends.
lines: Courtiers as free, as debonair, unarm'd,
As bending angels; that's their fame in peace:
But when they would seem soldiers, they have galls,
Good arms, strong joints, true swords; and,
Jove's accord,
Nothing so full of heart. But peace, AEneas,
Peace, Trojan; lay thy finger on thy lips!
The worthiness of praise distains his worth,
If that the praised himself bring the praise forth:
But what the repining enemy commends,
That breath fame blows; that praise, sole sure,
transcends.

speeches.speech: Sir, you of Troy, call you yourself AEneas?
lines: Sir, you of Troy, call you yourself AEneas?

speeches.speech: Ay, Greek, that is my name.
lines: Ay, Greek, that is my name.

speeches.speech: What's your affair I pray you?
lines: What's your affair I pray you?

speeches.speech: Sir, pardon; 'tis for Agamemnon's ears.
lines: Sir, pardon; 'tis for Agamemnon's ears.

speeches.speech: He hears naught privately that comes from Troy.
lines: He hears naught privately that comes from Troy.

speeches.speech: Nor I from Troy come not to whisper him: I bring a trumpet to awake his ear, To set his sense on the attentive bent, And then to speak.
lines: Nor I from Troy come not to whisper him:
I bring a trumpet to awake his ear,
To set his sense on the attentive bent,
And then to speak.

speeches.speech: Speak frankly as the wind; It is not Agamemnon's sleeping hour: That thou shalt know. Trojan, he is awake, He tells thee so himself.
lines: Speak frankly as the wind;
It is not Agamemnon's sleeping hour:
That thou shalt know. Trojan, he is awake,
He tells thee so himself.

speeches.speech: Trumpet, blow loud, Send thy brass voice through all these lazy tents; And every Greek of mettle, let him know, What Troy means fairly shall be spoke aloud. [Trumpet sounds] We have, great Agamemnon, here in Troy A prince call'd Hector,--Priam is his father,-- Who in this dull and long-continued truce Is rusty grown: he bade me take a trumpet, And to this purpose speak. Kings, princes, lords! If there be one among the fair'st of Greece That holds his honour higher than his ease, That seeks his praise more than he fears his peril, That knows his valour, and knows not his fear, That loves his mistress more than in confession, With truant vows to her own lips he loves, And dare avow her beauty and her worth In other arms than hers,--to him this challenge. Hector, in view of Trojans and of Greeks, Shall make it good, or do his best to do it, He hath a lady, wiser, fairer, truer, Than ever Greek did compass in his arms, And will to-morrow with his trumpet call Midway between your tents and walls of Troy, To rouse a Grecian that is true in love: If any come, Hector shall honour him; If none, he'll say in Troy when he retires, The Grecian dames are sunburnt and not worth The splinter of a lance. Even so much.
lines: Trumpet, blow loud,
Send thy brass voice through all these lazy tents;
And every Greek of mettle, let him know,
What Troy means fairly shall be spoke aloud.
[Trumpet sounds]
We have, great Agamemnon, here in Troy
A prince call'd Hector,--Priam is his father,--
Who in this dull and long-continued truce
Is rusty grown: he bade me take a trumpet,
And to this purpose speak. Kings, princes, lords!
If there be one among the fair'st of Greece
That holds his honour higher than his ease,
That seeks his praise more than he fears his peril,
That knows his valour, and knows not his fear,
That loves his mistress more than in confession,
With truant vows to her own lips he loves,
And dare avow her beauty and her worth
In other arms than hers,--to him this challenge.
Hector, in view of Trojans and of Greeks,
Shall make it good, or do his best to do it,
He hath a lady, wiser, fairer, truer,
Than ever Greek did compass in his arms,
And will to-morrow with his trumpet call
Midway between your tents and walls of Troy,
To rouse a Grecian that is true in love:
If any come, Hector shall honour him;
If none, he'll say in Troy when he retires,
The Grecian dames are sunburnt and not worth
The splinter of a lance. Even so much.

speeches.speech: This shall be told our lovers, Lord AEneas; If none of them have soul in such a kind, We left them all at home: but we are soldiers; And may that soldier a mere recreant prove, That means not, hath not, or is not in love! If then one is, or hath, or means to be, That one meets Hector; if none else, I am he.
lines: This shall be told our lovers, Lord AEneas;
If none of them have soul in such a kind,
We left them all at home: but we are soldiers;
And may that soldier a mere recreant prove,
That means not, hath not, or is not in love!
If then one is, or hath, or means to be,
That one meets Hector; if none else, I am he.

speeches.speech: Tell him of Nestor, one that was a man When Hector's grandsire suck'd: he is old now; But if there be not in our Grecian host One noble man that hath one spark of fire, To answer for his love, tell him from me I'll hide my silver beard in a gold beaver And in my vantbrace put this wither'd brawn, And meeting him will tell him that my lady Was fairer than his grandam and as chaste As may be in the world: his youth in flood, I'll prove this truth with my three drops of blood.
lines: Tell him of Nestor, one that was a man
When Hector's grandsire suck'd: he is old now;
But if there be not in our Grecian host
One noble man that hath one spark of fire,
To answer for his love, tell him from me
I'll hide my silver beard in a gold beaver
And in my vantbrace put this wither'd brawn,
And meeting him will tell him that my lady
Was fairer than his grandam and as chaste
As may be in the world: his youth in flood,
I'll prove this truth with my three drops of blood.

speeches.speech: Now heavens forbid such scarcity of youth!
lines: Now heavens forbid such scarcity of youth!

speeches.speech: Amen.
lines: Amen.

speeches.speech: Fair Lord AEneas, let me touch your hand; To our pavilion shall I lead you, sir. Achilles shall have word of this intent; So shall each lord of Greece, from tent to tent: Yourself shall feast with us before you go And find the welcome of a noble foe.
lines: Fair Lord AEneas, let me touch your hand;
To our pavilion shall I lead you, sir.
Achilles shall have word of this intent;
So shall each lord of Greece, from tent to tent:
Yourself shall feast with us before you go
And find the welcome of a noble foe.

speeches.speech: Nestor!
lines: Nestor!

speeches.speech: What says Ulysses?
lines: What says Ulysses?

speeches.speech: I have a young conception in my brain; Be you my time to bring it to some shape.
lines: I have a young conception in my brain;
Be you my time to bring it to some shape.

speeches.speech: What is't?
lines: What is't?

speeches.speech: This 'tis: Blunt wedges rive hard knots: the seeded pride That hath to this maturity blown up In rank Achilles must or now be cropp'd, Or, shedding, breed a nursery of like evil, To overbulk us all.
lines: This 'tis:
Blunt wedges rive hard knots: the seeded pride
That hath to this maturity blown up
In rank Achilles must or now be cropp'd,
Or, shedding, breed a nursery of like evil,
To overbulk us all.

speeches.speech: Well, and how?
lines: Well, and how?

speeches.speech: This challenge that the gallant Hector sends, However it is spread in general name, Relates in purpose only to Achilles.
lines: This challenge that the gallant Hector sends,
However it is spread in general name,
Relates in purpose only to Achilles.

speeches.speech: The purpose is perspicuous even as substance, Whose grossness little characters sum up: And, in the publication, make no strain, But that Achilles, were his brain as barren As banks of Libya,--though, Apollo knows, 'Tis dry enough,--will, with great speed of judgment, Ay, with celerity, find Hector's purpose Pointing on him.
lines: The purpose is perspicuous even as substance,
Whose grossness little characters sum up:
And, in the publication, make no strain,
But that Achilles, were his brain as barren
As banks of Libya,--though, Apollo knows,
'Tis dry enough,--will, with great speed of judgment,
Ay, with celerity, find Hector's purpose
Pointing on him.

speeches.speech: And wake him to the answer, think you?
lines: And wake him to the answer, think you?

speeches.speech: Yes, 'tis most meet: whom may you else oppose, That can from Hector bring his honour off, If not Achilles? Though't be a sportful combat, Yet in the trial much opinion dwells; For here the Trojans taste our dear'st repute With their finest palate: and trust to me, Ulysses, Our imputation shall be oddly poised In this wild action; for the success, Although particular, shall give a scantling Of good or bad unto the general; And in such indexes, although small pricks To their subsequent volumes, there is seen The baby figure of the giant mass Of things to come at large. It is supposed He that meets Hector issues from our choice And choice, being mutual act of all our souls, Makes merit her election, and doth boil, As 'twere from us all, a man distill'd Out of our virtues; who miscarrying, What heart receives from hence the conquering part, To steel a strong opinion to themselves? Which entertain'd, limbs are his instruments, In no less working than are swords and bows Directive by the limbs.
lines: Yes, 'tis most meet: whom may you else oppose,
That can from Hector bring his honour off,
If not Achilles? Though't be a sportful combat,
Yet in the trial much opinion dwells;
For here the Trojans taste our dear'st repute
With their finest palate: and trust to me, Ulysses,
Our imputation shall be oddly poised
In this wild action; for the success,
Although particular, shall give a scantling
Of good or bad unto the general;
And in such indexes, although small pricks
To their subsequent volumes, there is seen
The baby figure of the giant mass
Of things to come at large. It is supposed
He that meets Hector issues from our choice
And choice, being mutual act of all our souls,
Makes merit her election, and doth boil,
As 'twere from us all, a man distill'd
Out of our virtues; who miscarrying,
What heart receives from hence the conquering part,
To steel a strong opinion to themselves?
Which entertain'd, limbs are his instruments,
In no less working than are swords and bows
Directive by the limbs.

speeches.speech: Give pardon to my speech: Therefore 'tis meet Achilles meet not Hector. Let us, like merchants, show our foulest wares, And think, perchance, they'll sell; if not, The lustre of the better yet to show, Shall show the better. Do not consent That ever Hector and Achilles meet; For both our honour and our shame in this Are dogg'd with two strange followers.
lines: Give pardon to my speech:
Therefore 'tis meet Achilles meet not Hector.
Let us, like merchants, show our foulest wares,
And think, perchance, they'll sell; if not,
The lustre of the better yet to show,
Shall show the better. Do not consent
That ever Hector and Achilles meet;
For both our honour and our shame in this
Are dogg'd with two strange followers.

speeches.speech: I see them not with my old eyes: what are they?
lines: I see them not with my old eyes: what are they?

speeches.speech: What glory our Achilles shares from Hector, Were he not proud, we all should share with him: But he already is too insolent; And we were better parch in Afric sun Than in the pride and salt scorn of his eyes, Should he 'scape Hector fair: if he were foil'd, Why then, we did our main opinion crush In taint of our best man. No, make a lottery; And, by device, let blockish Ajax draw The sort to fight with Hector: among ourselves Give him allowance for the better man; For that will physic the great Myrmidon Who broils in loud applause, and make him fall His crest that prouder than blue Iris bends. If the dull brainless Ajax come safe off, We'll dress him up in voices: if he fail, Yet go we under our opinion still That we have better men. But, hit or miss, Our project's life this shape of sense assumes: Ajax employ'd plucks down Achilles' plumes.
lines: What glory our Achilles shares from Hector,
Were he not proud, we all should share with him:
But he already is too insolent;
And we were better parch in Afric sun
Than in the pride and salt scorn of his eyes,
Should he 'scape Hector fair: if he were foil'd,
Why then, we did our main opinion crush
In taint of our best man. No, make a lottery;
And, by device, let blockish Ajax draw
The sort to fight with Hector: among ourselves
Give him allowance for the better man;
For that will physic the great Myrmidon
Who broils in loud applause, and make him fall
His crest that prouder than blue Iris bends.
If the dull brainless Ajax come safe off,
We'll dress him up in voices: if he fail,
Yet go we under our opinion still
That we have better men. But, hit or miss,
Our project's life this shape of sense assumes:
Ajax employ'd plucks down Achilles' plumes.

speeches.speech: Ulysses, Now I begin to relish thy advice; And I will give a taste of it forthwith To Agamemnon: go we to him straight. Two curs shall tame each other: pride alone Must tarre the mastiffs on, as 'twere their bone.
lines: Ulysses,
Now I begin to relish thy advice;
And I will give a taste of it forthwith
To Agamemnon: go we to him straight.
Two curs shall tame each other: pride alone
Must tarre the mastiffs on, as 'twere their bone.

speeches.speech: Thersites!
lines: Thersites!

speeches.speech: Agamemnon, how if he had boils? full, all over, generally?
lines: Agamemnon, how if he had boils? full, all over,
generally?

speeches.speech: Thersites!
lines: Thersites!

speeches.speech: And those boils did run? say so: did not the general run then? were not that a botchy core?
lines: And those boils did run? say so: did not the
general run then? were not that a botchy core?

speeches.speech: Dog!
lines: Dog!

speeches.speech: Then would come some matter from him; I see none now.
lines: Then would come some matter from him; I see none now.

speeches.speech: Thou bitch-wolf's son, canst thou not hear? [Beating him] Feel, then.
lines: Thou bitch-wolf's son, canst thou not hear?
[Beating him]
Feel, then.

speeches.speech: The plague of Greece upon thee, thou mongrel beef-witted lord!
lines: The plague of Greece upon thee, thou mongrel
beef-witted lord!

speeches.speech: Speak then, thou vinewedst leaven, speak: I will beat thee into handsomeness.
lines: Speak then, thou vinewedst leaven, speak: I will
beat thee into handsomeness.

speeches.speech: I shall sooner rail thee into wit and holiness: but, I think, thy horse will sooner con an oration than thou learn a prayer without book. Thou canst strike, canst thou? a red murrain o' thy jade's tricks!
lines: I shall sooner rail thee into wit and holiness: but,
I think, thy horse will sooner con an oration than
thou learn a prayer without book. Thou canst strike,
canst thou? a red murrain o' thy jade's tricks!

speeches.speech: Toadstool, learn me the proclamation.
lines: Toadstool, learn me the proclamation.

speeches.speech: Dost thou think I have no sense, thou strikest me thus?
lines: Dost thou think I have no sense, thou strikest me thus?

speeches.speech: The proclamation!
lines: The proclamation!

speeches.speech: Thou art proclaimed a fool, I think.
lines: Thou art proclaimed a fool, I think.

speeches.speech: Do not, porpentine, do not: my fingers itch.
lines: Do not, porpentine, do not: my fingers itch.

speeches.speech: I would thou didst itch from head to foot and I had the scratching of thee; I would make thee the loathsomest scab in Greece. When thou art forth in the incursions, thou strikest as slow as another.
lines: I would thou didst itch from head to foot and I had
the scratching of thee; I would make thee the
loathsomest scab in Greece. When thou art forth in
the incursions, thou strikest as slow as another.

speeches.speech: I say, the proclamation!
lines: I say, the proclamation!

speeches.speech: Thou grumblest and railest every hour on Achilles, and thou art as full of envy at his greatness as Cerberus is at Proserpine's beauty, ay, that thou barkest at him.
lines: Thou grumblest and railest every hour on Achilles,
and thou art as full of envy at his greatness as
Cerberus is at Proserpine's beauty, ay, that thou
barkest at him.

speeches.speech: Mistress Thersites!
lines: Mistress Thersites!

speeches.speech: Thou shouldest strike him.
lines: Thou shouldest strike him.

speeches.speech: Cobloaf!
lines: Cobloaf!

speeches.speech: He would pun thee into shivers with his fist, as a sailor breaks a biscuit.
lines: He would pun thee into shivers with his fist, as a
sailor breaks a biscuit.

speeches.speech: [Beating him] You whoreson cur!
lines: [Beating him] You whoreson cur!

speeches.speech: Do, do.
lines: Do, do.

speeches.speech: Thou stool for a witch!
lines: Thou stool for a witch!

speeches.speech: Ay, do, do; thou sodden-witted lord! thou hast no more brain than I have in mine elbows; an assinego may tutor thee: thou scurvy-valiant ass! thou art here but to thrash Trojans; and thou art bought and sold among those of any wit, like a barbarian slave. If thou use to beat me, I will begin at thy heel, and tell what thou art by inches, thou thing of no bowels, thou!
lines: Ay, do, do; thou sodden-witted lord! thou hast no
more brain than I have in mine elbows; an assinego
may tutor thee: thou scurvy-valiant ass! thou art
here but to thrash Trojans; and thou art bought and
sold among those of any wit, like a barbarian slave.
If thou use to beat me, I will begin at thy heel, and
tell what thou art by inches, thou thing of no
bowels, thou!

speeches.speech: You dog!
lines: You dog!

speeches.speech: You scurvy lord!
lines: You scurvy lord!

speeches.speech: [Beating him] You cur!
lines: [Beating him] You cur!

speeches.speech: Mars his idiot! do, rudeness; do, camel; do, do.
lines: Mars his idiot! do, rudeness; do, camel; do, do.

speeches.speech: Why, how now, Ajax! wherefore do you thus? How now, Thersites! what's the matter, man?
lines: Why, how now, Ajax! wherefore do you thus? How now,
Thersites! what's the matter, man?

speeches.speech: You see him there, do you?
lines: You see him there, do you?

speeches.speech: Ay; what's the matter?
lines: Ay; what's the matter?

speeches.speech: Nay, look upon him.
lines: Nay, look upon him.

speeches.speech: So I do: what's the matter?
lines: So I do: what's the matter?

speeches.speech: Nay, but regard him well.
lines: Nay, but regard him well.

speeches.speech: 'Well!' why, I do so.
lines: 'Well!' why, I do so.

speeches.speech: But yet you look not well upon him; for whosoever you take him to be, he is Ajax.
lines: But yet you look not well upon him; for whosoever you
take him to be, he is Ajax.

speeches.speech: I know that, fool.
lines: I know that, fool.

speeches.speech: Ay, but that fool knows not himself.
lines: Ay, but that fool knows not himself.

speeches.speech: Therefore I beat thee.
lines: Therefore I beat thee.

speeches.speech: Lo, lo, lo, lo, what modicums of wit he utters! his evasions have ears thus long. I have bobbed his brain more than he has beat my bones: I will buy nine sparrows for a penny, and his pia mater is not worth the nineth part of a sparrow. This lord, Achilles, Ajax, who wears his wit in his belly and his guts in his head, I'll tell you what I say of him.
lines: Lo, lo, lo, lo, what modicums of wit he utters! his
evasions have ears thus long. I have bobbed his
brain more than he has beat my bones: I will buy
nine sparrows for a penny, and his pia mater is not
worth the nineth part of a sparrow. This lord,
Achilles, Ajax, who wears his wit in his belly and
his guts in his head, I'll tell you what I say of
him.

speeches.speech: What?
lines: What?

speeches.speech: I say, this Ajax--
lines: I say, this Ajax--

speeches.speech: Nay, good Ajax.
lines: Nay, good Ajax.

speeches.speech: Has not so much wit--
lines: Has not so much wit--

speeches.speech: Nay, I must hold you.
lines: Nay, I must hold you.

speeches.speech: As will stop the eye of Helen's needle, for whom he comes to fight.
lines: As will stop the eye of Helen's needle, for whom he
comes to fight.

speeches.speech: Peace, fool!
