lines: Peace, fool!

speeches.speech: I would have peace and quietness, but the fool will not: he there: that he: look you there.
lines: I would have peace and quietness, but the fool will
not: he there: that he: look you there.

speeches.speech: O thou damned cur! I shall--
lines: O thou damned cur! I shall--

speeches.speech: Will you set your wit to a fool's?
lines: Will you set your wit to a fool's?

speeches.speech: No, I warrant you; for a fools will shame it.
lines: No, I warrant you; for a fools will shame it.

speeches.speech: Good words, Thersites.
lines: Good words, Thersites.

speeches.speech: What's the quarrel?
lines: What's the quarrel?

speeches.speech: I bade the vile owl go learn me the tenor of the proclamation, and he rails upon me.
lines: I bade the vile owl go learn me the tenor of the
proclamation, and he rails upon me.

speeches.speech: I serve thee not.
lines: I serve thee not.

speeches.speech: Well, go to, go to.
lines: Well, go to, go to.

speeches.speech: I serve here voluntarily.
lines: I serve here voluntarily.

speeches.speech: Your last service was sufferance, 'twas not voluntary: no man is beaten voluntary: Ajax was here the voluntary, and you as under an impress.
lines: Your last service was sufferance, 'twas not
voluntary: no man is beaten voluntary: Ajax was
here the voluntary, and you as under an impress.

speeches.speech: E'en so; a great deal of your wit, too, lies in your sinews, or else there be liars. Hector have a great catch, if he knock out either of your brains: a' were as good crack a fusty nut with no kernel.
lines: E'en so; a great deal of your wit, too, lies in your
sinews, or else there be liars. Hector have a great
catch, if he knock out either of your brains: a'
were as good crack a fusty nut with no kernel.

speeches.speech: What, with me too, Thersites?
lines: What, with me too, Thersites?

speeches.speech: There's Ulysses and old Nestor, whose wit was mouldy ere your grandsires had nails on their toes, yoke you like draught-oxen and make you plough up the wars.
lines: There's Ulysses and old Nestor, whose wit was mouldy
ere your grandsires had nails on their toes, yoke you
like draught-oxen and make you plough up the wars.

speeches.speech: What, what?
lines: What, what?

speeches.speech: Yes, good sooth: to, Achilles! to, Ajax! to!
lines: Yes, good sooth: to, Achilles! to, Ajax! to!

speeches.speech: I shall cut out your tongue.
lines: I shall cut out your tongue.

speeches.speech: 'Tis no matter! I shall speak as much as thou afterwards.
lines: 'Tis no matter! I shall speak as much as thou
afterwards.

speeches.speech: No more words, Thersites; peace!
lines: No more words, Thersites; peace!

speeches.speech: I will hold my peace when Achilles' brach bids me, shall I?
lines: I will hold my peace when Achilles' brach bids me, shall I?

speeches.speech: There's for you, Patroclus.
lines: There's for you, Patroclus.

speeches.speech: I will see you hanged, like clotpoles, ere I come any more to your tents: I will keep where there is wit stirring and leave the faction of fools.
lines: I will see you hanged, like clotpoles, ere I come
any more to your tents: I will keep where there is
wit stirring and leave the faction of fools.

speeches.speech: A good riddance.
lines: A good riddance.

speeches.speech: Marry, this, sir, is proclaim'd through all our host: That Hector, by the fifth hour of the sun, Will with a trumpet 'twixt our tents and Troy To-morrow morning call some knight to arms That hath a stomach; and such a one that dare Maintain--I know not what: 'tis trash. Farewell.
lines: Marry, this, sir, is proclaim'd through all our host:
That Hector, by the fifth hour of the sun,
Will with a trumpet 'twixt our tents and Troy
To-morrow morning call some knight to arms
That hath a stomach; and such a one that dare
Maintain--I know not what: 'tis trash. Farewell.

speeches.speech: Farewell. Who shall answer him?
lines: Farewell. Who shall answer him?

speeches.speech: I know not: 'tis put to lottery; otherwise He knew his man.
lines: I know not: 'tis put to lottery; otherwise
He knew his man.

speeches.speech: O, meaning you. I will go learn more of it.
lines: O, meaning you. I will go learn more of it.

speeches.speech: After so many hours, lives, speeches spent, Thus once again says Nestor from the Greeks: 'Deliver Helen, and all damage else-- As honour, loss of time, travail, expense, Wounds, friends, and what else dear that is consumed In hot digestion of this cormorant war-- Shall be struck off.' Hector, what say you to't?
lines: After so many hours, lives, speeches spent,
Thus once again says Nestor from the Greeks:
'Deliver Helen, and all damage else--
As honour, loss of time, travail, expense,
Wounds, friends, and what else dear that is consumed
In hot digestion of this cormorant war--
Shall be struck off.' Hector, what say you to't?

speeches.speech: Though no man lesser fears the Greeks than I As far as toucheth my particular, Yet, dread Priam, There is no lady of more softer bowels, More spongy to suck in the sense of fear, More ready to cry out 'Who knows what follows?' Than Hector is: the wound of peace is surety, Surety secure; but modest doubt is call'd The beacon of the wise, the tent that searches To the bottom of the worst. Let Helen go: Since the first sword was drawn about this question, Every tithe soul, 'mongst many thousand dismes, Hath been as dear as Helen; I mean, of ours: If we have lost so many tenths of ours, To guard a thing not ours nor worth to us, Had it our name, the value of one ten, What merit's in that reason which denies The yielding of her up?
lines: Though no man lesser fears the Greeks than I
As far as toucheth my particular,
Yet, dread Priam,
There is no lady of more softer bowels,
More spongy to suck in the sense of fear,
More ready to cry out 'Who knows what follows?'
Than Hector is: the wound of peace is surety,
Surety secure; but modest doubt is call'd
The beacon of the wise, the tent that searches
To the bottom of the worst. Let Helen go:
Since the first sword was drawn about this question,
Every tithe soul, 'mongst many thousand dismes,
Hath been as dear as Helen; I mean, of ours:
If we have lost so many tenths of ours,
To guard a thing not ours nor worth to us,
Had it our name, the value of one ten,
What merit's in that reason which denies
The yielding of her up?

speeches.speech: Fie, fie, my brother! Weigh you the worth and honour of a king So great as our dread father in a scale Of common ounces? will you with counters sum The past proportion of his infinite? And buckle in a waist most fathomless With spans and inches so diminutive As fears and reasons? fie, for godly shame!
lines: Fie, fie, my brother!
Weigh you the worth and honour of a king
So great as our dread father in a scale
Of common ounces? will you with counters sum
The past proportion of his infinite?
And buckle in a waist most fathomless
With spans and inches so diminutive
As fears and reasons? fie, for godly shame!

speeches.speech: No marvel, though you bite so sharp at reasons, You are so empty of them. Should not our father Bear the great sway of his affairs with reasons, Because your speech hath none that tells him so?
lines: No marvel, though you bite so sharp at reasons,
You are so empty of them. Should not our father
Bear the great sway of his affairs with reasons,
Because your speech hath none that tells him so?

speeches.speech: You are for dreams and slumbers, brother priest; You fur your gloves with reason. Here are your reasons: You know an enemy intends you harm; You know a sword employ'd is perilous, And reason flies the object of all harm: Who marvels then, when Helenus beholds A Grecian and his sword, if he do set The very wings of reason to his heels And fly like chidden Mercury from Jove, Or like a star disorb'd? Nay, if we talk of reason, Let's shut our gates and sleep: manhood and honour Should have hare-hearts, would they but fat their thoughts With this cramm'd reason: reason and respect Make livers pale and lustihood deject.
lines: You are for dreams and slumbers, brother priest;
You fur your gloves with reason. Here are
your reasons:
You know an enemy intends you harm;
You know a sword employ'd is perilous,
And reason flies the object of all harm:
Who marvels then, when Helenus beholds
A Grecian and his sword, if he do set
The very wings of reason to his heels
And fly like chidden Mercury from Jove,
Or like a star disorb'd? Nay, if we talk of reason,
Let's shut our gates and sleep: manhood and honour
Should have hare-hearts, would they but fat
their thoughts
With this cramm'd reason: reason and respect
Make livers pale and lustihood deject.

speeches.speech: Brother, she is not worth what she doth cost The holding.
lines: Brother, she is not worth what she doth cost
The holding.

speeches.speech: What is aught, but as 'tis valued?
lines: What is aught, but as 'tis valued?

speeches.speech: But value dwells not in particular will; It holds his estimate and dignity As well wherein 'tis precious of itself As in the prizer: 'tis mad idolatry To make the service greater than the god And the will dotes that is attributive To what infectiously itself affects, Without some image of the affected merit.
lines: But value dwells not in particular will;
It holds his estimate and dignity
As well wherein 'tis precious of itself
As in the prizer: 'tis mad idolatry
To make the service greater than the god
And the will dotes that is attributive
To what infectiously itself affects,
Without some image of the affected merit.

speeches.speech: I take to-day a wife, and my election Is led on in the conduct of my will; My will enkindled by mine eyes and ears, Two traded pilots 'twixt the dangerous shores Of will and judgment: how may I avoid, Although my will distaste what it elected, The wife I chose? there can be no evasion To blench from this and to stand firm by honour: We turn not back the silks upon the merchant, When we have soil'd them, nor the remainder viands We do not throw in unrespective sieve, Because we now are full. It was thought meet Paris should do some vengeance on the Greeks: Your breath of full consent bellied his sails; The seas and winds, old wranglers, took a truce And did him service: he touch'd the ports desired, And for an old aunt whom the Greeks held captive, He brought a Grecian queen, whose youth and freshness Wrinkles Apollo's, and makes stale the morning. Why keep we her? the Grecians keep our aunt: Is she worth keeping? why, she is a pearl, Whose price hath launch'd above a thousand ships, And turn'd crown'd kings to merchants. If you'll avouch 'twas wisdom Paris went-- As you must needs, for you all cried 'Go, go,'-- If you'll confess he brought home noble prize-- As you must needs, for you all clapp'd your hands And cried 'Inestimable!'--why do you now The issue of your proper wisdoms rate, And do a deed that fortune never did, Beggar the estimation which you prized Richer than sea and land? O, theft most base, That we have stol'n what we do fear to keep! But, thieves, unworthy of a thing so stol'n, That in their country did them that disgrace, We fear to warrant in our native place!
lines: I take to-day a wife, and my election
Is led on in the conduct of my will;
My will enkindled by mine eyes and ears,
Two traded pilots 'twixt the dangerous shores
Of will and judgment: how may I avoid,
Although my will distaste what it elected,
The wife I chose? there can be no evasion
To blench from this and to stand firm by honour:
We turn not back the silks upon the merchant,
When we have soil'd them, nor the remainder viands
We do not throw in unrespective sieve,
Because we now are full. It was thought meet
Paris should do some vengeance on the Greeks:
Your breath of full consent bellied his sails;
The seas and winds, old wranglers, took a truce
And did him service: he touch'd the ports desired,
And for an old aunt whom the Greeks held captive,
He brought a Grecian queen, whose youth and freshness
Wrinkles Apollo's, and makes stale the morning.
Why keep we her? the Grecians keep our aunt:
Is she worth keeping? why, she is a pearl,
Whose price hath launch'd above a thousand ships,
And turn'd crown'd kings to merchants.
If you'll avouch 'twas wisdom Paris went--
As you must needs, for you all cried 'Go, go,'--
If you'll confess he brought home noble prize--
As you must needs, for you all clapp'd your hands
And cried 'Inestimable!'--why do you now
The issue of your proper wisdoms rate,
And do a deed that fortune never did,
Beggar the estimation which you prized
Richer than sea and land? O, theft most base,
That we have stol'n what we do fear to keep!
But, thieves, unworthy of a thing so stol'n,
That in their country did them that disgrace,
We fear to warrant in our native place!

speeches.speech: [Within] Cry, Trojans, cry!
lines: [Within] Cry, Trojans, cry!

speeches.speech: What noise? what shriek is this?
lines: What noise? what shriek is this?

speeches.speech: 'Tis our mad sister, I do know her voice.
lines: 'Tis our mad sister, I do know her voice.

speeches.speech: [Within] Cry, Trojans!
lines: [Within] Cry, Trojans!

speeches.speech: It is Cassandra.
lines: It is Cassandra.

speeches.speech: Cry, Trojans, cry! lend me ten thousand eyes, And I will fill them with prophetic tears.
lines: Cry, Trojans, cry! lend me ten thousand eyes,
And I will fill them with prophetic tears.

speeches.speech: Peace, sister, peace!
lines: Peace, sister, peace!

speeches.speech: Virgins and boys, mid-age and wrinkled eld, Soft infancy, that nothing canst but cry, Add to my clamours! let us pay betimes A moiety of that mass of moan to come. Cry, Trojans, cry! practise your eyes with tears! Troy must not be, nor goodly Ilion stand; Our firebrand brother, Paris, burns us all. Cry, Trojans, cry! a Helen and a woe: Cry, cry! Troy burns, or else let Helen go.
lines: Virgins and boys, mid-age and wrinkled eld,
Soft infancy, that nothing canst but cry,
Add to my clamours! let us pay betimes
A moiety of that mass of moan to come.
Cry, Trojans, cry! practise your eyes with tears!
Troy must not be, nor goodly Ilion stand;
Our firebrand brother, Paris, burns us all.
Cry, Trojans, cry! a Helen and a woe:
Cry, cry! Troy burns, or else let Helen go.

speeches.speech: Now, youthful Troilus, do not these high strains Of divination in our sister work Some touches of remorse? or is your blood So madly hot that no discourse of reason, Nor fear of bad success in a bad cause, Can qualify the same?
lines: Now, youthful Troilus, do not these high strains
Of divination in our sister work
Some touches of remorse? or is your blood
So madly hot that no discourse of reason,
Nor fear of bad success in a bad cause,
Can qualify the same?

speeches.speech: Why, brother Hector, We may not think the justness of each act Such and no other than event doth form it, Nor once deject the courage of our minds, Because Cassandra's mad: her brain-sick raptures Cannot distaste the goodness of a quarrel Which hath our several honours all engaged To make it gracious. For my private part, I am no more touch'd than all Priam's sons: And Jove forbid there should be done amongst us Such things as might offend the weakest spleen To fight for and maintain!
lines: Why, brother Hector,
We may not think the justness of each act
Such and no other than event doth form it,
Nor once deject the courage of our minds,
Because Cassandra's mad: her brain-sick raptures
Cannot distaste the goodness of a quarrel
Which hath our several honours all engaged
To make it gracious. For my private part,
I am no more touch'd than all Priam's sons:
And Jove forbid there should be done amongst us
Such things as might offend the weakest spleen
To fight for and maintain!

speeches.speech: Else might the world convince of levity As well my undertakings as your counsels: But I attest the gods, your full consent Gave wings to my propension and cut off All fears attending on so dire a project. For what, alas, can these my single arms? What Propugnation is in one man's valour, To stand the push and enmity of those This quarrel would excite? Yet, I protest, Were I alone to pass the difficulties And had as ample power as I have will, Paris should ne'er retract what he hath done, Nor faint in the pursuit.
lines: Else might the world convince of levity
As well my undertakings as your counsels:
But I attest the gods, your full consent
Gave wings to my propension and cut off
All fears attending on so dire a project.
For what, alas, can these my single arms?
What Propugnation is in one man's valour,
To stand the push and enmity of those
This quarrel would excite? Yet, I protest,
Were I alone to pass the difficulties
And had as ample power as I have will,
Paris should ne'er retract what he hath done,
Nor faint in the pursuit.

speeches.speech: Paris, you speak Like one besotted on your sweet delights: You have the honey still, but these the gall; So to be valiant is no praise at all.
lines: Paris, you speak
Like one besotted on your sweet delights:
You have the honey still, but these the gall;
So to be valiant is no praise at all.

speeches.speech: Sir, I propose not merely to myself The pleasures such a beauty brings with it; But I would have the soil of her fair rape Wiped off, in honourable keeping her. What treason were it to the ransack'd queen, Disgrace to your great worths and shame to me, Now to deliver her possession up On terms of base compulsion! Can it be That so degenerate a strain as this Should once set footing in your generous bosoms? There's not the meanest spirit on our party Without a heart to dare or sword to draw When Helen is defended, nor none so noble Whose life were ill bestow'd or death unfamed Where Helen is the subject; then, I say, Well may we fight for her whom, we know well, The world's large spaces cannot parallel.
lines: Sir, I propose not merely to myself
The pleasures such a beauty brings with it;
But I would have the soil of her fair rape
Wiped off, in honourable keeping her.
What treason were it to the ransack'd queen,
Disgrace to your great worths and shame to me,
Now to deliver her possession up
On terms of base compulsion! Can it be
That so degenerate a strain as this
Should once set footing in your generous bosoms?
There's not the meanest spirit on our party
Without a heart to dare or sword to draw
When Helen is defended, nor none so noble
Whose life were ill bestow'd or death unfamed
Where Helen is the subject; then, I say,
Well may we fight for her whom, we know well,
The world's large spaces cannot parallel.

speeches.speech: Paris and Troilus, you have both said well, And on the cause and question now in hand Have glozed, but superficially: not much Unlike young men, whom Aristotle thought Unfit to hear moral philosophy: The reasons you allege do more conduce To the hot passion of distemper'd blood Than to make up a free determination 'Twixt right and wrong, for pleasure and revenge Have ears more deaf than adders to the voice Of any true decision. Nature craves All dues be render'd to their owners: now, What nearer debt in all humanity Than wife is to the husband? If this law Of nature be corrupted through affection, And that great minds, of partial indulgence To their benumbed wills, resist the same, There is a law in each well-order'd nation To curb those raging appetites that are Most disobedient and refractory. If Helen then be wife to Sparta's king, As it is known she is, these moral laws Of nature and of nations speak aloud To have her back return'd: thus to persist In doing wrong extenuates not wrong, But makes it much more heavy. Hector's opinion Is this in way of truth; yet ne'ertheless, My spritely brethren, I propend to you In resolution to keep Helen still, For 'tis a cause that hath no mean dependance Upon our joint and several dignities.
lines: Paris and Troilus, you have both said well,
And on the cause and question now in hand
Have glozed, but superficially: not much
Unlike young men, whom Aristotle thought
Unfit to hear moral philosophy:
The reasons you allege do more conduce
To the hot passion of distemper'd blood
Than to make up a free determination
'Twixt right and wrong, for pleasure and revenge
Have ears more deaf than adders to the voice
Of any true decision. Nature craves
All dues be render'd to their owners: now,
What nearer debt in all humanity
Than wife is to the husband? If this law
Of nature be corrupted through affection,
And that great minds, of partial indulgence
To their benumbed wills, resist the same,
There is a law in each well-order'd nation
To curb those raging appetites that are
Most disobedient and refractory.
If Helen then be wife to Sparta's king,
As it is known she is, these moral laws
Of nature and of nations speak aloud
To have her back return'd: thus to persist
In doing wrong extenuates not wrong,
But makes it much more heavy. Hector's opinion
Is this in way of truth; yet ne'ertheless,
My spritely brethren, I propend to you
In resolution to keep Helen still,
For 'tis a cause that hath no mean dependance
Upon our joint and several dignities.

speeches.speech: Why, there you touch'd the life of our design: Were it not glory that we more affected Than the performance of our heaving spleens, I would not wish a drop of Trojan blood Spent more in her defence. But, worthy Hector, She is a theme of honour and renown, A spur to valiant and magnanimous deeds, Whose present courage may beat down our foes, And fame in time to come canonize us; For, I presume, brave Hector would not lose So rich advantage of a promised glory As smiles upon the forehead of this action For the wide world's revenue.
lines: Why, there you touch'd the life of our design:
Were it not glory that we more affected
Than the performance of our heaving spleens,
I would not wish a drop of Trojan blood
Spent more in her defence. But, worthy Hector,
She is a theme of honour and renown,
A spur to valiant and magnanimous deeds,
Whose present courage may beat down our foes,
And fame in time to come canonize us;
For, I presume, brave Hector would not lose
So rich advantage of a promised glory
As smiles upon the forehead of this action
For the wide world's revenue.

speeches.speech: I am yours, You valiant offspring of great Priamus. I have a roisting challenge sent amongst The dun and factious nobles of the Greeks Will strike amazement to their drowsy spirits: I was advertised their great general slept, Whilst emulation in the army crept: This, I presume, will wake him.
lines: I am yours,
You valiant offspring of great Priamus.
I have a roisting challenge sent amongst
The dun and factious nobles of the Greeks
Will strike amazement to their drowsy spirits:
I was advertised their great general slept,
Whilst emulation in the army crept:
This, I presume, will wake him.

speeches.speech: How now, Thersites! what lost in the labyrinth of thy fury! Shall the elephant Ajax carry it thus? He beats me, and I rail at him: O, worthy satisfaction! would it were otherwise; that I could beat him, whilst he railed at me. 'Sfoot, I'll learn to conjure and raise devils, but I'll see some issue of my spiteful execrations. Then there's Achilles, a rare enginer! If Troy be not taken till these two undermine it, the walls will stand till they fall of themselves. O thou great thunder-darter of Olympus, forget that thou art Jove, the king of gods and, Mercury, lose all the serpentine craft of thy caduceus, if ye take not that little, little less than little wit from them that they have! which short-armed ignorance itself knows is so abundant scarce, it will not in circumvention deliver a fly from a spider, without drawing their massy irons and cutting the web. After this, the vengeance on the whole camp! or rather, the bone-ache! for that, methinks, is the curse dependent on those that war for a placket. I have said my prayers and devil Envy say Amen. What ho! my Lord Achilles!
lines: How now, Thersites! what lost in the labyrinth of
thy fury! Shall the elephant Ajax carry it thus? He
beats me, and I rail at him: O, worthy satisfaction!
would it were otherwise; that I could beat him,
whilst he railed at me. 'Sfoot, I'll learn to
conjure and raise devils, but I'll see some issue of
my spiteful execrations. Then there's Achilles, a
rare enginer! If Troy be not taken till these two
undermine it, the walls will stand till they fall of
themselves. O thou great thunder-darter of Olympus,
forget that thou art Jove, the king of gods and,
Mercury, lose all the serpentine craft of thy
caduceus, if ye take not that little, little less
than little wit from them that they have! which
short-armed ignorance itself knows is so abundant
scarce, it will not in circumvention deliver a fly
from a spider, without drawing their massy irons and
cutting the web. After this, the vengeance on the
whole camp! or rather, the bone-ache! for that,
methinks, is the curse dependent on those that war
for a placket. I have said my prayers and devil Envy
say Amen. What ho! my Lord Achilles!

speeches.speech: Who's there? Thersites! Good Thersites, come in and rail.
lines: Who's there? Thersites! Good Thersites, come in and rail.

speeches.speech: If I could have remembered a gilt counterfeit, thou wouldst not have slipped out of my contemplation: but it is no matter; thyself upon thyself! The common curse of mankind, folly and ignorance, be thine in great revenue! heaven bless thee from a tutor, and discipline come not near thee! Let thy blood be thy direction till thy death! then if she that lays thee out says thou art a fair corse, I'll be sworn and sworn upon't she never shrouded any but lazars. Amen. Where's Achilles?
lines: If I could have remembered a gilt counterfeit, thou
wouldst not have slipped out of my contemplation: but
it is no matter; thyself upon thyself! The common
curse of mankind, folly and ignorance, be thine in
great revenue! heaven bless thee from a tutor, and
discipline come not near thee! Let thy blood be thy
direction till thy death! then if she that lays thee
out says thou art a fair corse, I'll be sworn and
sworn upon't she never shrouded any but lazars.
Amen. Where's Achilles?

speeches.speech: What, art thou devout? wast thou in prayer?
lines: What, art thou devout? wast thou in prayer?

speeches.speech: Ay: the heavens hear me!
lines: Ay: the heavens hear me!

speeches.speech: Who's there?
lines: Who's there?

speeches.speech: Thersites, my lord.
lines: Thersites, my lord.

speeches.speech: Where, where? Art thou come? why, my cheese, my digestion, why hast thou not served thyself in to my table so many meals? Come, what's Agamemnon?
lines: Where, where? Art thou come? why, my cheese, my
digestion, why hast thou not served thyself in to
my table so many meals? Come, what's Agamemnon?

speeches.speech: Thy commander, Achilles. Then tell me, Patroclus, what's Achilles?
lines: Thy commander, Achilles. Then tell me, Patroclus,
what's Achilles?

speeches.speech: Thy lord, Thersites: then tell me, I pray thee, what's thyself?
lines: Thy lord, Thersites: then tell me, I pray thee,
what's thyself?

speeches.speech: Thy knower, Patroclus: then tell me, Patroclus, what art thou?
lines: Thy knower, Patroclus: then tell me, Patroclus,
what art thou?

speeches.speech: Thou mayst tell that knowest.
lines: Thou mayst tell that knowest.

speeches.speech: O, tell, tell.
lines: O, tell, tell.

speeches.speech: I'll decline the whole question. Agamemnon commands Achilles; Achilles is my lord; I am Patroclus' knower, and Patroclus is a fool.
lines: I'll decline the whole question. Agamemnon commands
Achilles; Achilles is my lord; I am Patroclus'
knower, and Patroclus is a fool.

speeches.speech: You rascal!
lines: You rascal!

speeches.speech: Peace, fool! I have not done.
lines: Peace, fool! I have not done.

speeches.speech: He is a privileged man. Proceed, Thersites.
lines: He is a privileged man. Proceed, Thersites.

speeches.speech: Agamemnon is a fool; Achilles is a fool; Thersites is a fool, and, as aforesaid, Patroclus is a fool.
lines: Agamemnon is a fool; Achilles is a fool; Thersites
is a fool, and, as aforesaid, Patroclus is a fool.

speeches.speech: Derive this; come.
lines: Derive this; come.

speeches.speech: Agamemnon is a fool to offer to command Achilles; Achilles is a fool to be commanded of Agamemnon; Thersites is a fool to serve such a fool, and Patroclus is a fool positive.
lines: Agamemnon is a fool to offer to command Achilles;
Achilles is a fool to be commanded of Agamemnon;
Thersites is a fool to serve such a fool, and
Patroclus is a fool positive.

speeches.speech: Why am I a fool?
lines: Why am I a fool?

speeches.speech: Make that demand of the prover. It suffices me thou art. Look you, who comes here?
lines: Make that demand of the prover. It suffices me thou
art. Look you, who comes here?

speeches.speech: Patroclus, I'll speak with nobody. Come in with me, Thersites.
lines: Patroclus, I'll speak with nobody.
Come in with me, Thersites.

speeches.speech: Here is such patchery, such juggling and such knavery! all the argument is a cuckold and a whore; a good quarrel to draw emulous factions and bleed to death upon. Now, the dry serpigo on the subject! and war and lechery confound all!
lines: Here is such patchery, such juggling and such
knavery! all the argument is a cuckold and a
whore; a good quarrel to draw emulous factions
and bleed to death upon. Now, the dry serpigo on
the subject! and war and lechery confound all!

speeches.speech: Where is Achilles?
lines: Where is Achilles?

speeches.speech: Within his tent; but ill disposed, my lord.
lines: Within his tent; but ill disposed, my lord.

speeches.speech: Let it be known to him that we are here. He shent our messengers; and we lay by Our appertainments, visiting of him: Let him be told so; lest perchance he think We dare not move the question of our place, Or know not what we are.
lines: Let it be known to him that we are here.
He shent our messengers; and we lay by
Our appertainments, visiting of him:
Let him be told so; lest perchance he think
We dare not move the question of our place,
Or know not what we are.

speeches.speech: I shall say so to him.
lines: I shall say so to him.

speeches.speech: We saw him at the opening of his tent: He is not sick.
lines: We saw him at the opening of his tent:
He is not sick.

speeches.speech: Yes, lion-sick, sick of proud heart: you may call it melancholy, if you will favour the man; but, by my head, 'tis pride: but why, why? let him show us the cause. A word, my lord.
lines: Yes, lion-sick, sick of proud heart: you may call it
melancholy, if you will favour the man; but, by my
head, 'tis pride: but why, why? let him show us the
cause. A word, my lord.

speeches.speech: What moves Ajax thus to bay at him?
lines: What moves Ajax thus to bay at him?

speeches.speech: Achilles hath inveigled his fool from him.
lines: Achilles hath inveigled his fool from him.

speeches.speech: Who, Thersites?
lines: Who, Thersites?

speeches.speech: He.
lines: He.

speeches.speech: Then will Ajax lack matter, if he have lost his argument.
lines: Then will Ajax lack matter, if he have lost his argument.

speeches.speech: No, you see, he is his argument that has his argument, Achilles.
lines: No, you see, he is his argument that has his
argument, Achilles.

speeches.speech: All the better; their fraction is more our wish than their faction: but it was a strong composure a fool could disunite.
lines: All the better; their fraction is more our wish than
their faction: but it was a strong composure a fool
could disunite.

speeches.speech: The amity that wisdom knits not, folly may easily untie. Here comes Patroclus.
lines: The amity that wisdom knits not, folly may easily
untie. Here comes Patroclus.

speeches.speech: No Achilles with him.
lines: No Achilles with him.

speeches.speech: The elephant hath joints, but none for courtesy: his legs are legs for necessity, not for flexure.
lines: The elephant hath joints, but none for courtesy:
his legs are legs for necessity, not for flexure.

speeches.speech: Achilles bids me say, he is much sorry, If any thing more than your sport and pleasure Did move your greatness and this noble state To call upon him; he hopes it is no other But for your health and your digestion sake, And after-dinner's breath.
lines: Achilles bids me say, he is much sorry,
If any thing more than your sport and pleasure
Did move your greatness and this noble state
To call upon him; he hopes it is no other
But for your health and your digestion sake,
And after-dinner's breath.

speeches.speech: Hear you, Patroclus: We are too well acquainted with these answers: But his evasion, wing'd thus swift with scorn, Cannot outfly our apprehensions. Much attribute he hath, and much the reason Why we ascribe it to him; yet all his virtues, Not virtuously on his own part beheld, Do in our eyes begin to lose their gloss, Yea, like fair fruit in an unwholesome dish, Are like to rot untasted. Go and tell him, We come to speak with him; and you shall not sin, If you do say we think him over-proud And under-honest, in self-assumption greater Than in the note of judgment; and worthier than himself Here tend the savage strangeness he puts on, Disguise the holy strength of their command, And underwrite in an observing kind His humorous predominance; yea, watch His pettish lunes, his ebbs, his flows, as if The passage and whole carriage of this action Rode on his tide. Go tell him this, and add, That if he overhold his price so much, We'll none of him; but let him, like an engine Not portable, lie under this report: 'Bring action hither, this cannot go to war: A stirring dwarf we do allowance give Before a sleeping giant.' Tell him so.
lines: Hear you, Patroclus:
We are too well acquainted with these answers:
But his evasion, wing'd thus swift with scorn,
Cannot outfly our apprehensions.
Much attribute he hath, and much the reason
Why we ascribe it to him; yet all his virtues,
Not virtuously on his own part beheld,
Do in our eyes begin to lose their gloss,
Yea, like fair fruit in an unwholesome dish,
Are like to rot untasted. Go and tell him,
We come to speak with him; and you shall not sin,
If you do say we think him over-proud
And under-honest, in self-assumption greater
Than in the note of judgment; and worthier
than himself
Here tend the savage strangeness he puts on,
Disguise the holy strength of their command,
And underwrite in an observing kind
His humorous predominance; yea, watch
His pettish lunes, his ebbs, his flows, as if
The passage and whole carriage of this action
Rode on his tide. Go tell him this, and add,
That if he overhold his price so much,
We'll none of him; but let him, like an engine
Not portable, lie under this report:
'Bring action hither, this cannot go to war:
A stirring dwarf we do allowance give
Before a sleeping giant.' Tell him so.

speeches.speech: I shall; and bring his answer presently.
lines: I shall; and bring his answer presently.

speeches.speech: In second voice we'll not be satisfied; We come to speak with him. Ulysses, enter you.
lines: In second voice we'll not be satisfied;
We come to speak with him. Ulysses, enter you.

speeches.speech: What is he more than another?
lines: What is he more than another?

speeches.speech: No more than what he thinks he is.
lines: No more than what he thinks he is.

speeches.speech: Is he so much? Do you not think he thinks himself a better man than I am?
lines: Is he so much? Do you not think he thinks himself a
better man than I am?

speeches.speech: No question.
lines: No question.

speeches.speech: Will you subscribe his thought, and say he is?
lines: Will you subscribe his thought, and say he is?

speeches.speech: No, noble Ajax; you are as strong, as valiant, as wise, no less noble, much more gentle, and altogether more tractable.
lines: No, noble Ajax; you are as strong, as valiant, as
wise, no less noble, much more gentle, and altogether
more tractable.

speeches.speech: Why should a man be proud? How doth pride grow? I know not what pride is.
lines: Why should a man be proud? How doth pride grow? I
know not what pride is.

speeches.speech: Your mind is the clearer, Ajax, and your virtues the fairer. He that is proud eats up himself: pride is his own glass, his own trumpet, his own chronicle; and whatever praises itself but in the deed, devours the deed in the praise.
lines: Your mind is the clearer, Ajax, and your virtues the
fairer. He that is proud eats up himself: pride is
his own glass, his own trumpet, his own chronicle;
and whatever praises itself but in the deed, devours
the deed in the praise.

speeches.speech: I do hate a proud man, as I hate the engendering of toads.
lines: I do hate a proud man, as I hate the engendering of toads.

speeches.speech: Yet he loves himself: is't not strange?
lines: Yet he loves himself: is't not strange?

speeches.speech: Achilles will not to the field to-morrow.
lines: Achilles will not to the field to-morrow.

speeches.speech: What's his excuse?
lines: What's his excuse?

speeches.speech: He doth rely on none, But carries on the stream of his dispose Without observance or respect of any, In will peculiar and in self-admission.
lines: He doth rely on none,
But carries on the stream of his dispose
Without observance or respect of any,
In will peculiar and in self-admission.

speeches.speech: Why will he not upon our fair request Untent his person and share the air with us?
lines: Why will he not upon our fair request
Untent his person and share the air with us?

speeches.speech: Things small as nothing, for request's sake only, He makes important: possess'd he is with greatness, And speaks not to himself but with a pride That quarrels at self-breath: imagined worth Holds in his blood such swoln and hot discourse That 'twixt his mental and his active parts Kingdom'd Achilles in commotion rages And batters down himself: what should I say? He is so plaguy proud that the death-tokens of it Cry 'No recovery.'AGAMEMNON. Let Ajax go to him. Dear lord, go you and greet him in his tent: 'Tis said he holds you well, and will be led At your request a little from himself.
lines: Things small as nothing, for request's sake only,
He makes important: possess'd he is with greatness,
And speaks not to himself but with a pride
That quarrels at self-breath: imagined worth
Holds in his blood such swoln and hot discourse
That 'twixt his mental and his active parts
Kingdom'd Achilles in commotion rages
And batters down himself: what should I say?
He is so plaguy proud that the death-tokens of it
Cry 'No recovery.'AGAMEMNON. Let Ajax go to him.
Dear lord, go you and greet him in his tent:
'Tis said he holds you well, and will be led
At your request a little from himself.

speeches.speech: O Agamemnon, let it not be so! We'll consecrate the steps that Ajax makes When they go from Achilles: shall the proud lord That bastes his arrogance with his own seam And never suffers matter of the world Enter his thoughts, save such as do revolve And ruminate himself, shall he be worshipp'd Of that we hold an idol more than he? No, this thrice worthy and right valiant lord Must not so stale his palm, nobly acquired; Nor, by my will, assubjugate his merit, As amply titled as Achilles is, By going to Achilles: That were to enlard his fat already pride And add more coals to Cancer when he burns With entertaining great Hyperion. This lord go to him! Jupiter forbid, And say in thunder 'Achilles go to him.'
lines: O Agamemnon, let it not be so!
We'll consecrate the steps that Ajax makes
When they go from Achilles: shall the proud lord
That bastes his arrogance with his own seam
And never suffers matter of the world
Enter his thoughts, save such as do revolve
And ruminate himself, shall he be worshipp'd
Of that we hold an idol more than he?
No, this thrice worthy and right valiant lord
Must not so stale his palm, nobly acquired;
Nor, by my will, assubjugate his merit,
As amply titled as Achilles is,
By going to Achilles:
That were to enlard his fat already pride
And add more coals to Cancer when he burns
With entertaining great Hyperion.
This lord go to him! Jupiter forbid,
And say in thunder 'Achilles go to him.'

speeches.speech: [Aside to DIOMEDES] O, this is well; he rubs the vein of him.
lines: [Aside to DIOMEDES] O, this is well; he rubs the
vein of him.

speeches.speech: [Aside to NESTOR] And how his silence drinks up this applause!
lines: [Aside to NESTOR] And how his silence drinks up
this applause!

speeches.speech: If I go to him, with my armed fist I'll pash him o'er the face.
lines: If I go to him, with my armed fist I'll pash him o'er the face.

speeches.speech: O, no, you shall not go.
lines: O, no, you shall not go.

speeches.speech: An a' be proud with me, I'll pheeze his pride: Let me go to him.
lines: An a' be proud with me, I'll pheeze his pride:
Let me go to him.

speeches.speech: Not for the worth that hangs upon our quarrel.
lines: Not for the worth that hangs upon our quarrel.

speeches.speech: A paltry, insolent fellow!
lines: A paltry, insolent fellow!

speeches.speech: How he describes himself!
lines: How he describes himself!

speeches.speech: Can he not be sociable?
lines: Can he not be sociable?

speeches.speech: The raven chides blackness.
lines: The raven chides blackness.

speeches.speech: I'll let his humours blood.
lines: I'll let his humours blood.

speeches.speech: He will be the physician that should be the patient.
lines: He will be the physician that should be the patient.

speeches.speech: An all men were o' my mind,--
lines: An all men were o' my mind,--

speeches.speech: Wit would be out of fashion.
lines: Wit would be out of fashion.

speeches.speech: A' should not bear it so, a' should eat swords first: shall pride carry it?
lines: A' should not bear it so, a' should eat swords first:
shall pride carry it?

speeches.speech: An 'twould, you'ld carry half.
lines: An 'twould, you'ld carry half.

speeches.speech: A' would have ten shares.
lines: A' would have ten shares.

speeches.speech: I will knead him; I'll make him supple.
lines: I will knead him; I'll make him supple.

speeches.speech: He's not yet through warm: force him with praises: pour in, pour in; his ambition is dry.
lines: He's not yet through warm: force him with praises:
pour in, pour in; his ambition is dry.

speeches.speech: [To AGAMEMNON] My lord, you feed too much on this dislike.
lines: [To AGAMEMNON] My lord, you feed too much on this dislike.

speeches.speech: Our noble general, do not do so.
lines: Our noble general, do not do so.

speeches.speech: You must prepare to fight without Achilles.
lines: You must prepare to fight without Achilles.

speeches.speech: Why, 'tis this naming of him does him harm. Here is a man--but 'tis before his face; I will be silent.
lines: Why, 'tis this naming of him does him harm.
Here is a man--but 'tis before his face;
I will be silent.

speeches.speech: Wherefore should you so? He is not emulous, as Achilles is.
lines: Wherefore should you so?
He is not emulous, as Achilles is.

speeches.speech: Know the whole world, he is as valiant.
lines: Know the whole world, he is as valiant.

speeches.speech: A whoreson dog, that shall pelter thus with us! Would he were a Trojan!
lines: A whoreson dog, that shall pelter thus with us!
Would he were a Trojan!

speeches.speech: What a vice were it in Ajax now,--
lines: What a vice were it in Ajax now,--

speeches.speech: If he were proud,--
lines: If he were proud,--

speeches.speech: Or covetous of praise,--
lines: Or covetous of praise,--

speeches.speech: Ay, or surly borne,--
lines: Ay, or surly borne,--

speeches.speech: Or strange, or self-affected!
lines: Or strange, or self-affected!

speeches.speech: Thank the heavens, lord, thou art of sweet composure; Praise him that got thee, she that gave thee suck: Famed be thy tutor, and thy parts of nature Thrice famed, beyond all erudition: But he that disciplined thy arms to fight, Let Mars divide eternity in twain, And give him half: and, for thy vigour, Bull-bearing Milo his addition yield To sinewy Ajax. I will not praise thy wisdom, Which, like a bourn, a pale, a shore, confines Thy spacious and dilated parts: here's Nestor; Instructed by the antiquary times, He must, he is, he cannot but be wise: Put pardon, father Nestor, were your days As green as Ajax' and your brain so temper'd, You should not have the eminence of him, But be as Ajax.
lines: Thank the heavens, lord, thou art of sweet composure;
Praise him that got thee, she that gave thee suck:
Famed be thy tutor, and thy parts of nature
Thrice famed, beyond all erudition:
But he that disciplined thy arms to fight,
Let Mars divide eternity in twain,
And give him half: and, for thy vigour,
Bull-bearing Milo his addition yield
To sinewy Ajax. I will not praise thy wisdom,
Which, like a bourn, a pale, a shore, confines
Thy spacious and dilated parts: here's Nestor;
Instructed by the antiquary times,
He must, he is, he cannot but be wise:
Put pardon, father Nestor, were your days
As green as Ajax' and your brain so temper'd,
You should not have the eminence of him,
But be as Ajax.

speeches.speech: Shall I call you father?
lines: Shall I call you father?

speeches.speech: Ay, my good son.
lines: Ay, my good son.

speeches.speech: Be ruled by him, Lord Ajax.
lines: Be ruled by him, Lord Ajax.

speeches.speech: There is no tarrying here; the hart Achilles Keeps thicket. Please it our great general To call together all his state of war; Fresh kings are come to Troy: to-morrow We must with all our main of power stand fast: And here's a lord,--come knights from east to west, And cull their flower, Ajax shall cope the best.
lines: There is no tarrying here; the hart Achilles
Keeps thicket. Please it our great general
To call together all his state of war;
Fresh kings are come to Troy: to-morrow
We must with all our main of power stand fast:
And here's a lord,--come knights from east to west,
And cull their flower, Ajax shall cope the best.

speeches.speech: Go we to council. Let Achilles sleep: Light boats sail swift, though greater hulks draw deep.
lines: Go we to council. Let Achilles sleep:
Light boats sail swift, though greater hulks draw deep.

speeches.speech: Friend, you! pray you, a word: do not you follow the young Lord Paris?
lines: Friend, you! pray you, a word: do not you follow
the young Lord Paris?

speeches.speech: Ay, sir, when he goes before me.
lines: Ay, sir, when he goes before me.

speeches.speech: You depend upon him, I mean?
lines: You depend upon him, I mean?

speeches.speech: Sir, I do depend upon the lord.
lines: Sir, I do depend upon the lord.

speeches.speech: You depend upon a noble gentleman; I must needs praise him.
lines: You depend upon a noble gentleman; I must needs
praise him.

speeches.speech: The lord be praised!
lines: The lord be praised!

speeches.speech: You know me, do you not?
lines: You know me, do you not?

speeches.speech: Faith, sir, superficially.
lines: Faith, sir, superficially.

speeches.speech: Friend, know me better; I am the Lord Pandarus.
lines: Friend, know me better; I am the Lord Pandarus.

speeches.speech: I hope I shall know your honour better.
lines: I hope I shall know your honour better.

speeches.speech: I do desire it.
lines: I do desire it.

speeches.speech: You are in the state of grace.
lines: You are in the state of grace.

speeches.speech: Grace! not so, friend: honour and lordship are my titles. [Music within] What music is this?
lines: Grace! not so, friend: honour and lordship are my titles.
[Music within]
What music is this?

speeches.speech: I do but partly know, sir: it is music in parts.
lines: I do but partly know, sir: it is music in parts.

speeches.speech: Know you the musicians?
lines: Know you the musicians?

speeches.speech: Wholly, sir.
lines: Wholly, sir.

speeches.speech: Who play they to?
lines: Who play they to?

speeches.speech: To the hearers, sir.
lines: To the hearers, sir.

speeches.speech: At whose pleasure, friend
lines: At whose pleasure, friend

speeches.speech: At mine, sir, and theirs that love music.
lines: At mine, sir, and theirs that love music.

speeches.speech: Command, I mean, friend.
lines: Command, I mean, friend.

speeches.speech: Who shall I command, sir?
lines: Who shall I command, sir?

speeches.speech: Friend, we understand not one another: I am too courtly and thou art too cunning. At whose request do these men play?
lines: Friend, we understand not one another: I am too
courtly and thou art too cunning. At whose request
do these men play?

speeches.speech: That's to 't indeed, sir: marry, sir, at the request of Paris my lord, who's there in person; with him, the mortal Venus, the heart-blood of beauty, love's invisible soul,--
lines: That's to 't indeed, sir: marry, sir, at the request
of Paris my lord, who's there in person; with him,
the mortal Venus, the heart-blood of beauty, love's
invisible soul,--

speeches.speech: Who, my cousin Cressida?
lines: Who, my cousin Cressida?

speeches.speech: No, sir, Helen: could you not find out that by her attributes?
lines: No, sir, Helen: could you not find out that by her
attributes?

speeches.speech: It should seem, fellow, that thou hast not seen the Lady Cressida. I come to speak with Paris from the Prince Troilus: I will make a complimental assault upon him, for my business seethes.
lines: It should seem, fellow, that thou hast not seen the
Lady Cressida. I come to speak with Paris from the
Prince Troilus: I will make a complimental assault
upon him, for my business seethes.

speeches.speech: Sodden business! there's a stewed phrase indeed!
lines: Sodden business! there's a stewed phrase indeed!

speeches.speech: Fair be to you, my lord, and to all this fair company! fair desires, in all fair measure, fairly guide them! especially to you, fair queen! fair thoughts be your fair pillow!
lines: Fair be to you, my lord, and to all this fair
company! fair desires, in all fair measure,
fairly guide them! especially to you, fair queen!
fair thoughts be your fair pillow!

speeches.speech: Dear lord, you are full of fair words.
lines: Dear lord, you are full of fair words.

speeches.speech: You speak your fair pleasure, sweet queen. Fair prince, here is good broken music.
lines: You speak your fair pleasure, sweet queen. Fair
prince, here is good broken music.

speeches.speech: You have broke it, cousin: and, by my life, you shall make it whole again; you shall piece it out with a piece of your performance. Nell, he is full of harmony.
lines: You have broke it, cousin: and, by my life, you
shall make it whole again; you shall piece it out
with a piece of your performance. Nell, he is full
of harmony.

speeches.speech: Truly, lady, no.
lines: Truly, lady, no.

speeches.speech: O, sir,--
lines: O, sir,--

speeches.speech: Rude, in sooth; in good sooth, very rude.
lines: Rude, in sooth; in good sooth, very rude.

speeches.speech: Well said, my lord! well, you say so in fits.
lines: Well said, my lord! well, you say so in fits.

speeches.speech: I have business to my lord, dear queen. My lord, will you vouchsafe me a word?
lines: I have business to my lord, dear queen. My lord,
will you vouchsafe me a word?

speeches.speech: Nay, this shall not hedge us out: we'll hear you sing, certainly.
lines: Nay, this shall not hedge us out: we'll hear you
sing, certainly.

speeches.speech: Well, sweet queen. you are pleasant with me. But, marry, thus, my lord: my dear lord and most esteemed friend, your brother Troilus,--
lines: Well, sweet queen. you are pleasant with me. But,
marry, thus, my lord: my dear lord and most esteemed
friend, your brother Troilus,--

speeches.speech: My Lord Pandarus; honey-sweet lord,--
lines: My Lord Pandarus; honey-sweet lord,--

speeches.speech: Go to, sweet queen, to go:--commends himself most affectionately to you,--
lines: Go to, sweet queen, to go:--commends himself most
affectionately to you,--

speeches.speech: You shall not bob us out of our melody: if you do, our melancholy upon your head!
lines: You shall not bob us out of our melody: if you do,
our melancholy upon your head!

speeches.speech: Sweet queen, sweet queen! that's a sweet queen, i' faith.
lines: Sweet queen, sweet queen! that's a sweet queen, i' faith.

speeches.speech: And to make a sweet lady sad is a sour offence.
lines: And to make a sweet lady sad is a sour offence.

speeches.speech: Nay, that shall not serve your turn; that shall not, in truth, la. Nay, I care not for such words; no, no. And, my lord, he desires you, that if the king call for him at supper, you will make his excuse.
lines: Nay, that shall not serve your turn; that shall not,
in truth, la. Nay, I care not for such words; no,
no. And, my lord, he desires you, that if the king
call for him at supper, you will make his excuse.

speeches.speech: My Lord Pandarus,--
lines: My Lord Pandarus,--

speeches.speech: What says my sweet queen, my very very sweet queen?
lines: What says my sweet queen, my very very sweet queen?

speeches.speech: What exploit's in hand? where sups he to-night?
lines: What exploit's in hand? where sups he to-night?

speeches.speech: Nay, but, my lord,--
lines: Nay, but, my lord,--

speeches.speech: What says my sweet queen? My cousin will fall out with you. You must not know where he sups.
lines: What says my sweet queen? My cousin will fall out
with you. You must not know where he sups.

speeches.speech: I'll lay my life, with my disposer Cressida.
lines: I'll lay my life, with my disposer Cressida.

speeches.speech: No, no, no such matter; you are wide: come, your disposer is sick.
lines: No, no, no such matter; you are wide: come, your
disposer is sick.

speeches.speech: Well, I'll make excuse.
lines: Well, I'll make excuse.

speeches.speech: Ay, good my lord. Why should you say Cressida? no, your poor disposer's sick.
lines: Ay, good my lord. Why should you say Cressida? no,
your poor disposer's sick.

speeches.speech: I spy.
lines: I spy.

speeches.speech: You spy! what do you spy? Come, give me an instrument. Now, sweet queen.
lines: You spy! what do you spy? Come, give me an
instrument. Now, sweet queen.

speeches.speech: Why, this is kindly done.
lines: Why, this is kindly done.

speeches.speech: My niece is horribly in love with a thing you have, sweet queen.
lines: My niece is horribly in love with a thing you have,
sweet queen.

speeches.speech: She shall have it, my lord, if it be not my lord Paris.
lines: She shall have it, my lord, if it be not my lord Paris.

speeches.speech: He! no, she'll none of him; they two are twain.
lines: He! no, she'll none of him; they two are twain.

speeches.speech: Falling in, after falling out, may make them three.
lines: Falling in, after falling out, may make them three.

speeches.speech: Come, come, I'll hear no more of this; I'll sing you a song now.
lines: Come, come, I'll hear no more of this; I'll sing
you a song now.

speeches.speech: Ay, ay, prithee now. By my troth, sweet lord, thou hast a fine forehead.
lines: Ay, ay, prithee now. By my troth, sweet lord, thou
hast a fine forehead.

speeches.speech: Ay, you may, you may.
lines: Ay, you may, you may.

speeches.speech: Let thy song be love: this love will undo us all. O Cupid, Cupid, Cupid!
lines: Let thy song be love: this love will undo us all.
O Cupid, Cupid, Cupid!

speeches.speech: Love! ay, that it shall, i' faith.
lines: Love! ay, that it shall, i' faith.

speeches.speech: Ay, good now, love, love, nothing but love.
lines: Ay, good now, love, love, nothing but love.

speeches.speech: In good troth, it begins so. [Sings] Love, love, nothing but love, still more! For, O, love's bow Shoots buck and doe: The shaft confounds, Not that it wounds, But tickles still the sore. These lovers cry Oh! oh! they die! Yet that which seems the wound to kill, Doth turn oh! oh! to ha! ha! he! So dying love lives still: Oh! oh! a while, but ha! ha! ha! Oh! oh! groans out for ha! ha! ha! Heigh-ho!
lines: In good troth, it begins so.
[Sings]
Love, love, nothing but love, still more!
For, O, love's bow
Shoots buck and doe:
The shaft confounds,
Not that it wounds,
But tickles still the sore.
These lovers cry Oh! oh! they die!
Yet that which seems the wound to kill,
Doth turn oh! oh! to ha! ha! he!
So dying love lives still:
Oh! oh! a while, but ha! ha! ha!
Oh! oh! groans out for ha! ha! ha!
Heigh-ho!

speeches.speech: In love, i' faith, to the very tip of the nose.
lines: In love, i' faith, to the very tip of the nose.

speeches.speech: He eats nothing but doves, love, and that breeds hot blood, and hot blood begets hot thoughts, and hot thoughts beget hot deeds, and hot deeds is love.
lines: He eats nothing but doves, love, and that breeds hot
blood, and hot blood begets hot thoughts, and hot
thoughts beget hot deeds, and hot deeds is love.

speeches.speech: Is this the generation of love? hot blood, hot thoughts, and hot deeds? Why, they are vipers: is love a generation of vipers? Sweet lord, who's a-field to-day?
lines: Is this the generation of love? hot blood, hot
thoughts, and hot deeds? Why, they are vipers:
is love a generation of vipers? Sweet lord, who's
a-field to-day?

speeches.speech: Hector, Deiphobus, Helenus, Antenor, and all the gallantry of Troy: I would fain have armed to-day, but my Nell would not have it so. How chance my brother Troilus went not?
lines: Hector, Deiphobus, Helenus, Antenor, and all the
gallantry of Troy: I would fain have armed to-day,
but my Nell would not have it so. How chance my
brother Troilus went not?

speeches.speech: He hangs the lip at something: you know all, Lord Pandarus.
lines: He hangs the lip at something: you know all, Lord Pandarus.

speeches.speech: Not I, honey-sweet queen. I long to hear how they sped to-day. You'll remember your brother's excuse?
lines: Not I, honey-sweet queen. I long to hear how they
sped to-day. You'll remember your brother's excuse?

speeches.speech: To a hair.
lines: To a hair.

speeches.speech: Farewell, sweet queen.
lines: Farewell, sweet queen.

speeches.speech: Commend me to your niece.
lines: Commend me to your niece.

speeches.speech: I will, sweet queen.
lines: I will, sweet queen.

speeches.speech: They're come from field: let us to Priam's hall, To greet the warriors. Sweet Helen, I must woo you To help unarm our Hector: his stubborn buckles, With these your white enchanting fingers touch'd, Shall more obey than to the edge of steel Or force of Greekish sinews; you shall do more Than all the island kings,--disarm great Hector.
lines: They're come from field: let us to Priam's hall,
To greet the warriors. Sweet Helen, I must woo you
To help unarm our Hector: his stubborn buckles,
With these your white enchanting fingers touch'd,
Shall more obey than to the edge of steel
Or force of Greekish sinews; you shall do more
Than all the island kings,--disarm great Hector.

speeches.speech: 'Twill make us proud to be his servant, Paris; Yea, what he shall receive of us in duty Gives us more palm in beauty than we have, Yea, overshines ourself.
lines: 'Twill make us proud to be his servant, Paris;
Yea, what he shall receive of us in duty
Gives us more palm in beauty than we have,
Yea, overshines ourself.

speeches.speech: Sweet, above thought I love thee.
lines: Sweet, above thought I love thee.

speeches.speech: How now! where's thy master? at my cousin Cressida's?
lines: How now! where's thy master? at my cousin
Cressida's?

speeches.speech: No, sir; he stays for you to conduct him thither.
lines: No, sir; he stays for you to conduct him thither.

speeches.speech: O, here he comes. [Enter TROILUS] How now, how now!
lines: O, here he comes.
[Enter TROILUS]
How now, how now!

speeches.speech: Sirrah, walk off.
lines: Sirrah, walk off.

speeches.speech: Have you seen my cousin?
lines: Have you seen my cousin?

speeches.speech: No, Pandarus: I stalk about her door, Like a strange soul upon the Stygian banks Staying for waftage. O, be thou my Charon, And give me swift transportance to those fields Where I may wallow in the lily-beds Proposed for the deserver! O gentle Pandarus, From Cupid's shoulder pluck his painted wings And fly with me to Cressid!
lines: No, Pandarus: I stalk about her door,
Like a strange soul upon the Stygian banks
Staying for waftage. O, be thou my Charon,
And give me swift transportance to those fields
Where I may wallow in the lily-beds
Proposed for the deserver! O gentle Pandarus,
From Cupid's shoulder pluck his painted wings
And fly with me to Cressid!

speeches.speech: Walk here i' the orchard, I'll bring her straight.
lines: Walk here i' the orchard, I'll bring her straight.

speeches.speech: I am giddy; expectation whirls me round. The imaginary relish is so sweet That it enchants my sense: what will it be, When that the watery palate tastes indeed Love's thrice repured nectar? death, I fear me, Swooning destruction, or some joy too fine, Too subtle-potent, tuned too sharp in sweetness, For the capacity of my ruder powers: I fear it much; and I do fear besides, That I shall lose distinction in my joys; As doth a battle, when they charge on heaps The enemy flying.
lines: I am giddy; expectation whirls me round.
The imaginary relish is so sweet
That it enchants my sense: what will it be,
When that the watery palate tastes indeed
Love's thrice repured nectar? death, I fear me,
Swooning destruction, or some joy too fine,
Too subtle-potent, tuned too sharp in sweetness,
For the capacity of my ruder powers:
I fear it much; and I do fear besides,
That I shall lose distinction in my joys;
As doth a battle, when they charge on heaps
The enemy flying.

speeches.speech: She's making her ready, she'll come straight: you must be witty now. She does so blush, and fetches her wind so short, as if she were frayed with a sprite: I'll fetch her. It is the prettiest villain: she fetches her breath as short as a new-ta'en sparrow.
lines: She's making her ready, she'll come straight: you
must be witty now. She does so blush, and fetches
her wind so short, as if she were frayed with a
sprite: I'll fetch her. It is the prettiest
villain: she fetches her breath as short as a
new-ta'en sparrow.

speeches.speech: Even such a passion doth embrace my bosom: My heart beats thicker than a feverous pulse; And all my powers do their bestowing lose, Like vassalage at unawares encountering The eye of majesty.
lines: Even such a passion doth embrace my bosom:
My heart beats thicker than a feverous pulse;
And all my powers do their bestowing lose,
Like vassalage at unawares encountering
The eye of majesty.

speeches.speech: Come, come, what need you blush? shame's a baby. Here she is now: swear the oaths now to her that you have sworn to me. What, are you gone again? you must be watched ere you be made tame, must you? Come your ways, come your ways; an you draw backward, we'll put you i' the fills. Why do you not speak to her? Come, draw this curtain, and let's see your picture. Alas the day, how loath you are to offend daylight! an 'twere dark, you'ld close sooner. So, so; rub on, and kiss the mistress. How now! a kiss in fee-farm! build there, carpenter; the air is sweet. Nay, you shall fight your hearts out ere I part you. The falcon as the tercel, for all the ducks i' the river: go to, go to.
lines: Come, come, what need you blush? shame's a baby.
Here she is now: swear the oaths now to her that
you have sworn to me. What, are you gone again?
you must be watched ere you be made tame, must you?
Come your ways, come your ways; an you draw backward,
we'll put you i' the fills. Why do you not speak to
her? Come, draw this curtain, and let's see your
picture. Alas the day, how loath you are to offend
daylight! an 'twere dark, you'ld close sooner.
So, so; rub on, and kiss the mistress. How now!
a kiss in fee-farm! build there, carpenter; the air
is sweet. Nay, you shall fight your hearts out ere
I part you. The falcon as the tercel, for all the
ducks i' the river: go to, go to.

speeches.speech: You have bereft me of all words, lady.
lines: You have bereft me of all words, lady.

speeches.speech: Words pay no debts, give her deeds: but she'll bereave you o' the deeds too, if she call your activity in question. What, billing again? Here's 'In witness whereof the parties interchangeably'-- Come in, come in: I'll go get a fire.
lines: Words pay no debts, give her deeds: but she'll
bereave you o' the deeds too, if she call your
activity in question. What, billing again? Here's
'In witness whereof the parties interchangeably'--
Come in, come in: I'll go get a fire.

speeches.speech: Will you walk in, my lord?
lines: Will you walk in, my lord?

speeches.speech: O Cressida, how often have I wished me thus!
lines: O Cressida, how often have I wished me thus!

speeches.speech: Wished, my lord! The gods grant,--O my lord!
lines: Wished, my lord! The gods grant,--O my lord!

speeches.speech: What should they grant? what makes this pretty abruption? What too curious dreg espies my sweet lady in the fountain of our love?
lines: What should they grant? what makes this pretty
abruption? What too curious dreg espies my sweet
lady in the fountain of our love?

speeches.speech: More dregs than water, if my fears have eyes.
lines: More dregs than water, if my fears have eyes.

speeches.speech: Fears make devils of cherubims; they never see truly.
lines: Fears make devils of cherubims; they never see truly.

speeches.speech: Blind fear, that seeing reason leads, finds safer footing than blind reason stumbling without fear: to fear the worst oft cures the worse.
lines: Blind fear, that seeing reason leads, finds safer
footing than blind reason stumbling without fear: to
fear the worst oft cures the worse.

speeches.speech: O, let my lady apprehend no fear: in all Cupid's pageant there is presented no monster.
lines: O, let my lady apprehend no fear: in all Cupid's
pageant there is presented no monster.

speeches.speech: Nor nothing monstrous neither?
lines: Nor nothing monstrous neither?

speeches.speech: Nothing, but our undertakings; when we vow to weep seas, live in fire, eat rocks, tame tigers; thinking it harder for our mistress to devise imposition enough than for us to undergo any difficulty imposed. This is the monstruosity in love, lady, that the will is infinite and the execution confined, that the desire is boundless and the act a slave to limit.
lines: Nothing, but our undertakings; when we vow to weep
seas, live in fire, eat rocks, tame tigers; thinking
it harder for our mistress to devise imposition
enough than for us to undergo any difficulty imposed.
This is the monstruosity in love, lady, that the will
is infinite and the execution confined, that the
desire is boundless and the act a slave to limit.

speeches.speech: They say all lovers swear more performance than they are able and yet reserve an ability that they never perform, vowing more than the perfection of ten and discharging less than the tenth part of one. They that have the voice of lions and the act of hares, are they not monsters?
lines: They say all lovers swear more performance than they
are able and yet reserve an ability that they never
perform, vowing more than the perfection of ten and
discharging less than the tenth part of one. They
that have the voice of lions and the act of hares,
are they not monsters?

speeches.speech: Are there such? such are not we: praise us as we are tasted, allow us as we prove; our head shall go bare till merit crown it: no perfection in reversion shall have a praise in present: we will not name desert before his birth, and, being born, his addition shall be humble. Few words to fair faith: Troilus shall be such to Cressid as what envy can say worst shall be a mock for his truth, and what truth can speak truest not truer than Troilus.
lines: Are there such? such are not we: praise us as we
are tasted, allow us as we prove; our head shall go
bare till merit crown it: no perfection in reversion
shall have a praise in present: we will not name
desert before his birth, and, being born, his addition
shall be humble. Few words to fair faith: Troilus
shall be such to Cressid as what envy can say worst
shall be a mock for his truth, and what truth can
speak truest not truer than Troilus.

speeches.speech: Will you walk in, my lord?
lines: Will you walk in, my lord?

speeches.speech: What, blushing still? have you not done talking yet?
lines: What, blushing still? have you not done talking yet?

speeches.speech: Well, uncle, what folly I commit, I dedicate to you.
lines: Well, uncle, what folly I commit, I dedicate to you.

speeches.speech: I thank you for that: if my lord get a boy of you, you'll give him me. Be true to my lord: if he flinch, chide me for it.
lines: I thank you for that: if my lord get a boy of you,
you'll give him me. Be true to my lord: if he
flinch, chide me for it.

speeches.speech: You know now your hostages; your uncle's word and my firm faith.
lines: You know now your hostages; your uncle's word and my
firm faith.

speeches.speech: Nay, I'll give my word for her too: our kindred, though they be long ere they are wooed, they are constant being won: they are burs, I can tell you; they'll stick where they are thrown.
lines: Nay, I'll give my word for her too: our kindred,
though they be long ere they are wooed, they are
constant being won: they are burs, I can tell you;
they'll stick where they are thrown.

speeches.speech: Boldness comes to me now, and brings me heart. Prince Troilus, I have loved you night and day For many weary months.
lines: Boldness comes to me now, and brings me heart.
Prince Troilus, I have loved you night and day
For many weary months.

speeches.speech: Why was my Cressid then so hard to win?
lines: Why was my Cressid then so hard to win?

speeches.speech: Hard to seem won: but I was won, my lord, With the first glance that ever--pardon me-- If I confess much, you will play the tyrant. I love you now; but not, till now, so much But I might master it: in faith, I lie; My thoughts were like unbridled children, grown Too headstrong for their mother. See, we fools! Why have I blabb'd? who shall be true to us, When we are so unsecret to ourselves? But, though I loved you well, I woo'd you not; And yet, good faith, I wish'd myself a man, Or that we women had men's privilege Of speaking first. Sweet, bid me hold my tongue, For in this rapture I shall surely speak The thing I shall repent. See, see, your silence, Cunning in dumbness, from my weakness draws My very soul of counsel! stop my mouth.
lines: Hard to seem won: but I was won, my lord,
With the first glance that ever--pardon me--
If I confess much, you will play the tyrant.
I love you now; but not, till now, so much
But I might master it: in faith, I lie;
My thoughts were like unbridled children, grown
Too headstrong for their mother. See, we fools!
Why have I blabb'd? who shall be true to us,
When we are so unsecret to ourselves?
But, though I loved you well, I woo'd you not;
And yet, good faith, I wish'd myself a man,
Or that we women had men's privilege
Of speaking first. Sweet, bid me hold my tongue,
For in this rapture I shall surely speak
The thing I shall repent. See, see, your silence,
Cunning in dumbness, from my weakness draws
My very soul of counsel! stop my mouth.

speeches.speech: And shall, albeit sweet music issues thence.
lines: And shall, albeit sweet music issues thence.

speeches.speech: Pretty, i' faith.
lines: Pretty, i' faith.

speeches.speech: My lord, I do beseech you, pardon me; 'Twas not my purpose, thus to beg a kiss: I am ashamed. O heavens! what have I done? For this time will I take my leave, my lord.
lines: My lord, I do beseech you, pardon me;
'Twas not my purpose, thus to beg a kiss:
I am ashamed. O heavens! what have I done?
For this time will I take my leave, my lord.

speeches.speech: Your leave, sweet Cressid!
lines: Your leave, sweet Cressid!

speeches.speech: Leave! an you take leave till to-morrow morning,--
lines: Leave! an you take leave till to-morrow morning,--

speeches.speech: Pray you, content you.
lines: Pray you, content you.

speeches.speech: What offends you, lady?
lines: What offends you, lady?

speeches.speech: Sir, mine own company.
lines: Sir, mine own company.

speeches.speech: You cannot shun Yourself.
lines: You cannot shun Yourself.

speeches.speech: Let me go and try: I have a kind of self resides with you; But an unkind self, that itself will leave, To be another's fool. I would be gone: Where is my wit? I know not what I speak.
lines: Let me go and try:
I have a kind of self resides with you;
But an unkind self, that itself will leave,
To be another's fool. I would be gone:
Where is my wit? I know not what I speak.

speeches.speech: Well know they what they speak that speak so wisely.
lines: Well know they what they speak that speak so wisely.

speeches.speech: Perchance, my lord, I show more craft than love; And fell so roundly to a large confession, To angle for your thoughts: but you are wise, Or else you love not, for to be wise and love Exceeds man's might; that dwells with gods above.
lines: Perchance, my lord, I show more craft than love;
And fell so roundly to a large confession,
To angle for your thoughts: but you are wise,
Or else you love not, for to be wise and love
Exceeds man's might; that dwells with gods above.

speeches.speech: O that I thought it could be in a woman-- As, if it can, I will presume in you-- To feed for aye her ramp and flames of love; To keep her constancy in plight and youth, Outliving beauty's outward, with a mind That doth renew swifter than blood decays! Or that persuasion could but thus convince me, That my integrity and truth to you Might be affronted with the match and weight Of such a winnow'd purity in love; How were I then uplifted! but, alas! I am as true as truth's simplicity And simpler than the infancy of truth.
lines: O that I thought it could be in a woman--
As, if it can, I will presume in you--
To feed for aye her ramp and flames of love;
To keep her constancy in plight and youth,
Outliving beauty's outward, with a mind
That doth renew swifter than blood decays!
Or that persuasion could but thus convince me,
That my integrity and truth to you
Might be affronted with the match and weight
Of such a winnow'd purity in love;
How were I then uplifted! but, alas!
I am as true as truth's simplicity
And simpler than the infancy of truth.

speeches.speech: In that I'll war with you.
lines: In that I'll war with you.

speeches.speech: O virtuous fight, When right with right wars who shall be most right! True swains in love shall in the world to come Approve their truths by Troilus: when their rhymes, Full of protest, of oath and big compare, Want similes, truth tired with iteration, As true as steel, as plantage to the moon, As sun to day, as turtle to her mate, As iron to adamant, as earth to the centre, Yet, after all comparisons of truth, As truth's authentic author to be cited, 'As true as Troilus' shall crown up the verse, And sanctify the numbers.
lines: O virtuous fight,
When right with right wars who shall be most right!
True swains in love shall in the world to come
Approve their truths by Troilus: when their rhymes,
Full of protest, of oath and big compare,
Want similes, truth tired with iteration,
As true as steel, as plantage to the moon,
As sun to day, as turtle to her mate,
As iron to adamant, as earth to the centre,
Yet, after all comparisons of truth,
As truth's authentic author to be cited,
'As true as Troilus' shall crown up the verse,
And sanctify the numbers.

speeches.speech: Prophet may you be! If I be false, or swerve a hair from truth, When time is old and hath forgot itself, When waterdrops have worn the stones of Troy, And blind oblivion swallow'd cities up, And mighty states characterless are grated To dusty nothing, yet let memory, From false to false, among false maids in love, Upbraid my falsehood! when they've said 'as false As air, as water, wind, or sandy earth, As fox to lamb, as wolf to heifer's calf, Pard to the hind, or stepdame to her son,' 'Yea,' let them say, to stick the heart of falsehood, 'As false as Cressid.'
lines: Prophet may you be!
If I be false, or swerve a hair from truth,
When time is old and hath forgot itself,
When waterdrops have worn the stones of Troy,
And blind oblivion swallow'd cities up,
And mighty states characterless are grated
To dusty nothing, yet let memory,
From false to false, among false maids in love,
Upbraid my falsehood! when they've said 'as false
As air, as water, wind, or sandy earth,
As fox to lamb, as wolf to heifer's calf,
Pard to the hind, or stepdame to her son,'
'Yea,' let them say, to stick the heart of falsehood,
'As false as Cressid.'

speeches.speech: Go to, a bargain made: seal it, seal it; I'll be the witness. Here I hold your hand, here my cousin's. If ever you prove false one to another, since I have taken such pains to bring you together, let all pitiful goers-between be called to the world's end after my name; call them all Pandars; let all constant men be Troiluses, all false women Cressids, and all brokers-between Pandars! say, amen.
lines: Go to, a bargain made: seal it, seal it; I'll be the
witness. Here I hold your hand, here my cousin's.
If ever you prove false one to another, since I have
taken such pains to bring you together, let all
pitiful goers-between be called to the world's end
after my name; call them all Pandars; let all
constant men be Troiluses, all false women Cressids,
and all brokers-between Pandars! say, amen.

speeches.speech: Amen.
lines: Amen.

speeches.speech: Amen.
lines: Amen.

speeches.speech: Amen. Whereupon I will show you a chamber with a bed; which bed, because it shall not speak of your pretty encounters, press it to death: away! And Cupid grant all tongue-tied maidens here Bed, chamber, Pandar to provide this gear!
lines: Amen. Whereupon I will show you a chamber with a
bed; which bed, because it shall not speak of your
pretty encounters, press it to death: away!
And Cupid grant all tongue-tied maidens here
Bed, chamber, Pandar to provide this gear!

speeches.speech: Now, princes, for the service I have done you, The advantage of the time prompts me aloud To call for recompense. Appear it to your mind That, through the sight I bear in things to love, I have abandon'd Troy, left my possession, Incurr'd a traitor's name; exposed myself, From certain and possess'd conveniences, To doubtful fortunes; sequestering from me all That time, acquaintance, custom and condition Made tame and most familiar to my nature, And here, to do you service, am become As new into the world, strange, unacquainted: I do beseech you, as in way of taste, To give me now a little benefit, Out of those many register'd in promise, Which, you say, live to come in my behalf.
lines: Now, princes, for the service I have done you,
The advantage of the time prompts me aloud
To call for recompense. Appear it to your mind
That, through the sight I bear in things to love,
I have abandon'd Troy, left my possession,
Incurr'd a traitor's name; exposed myself,
From certain and possess'd conveniences,
To doubtful fortunes; sequestering from me all
That time, acquaintance, custom and condition
Made tame and most familiar to my nature,
And here, to do you service, am become
As new into the world, strange, unacquainted:
I do beseech you, as in way of taste,
To give me now a little benefit,
Out of those many register'd in promise,
Which, you say, live to come in my behalf.

speeches.speech: What wouldst thou of us, Trojan? make demand.
lines: What wouldst thou of us, Trojan? make demand.

speeches.speech: You have a Trojan prisoner, call'd Antenor, Yesterday took: Troy holds him very dear.
