Oft have you--often have you thanks therefore-- Desired my Cressid in right great exchange, Whom Troy hath still denied: but this Antenor, I know, is such a wrest in their affairs That their negotiations all must slack, Wanting his manage; and they will almost Give us a prince of blood, a son of Priam, In change of him: let him be sent, great princes, And he shall buy my daughter; and her presence Shall quite strike off all service I have done, In most accepted pain.
lines: You have a Trojan prisoner, call'd Antenor,
Yesterday took: Troy holds him very dear.
Oft have you--often have you thanks therefore--
Desired my Cressid in right great exchange,
Whom Troy hath still denied: but this Antenor,
I know, is such a wrest in their affairs
That their negotiations all must slack,
Wanting his manage; and they will almost
Give us a prince of blood, a son of Priam,
In change of him: let him be sent, great princes,
And he shall buy my daughter; and her presence
Shall quite strike off all service I have done,
In most accepted pain.

speeches.speech: Let Diomedes bear him, And bring us Cressid hither: Calchas shall have What he requests of us. Good Diomed, Furnish you fairly for this interchange: Withal bring word if Hector will to-morrow Be answer'd in his challenge: Ajax is ready.
lines: Let Diomedes bear him,
And bring us Cressid hither: Calchas shall have
What he requests of us. Good Diomed,
Furnish you fairly for this interchange:
Withal bring word if Hector will to-morrow
Be answer'd in his challenge: Ajax is ready.

speeches.speech: This shall I undertake; and 'tis a burden Which I am proud to bear.
lines: This shall I undertake; and 'tis a burden
Which I am proud to bear.

speeches.speech: Achilles stands i' the entrance of his tent: Please it our general to pass strangely by him, As if he were forgot; and, princes all, Lay negligent and loose regard upon him: I will come last. 'Tis like he'll question me Why such unplausive eyes are bent on him: If so, I have derision medicinable, To use between your strangeness and his pride, Which his own will shall have desire to drink: It may be good: pride hath no other glass To show itself but pride, for supple knees Feed arrogance and are the proud man's fees.
lines: Achilles stands i' the entrance of his tent:
Please it our general to pass strangely by him,
As if he were forgot; and, princes all,
Lay negligent and loose regard upon him:
I will come last. 'Tis like he'll question me
Why such unplausive eyes are bent on him:
If so, I have derision medicinable,
To use between your strangeness and his pride,
Which his own will shall have desire to drink:
It may be good: pride hath no other glass
To show itself but pride, for supple knees
Feed arrogance and are the proud man's fees.

speeches.speech: We'll execute your purpose, and put on A form of strangeness as we pass along: So do each lord, and either greet him not, Or else disdainfully, which shall shake him more Than if not look'd on. I will lead the way.
lines: We'll execute your purpose, and put on
A form of strangeness as we pass along:
So do each lord, and either greet him not,
Or else disdainfully, which shall shake him more
Than if not look'd on. I will lead the way.

speeches.speech: What, comes the general to speak with me? You know my mind, I'll fight no more 'gainst Troy.
lines: What, comes the general to speak with me?
You know my mind, I'll fight no more 'gainst Troy.

speeches.speech: What says Achilles? would he aught with us?
lines: What says Achilles? would he aught with us?

speeches.speech: Would you, my lord, aught with the general?
lines: Would you, my lord, aught with the general?

speeches.speech: No.
lines: No.

speeches.speech: Nothing, my lord.
lines: Nothing, my lord.

speeches.speech: The better.
lines: The better.

speeches.speech: Good day, good day.
lines: Good day, good day.

speeches.speech: How do you? how do you?
lines: How do you? how do you?

speeches.speech: What, does the cuckold scorn me?
lines: What, does the cuckold scorn me?

speeches.speech: How now, Patroclus!
lines: How now, Patroclus!

speeches.speech: Good morrow, Ajax.
lines: Good morrow, Ajax.

speeches.speech: Ha?
lines: Ha?

speeches.speech: Good morrow.
lines: Good morrow.

speeches.speech: Ay, and good next day too.
lines: Ay, and good next day too.

speeches.speech: What mean these fellows? Know they not Achilles?
lines: What mean these fellows? Know they not Achilles?

speeches.speech: They pass by strangely: they were used to bend To send their smiles before them to Achilles; To come as humbly as they used to creep To holy altars.
lines: They pass by strangely: they were used to bend
To send their smiles before them to Achilles;
To come as humbly as they used to creep
To holy altars.

speeches.speech: What, am I poor of late? 'Tis certain, greatness, once fall'n out with fortune, Must fall out with men too: what the declined is He shall as soon read in the eyes of others As feel in his own fall; for men, like butterflies, Show not their mealy wings but to the summer, And not a man, for being simply man, Hath any honour, but honour for those honours That are without him, as place, riches, favour, Prizes of accident as oft as merit: Which when they fall, as being slippery standers, The love that lean'd on them as slippery too, Do one pluck down another and together Die in the fall. But 'tis not so with me: Fortune and I are friends: I do enjoy At ample point all that I did possess, Save these men's looks; who do, methinks, find out Something not worth in me such rich beholding As they have often given. Here is Ulysses; I'll interrupt his reading. How now Ulysses!
lines: What, am I poor of late?
'Tis certain, greatness, once fall'n out with fortune,
Must fall out with men too: what the declined is
He shall as soon read in the eyes of others
As feel in his own fall; for men, like butterflies,
Show not their mealy wings but to the summer,
And not a man, for being simply man,
Hath any honour, but honour for those honours
That are without him, as place, riches, favour,
Prizes of accident as oft as merit:
Which when they fall, as being slippery standers,
The love that lean'd on them as slippery too,
Do one pluck down another and together
Die in the fall. But 'tis not so with me:
Fortune and I are friends: I do enjoy
At ample point all that I did possess,
Save these men's looks; who do, methinks, find out
Something not worth in me such rich beholding
As they have often given. Here is Ulysses;
I'll interrupt his reading.
How now Ulysses!

speeches.speech: Now, great Thetis' son!
lines: Now, great Thetis' son!

speeches.speech: What are you reading?
lines: What are you reading?

speeches.speech: A strange fellow here Writes me: 'That man, how dearly ever parted, How much in having, or without or in, Cannot make boast to have that which he hath, Nor feels not what he owes, but by reflection; As when his virtues shining upon others Heat them and they retort that heat again To the first giver.'
lines: A strange fellow here
Writes me: 'That man, how dearly ever parted,
How much in having, or without or in,
Cannot make boast to have that which he hath,
Nor feels not what he owes, but by reflection;
As when his virtues shining upon others
Heat them and they retort that heat again
To the first giver.'

speeches.speech: This is not strange, Ulysses. The beauty that is borne here in the face The bearer knows not, but commends itself To others' eyes; nor doth the eye itself, That most pure spirit of sense, behold itself, Not going from itself; but eye to eye opposed Salutes each other with each other's form; For speculation turns not to itself, Till it hath travell'd and is mirror'd there Where it may see itself. This is not strange at all.
lines: This is not strange, Ulysses.
The beauty that is borne here in the face
The bearer knows not, but commends itself
To others' eyes; nor doth the eye itself,
That most pure spirit of sense, behold itself,
Not going from itself; but eye to eye opposed
Salutes each other with each other's form;
For speculation turns not to itself,
Till it hath travell'd and is mirror'd there
Where it may see itself. This is not strange at all.

speeches.speech: I do not strain at the position,-- It is familiar,--but at the author's drift; Who, in his circumstance, expressly proves That no man is the lord of any thing, Though in and of him there be much consisting, Till he communicate his parts to others: Nor doth he of himself know them for aught Till he behold them form'd in the applause Where they're extended; who, like an arch, reverberates The voice again, or, like a gate of steel Fronting the sun, receives and renders back His figure and his heat. I was much wrapt in this; And apprehended here immediately The unknown Ajax. Heavens, what a man is there! a very horse, That has he knows not what. Nature, what things there are Most abject in regard and dear in use! What things again most dear in the esteem And poor in worth! Now shall we see to-morrow-- An act that very chance doth throw upon him-- Ajax renown'd. O heavens, what some men do, While some men leave to do! How some men creep in skittish fortune's hall, Whiles others play the idiots in her eyes! How one man eats into another's pride, While pride is fasting in his wantonness! To see these Grecian lords!--why, even already They clap the lubber Ajax on the shoulder, As if his foot were on brave Hector's breast And great Troy shrieking.
lines: I do not strain at the position,--
It is familiar,--but at the author's drift;
Who, in his circumstance, expressly proves
That no man is the lord of any thing,
Though in and of him there be much consisting,
Till he communicate his parts to others:
Nor doth he of himself know them for aught
Till he behold them form'd in the applause
Where they're extended; who, like an arch,
reverberates
The voice again, or, like a gate of steel
Fronting the sun, receives and renders back
His figure and his heat. I was much wrapt in this;
And apprehended here immediately
The unknown Ajax.
Heavens, what a man is there! a very horse,
That has he knows not what. Nature, what things there are
Most abject in regard and dear in use!
What things again most dear in the esteem
And poor in worth! Now shall we see to-morrow--
An act that very chance doth throw upon him--
Ajax renown'd. O heavens, what some men do,
While some men leave to do!
How some men creep in skittish fortune's hall,
Whiles others play the idiots in her eyes!
How one man eats into another's pride,
While pride is fasting in his wantonness!
To see these Grecian lords!--why, even already
They clap the lubber Ajax on the shoulder,
As if his foot were on brave Hector's breast
And great Troy shrieking.

speeches.speech: I do believe it; for they pass'd by me As misers do by beggars, neither gave to me Good word nor look: what, are my deeds forgot?
lines: I do believe it; for they pass'd by me
As misers do by beggars, neither gave to me
Good word nor look: what, are my deeds forgot?

speeches.speech: Time hath, my lord, a wallet at his back, Wherein he puts alms for oblivion, A great-sized monster of ingratitudes: Those scraps are good deeds past; which are devour'd As fast as they are made, forgot as soon As done: perseverance, dear my lord, Keeps honour bright: to have done is to hang Quite out of fashion, like a rusty mail In monumental mockery. Take the instant way; For honour travels in a strait so narrow, Where one but goes abreast: keep then the path; For emulation hath a thousand sons That one by one pursue: if you give way, Or hedge aside from the direct forthright, Like to an enter'd tide, they all rush by And leave you hindmost; Or like a gallant horse fall'n in first rank, Lie there for pavement to the abject rear, O'er-run and trampled on: then what they do in present, Though less than yours in past, must o'ertop yours; For time is like a fashionable host That slightly shakes his parting guest by the hand, And with his arms outstretch'd, as he would fly, Grasps in the comer: welcome ever smiles, And farewell goes out sighing. O, let not virtue seek Remuneration for the thing it was; For beauty, wit, High birth, vigour of bone, desert in service, Love, friendship, charity, are subjects all To envious and calumniating time. One touch of nature makes the whole world kin, That all with one consent praise new-born gawds, Though they are made and moulded of things past, And give to dust that is a little gilt More laud than gilt o'er-dusted. The present eye praises the present object. Then marvel not, thou great and complete man, That all the Greeks begin to worship Ajax; Since things in motion sooner catch the eye Than what not stirs. The cry went once on thee, And still it might, and yet it may again, If thou wouldst not entomb thyself alive And case thy reputation in thy tent; Whose glorious deeds, but in these fields of late, Made emulous missions 'mongst the gods themselves And drave great Mars to faction.
lines: Time hath, my lord, a wallet at his back,
Wherein he puts alms for oblivion,
A great-sized monster of ingratitudes:
Those scraps are good deeds past; which are devour'd
As fast as they are made, forgot as soon
As done: perseverance, dear my lord,
Keeps honour bright: to have done is to hang
Quite out of fashion, like a rusty mail
In monumental mockery. Take the instant way;
For honour travels in a strait so narrow,
Where one but goes abreast: keep then the path;
For emulation hath a thousand sons
That one by one pursue: if you give way,
Or hedge aside from the direct forthright,
Like to an enter'd tide, they all rush by
And leave you hindmost;
Or like a gallant horse fall'n in first rank,
Lie there for pavement to the abject rear,
O'er-run and trampled on: then what they do in present,
Though less than yours in past, must o'ertop yours;
For time is like a fashionable host
That slightly shakes his parting guest by the hand,
And with his arms outstretch'd, as he would fly,
Grasps in the comer: welcome ever smiles,
And farewell goes out sighing. O, let not
virtue seek
Remuneration for the thing it was;
For beauty, wit,
High birth, vigour of bone, desert in service,
Love, friendship, charity, are subjects all
To envious and calumniating time.
One touch of nature makes the whole world kin,
That all with one consent praise new-born gawds,
Though they are made and moulded of things past,
And give to dust that is a little gilt
More laud than gilt o'er-dusted.
The present eye praises the present object.
Then marvel not, thou great and complete man,
That all the Greeks begin to worship Ajax;
Since things in motion sooner catch the eye
Than what not stirs. The cry went once on thee,
And still it might, and yet it may again,
If thou wouldst not entomb thyself alive
And case thy reputation in thy tent;
Whose glorious deeds, but in these fields of late,
Made emulous missions 'mongst the gods themselves
And drave great Mars to faction.

speeches.speech: Of this my privacy I have strong reasons.
lines: Of this my privacy
I have strong reasons.

speeches.speech: But 'gainst your privacy The reasons are more potent and heroical: 'Tis known, Achilles, that you are in love With one of Priam's daughters.
lines: But 'gainst your privacy
The reasons are more potent and heroical:
'Tis known, Achilles, that you are in love
With one of Priam's daughters.

speeches.speech: Ha! known!
lines: Ha! known!

speeches.speech: Is that a wonder? The providence that's in a watchful state Knows almost every grain of Plutus' gold, Finds bottom in the uncomprehensive deeps, Keeps place with thought and almost, like the gods, Does thoughts unveil in their dumb cradles. There is a mystery--with whom relation Durst never meddle--in the soul of state; Which hath an operation more divine Than breath or pen can give expressure to: All the commerce that you have had with Troy As perfectly is ours as yours, my lord; And better would it fit Achilles much To throw down Hector than Polyxena: But it must grieve young Pyrrhus now at home, When fame shall in our islands sound her trump, And all the Greekish girls shall tripping sing, 'Great Hector's sister did Achilles win, But our great Ajax bravely beat down him.' Farewell, my lord: I as your lover speak; The fool slides o'er the ice that you should break.
lines: Is that a wonder?
The providence that's in a watchful state
Knows almost every grain of Plutus' gold,
Finds bottom in the uncomprehensive deeps,
Keeps place with thought and almost, like the gods,
Does thoughts unveil in their dumb cradles.
There is a mystery--with whom relation
Durst never meddle--in the soul of state;
Which hath an operation more divine
Than breath or pen can give expressure to:
All the commerce that you have had with Troy
As perfectly is ours as yours, my lord;
And better would it fit Achilles much
To throw down Hector than Polyxena:
But it must grieve young Pyrrhus now at home,
When fame shall in our islands sound her trump,
And all the Greekish girls shall tripping sing,
'Great Hector's sister did Achilles win,
But our great Ajax bravely beat down him.'
Farewell, my lord: I as your lover speak;
The fool slides o'er the ice that you should break.

speeches.speech: To this effect, Achilles, have I moved you: A woman impudent and mannish grown Is not more loathed than an effeminate man In time of action. I stand condemn'd for this; They think my little stomach to the war And your great love to me restrains you thus: Sweet, rouse yourself; and the weak wanton Cupid Shall from your neck unloose his amorous fold, And, like a dew-drop from the lion's mane, Be shook to air.
lines: To this effect, Achilles, have I moved you:
A woman impudent and mannish grown
Is not more loathed than an effeminate man
In time of action. I stand condemn'd for this;
They think my little stomach to the war
And your great love to me restrains you thus:
Sweet, rouse yourself; and the weak wanton Cupid
Shall from your neck unloose his amorous fold,
And, like a dew-drop from the lion's mane,
Be shook to air.

speeches.speech: Shall Ajax fight with Hector?
lines: Shall Ajax fight with Hector?

speeches.speech: Ay, and perhaps receive much honour by him.
lines: Ay, and perhaps receive much honour by him.

speeches.speech: I see my reputation is at stake My fame is shrewdly gored.
lines: I see my reputation is at stake
My fame is shrewdly gored.

speeches.speech: O, then, beware; Those wounds heal ill that men do give themselves: Omission to do what is necessary Seals a commission to a blank of danger; And danger, like an ague, subtly taints Even then when we sit idly in the sun.
lines: O, then, beware;
Those wounds heal ill that men do give themselves:
Omission to do what is necessary
Seals a commission to a blank of danger;
And danger, like an ague, subtly taints
Even then when we sit idly in the sun.

speeches.speech: Go call Thersites hither, sweet Patroclus: I'll send the fool to Ajax and desire him To invite the Trojan lords after the combat To see us here unarm'd: I have a woman's longing, An appetite that I am sick withal, To see great Hector in his weeds of peace, To talk with him and to behold his visage, Even to my full of view. [Enter THERSITES] A labour saved!
lines: Go call Thersites hither, sweet Patroclus:
I'll send the fool to Ajax and desire him
To invite the Trojan lords after the combat
To see us here unarm'd: I have a woman's longing,
An appetite that I am sick withal,
To see great Hector in his weeds of peace,
To talk with him and to behold his visage,
Even to my full of view.
[Enter THERSITES]
A labour saved!

speeches.speech: A wonder!
lines: A wonder!

speeches.speech: What?
lines: What?

speeches.speech: Ajax goes up and down the field, asking for himself.
lines: Ajax goes up and down the field, asking for himself.

speeches.speech: How so?
lines: How so?

speeches.speech: He must fight singly to-morrow with Hector, and is so prophetically proud of an heroical cudgelling that he raves in saying nothing.
lines: He must fight singly to-morrow with Hector, and is so
prophetically proud of an heroical cudgelling that he
raves in saying nothing.

speeches.speech: How can that be?
lines: How can that be?

speeches.speech: Why, he stalks up and down like a peacock,--a stride and a stand: ruminates like an hostess that hath no arithmetic but her brain to set down her reckoning: bites his lip with a politic regard, as who should say 'There were wit in this head, an 'twould out;' and so there is, but it lies as coldly in him as fire in a flint, which will not show without knocking. The man's undone forever; for if Hector break not his neck i' the combat, he'll break 't himself in vain-glory. He knows not me: I said 'Good morrow, Ajax;' and he replies 'Thanks, Agamemnon.' What think you of this man that takes me for the general? He's grown a very land-fish, language-less, a monster. A plague of opinion! a man may wear it on both sides, like a leather jerkin.
lines: Why, he stalks up and down like a peacock,--a stride
and a stand: ruminates like an hostess that hath no
arithmetic but her brain to set down her reckoning:
bites his lip with a politic regard, as who should
say 'There were wit in this head, an 'twould out;'
and so there is, but it lies as coldly in him as fire
in a flint, which will not show without knocking.
The man's undone forever; for if Hector break not his
neck i' the combat, he'll break 't himself in
vain-glory. He knows not me: I said 'Good morrow,
Ajax;' and he replies 'Thanks, Agamemnon.' What think
you of this man that takes me for the general? He's
grown a very land-fish, language-less, a monster.
A plague of opinion! a man may wear it on both
sides, like a leather jerkin.

speeches.speech: Thou must be my ambassador to him, Thersites.
lines: Thou must be my ambassador to him, Thersites.

speeches.speech: Who, I? why, he'll answer nobody; he professes not answering: speaking is for beggars; he wears his tongue in's arms. I will put on his presence: let Patroclus make demands to me, you shall see the pageant of Ajax.
lines: Who, I? why, he'll answer nobody; he professes not
answering: speaking is for beggars; he wears his
tongue in's arms. I will put on his presence: let
Patroclus make demands to me, you shall see the
pageant of Ajax.

speeches.speech: To him, Patroclus; tell him I humbly desire the valiant Ajax to invite the most valorous Hector to come unarmed to my tent, and to procure safe-conduct for his person of the magnanimous and most illustrious six-or-seven-times-honoured captain-general of the Grecian army, Agamemnon, et cetera. Do this.
lines: To him, Patroclus; tell him I humbly desire the
valiant Ajax to invite the most valorous Hector
to come unarmed to my tent, and to procure
safe-conduct for his person of the magnanimous
and most illustrious six-or-seven-times-honoured
captain-general of the Grecian army, Agamemnon,
et cetera. Do this.

speeches.speech: Jove bless great Ajax!
lines: Jove bless great Ajax!

speeches.speech: Hum!
lines: Hum!

speeches.speech: I come from the worthy Achilles,--
lines: I come from the worthy Achilles,--

speeches.speech: Ha!
lines: Ha!

speeches.speech: Who most humbly desires you to invite Hector to his tent,--
lines: Who most humbly desires you to invite Hector to his tent,--

speeches.speech: Hum!
lines: Hum!

speeches.speech: And to procure safe-conduct from Agamemnon.
lines: And to procure safe-conduct from Agamemnon.

speeches.speech: Agamemnon!
lines: Agamemnon!

speeches.speech: Ay, my lord.
lines: Ay, my lord.

speeches.speech: Ha!
lines: Ha!

speeches.speech: What say you to't?
lines: What say you to't?

speeches.speech: God b' wi' you, with all my heart.
lines: God b' wi' you, with all my heart.

speeches.speech: Your answer, sir.
lines: Your answer, sir.

speeches.speech: If to-morrow be a fair day, by eleven o'clock it will go one way or other: howsoever, he shall pay for me ere he has me.
lines: If to-morrow be a fair day, by eleven o'clock it will
go one way or other: howsoever, he shall pay for me
ere he has me.

speeches.speech: Your answer, sir.
lines: Your answer, sir.

speeches.speech: Fare you well, with all my heart.
lines: Fare you well, with all my heart.

speeches.speech: Why, but he is not in this tune, is he?
lines: Why, but he is not in this tune, is he?

speeches.speech: No, but he's out o' tune thus. What music will be in him when Hector has knocked out his brains, I know not; but, I am sure, none, unless the fiddler Apollo get his sinews to make catlings on.
lines: No, but he's out o' tune thus. What music will be in
him when Hector has knocked out his brains, I know
not; but, I am sure, none, unless the fiddler Apollo
get his sinews to make catlings on.

speeches.speech: Come, thou shalt bear a letter to him straight.
lines: Come, thou shalt bear a letter to him straight.

speeches.speech: Let me bear another to his horse; for that's the more capable creature.
lines: Let me bear another to his horse; for that's the more
capable creature.

speeches.speech: My mind is troubled, like a fountain stirr'd; And I myself see not the bottom of it.
lines: My mind is troubled, like a fountain stirr'd;
And I myself see not the bottom of it.

speeches.speech: Would the fountain of your mind were clear again, that I might water an ass at it! I had rather be a tick in a sheep than such a valiant ignorance.
lines: Would the fountain of your mind were clear again,
that I might water an ass at it! I had rather be a
tick in a sheep than such a valiant ignorance.

speeches.speech: See, ho! who is that there?
lines: See, ho! who is that there?

speeches.speech: It is the Lord AEneas.
lines: It is the Lord AEneas.

speeches.speech: Is the prince there in person? Had I so good occasion to lie long As you, prince Paris, nothing but heavenly business Should rob my bed-mate of my company.
lines: Is the prince there in person?
Had I so good occasion to lie long
As you, prince Paris, nothing but heavenly business
Should rob my bed-mate of my company.

speeches.speech: That's my mind too. Good morrow, Lord AEneas.
lines: That's my mind too. Good morrow, Lord AEneas.

speeches.speech: A valiant Greek, AEneas,--take his hand,-- Witness the process of your speech, wherein You told how Diomed, a whole week by days, Did haunt you in the field.
lines: A valiant Greek, AEneas,--take his hand,--
Witness the process of your speech, wherein
You told how Diomed, a whole week by days,
Did haunt you in the field.

speeches.speech: Health to you, valiant sir, During all question of the gentle truce; But when I meet you arm'd, as black defiance As heart can think or courage execute.
lines: Health to you, valiant sir,
During all question of the gentle truce;
But when I meet you arm'd, as black defiance
As heart can think or courage execute.

speeches.speech: The one and other Diomed embraces. Our bloods are now in calm; and, so long, health! But when contention and occasion meet, By Jove, I'll play the hunter for thy life With all my force, pursuit and policy.
lines: The one and other Diomed embraces.
Our bloods are now in calm; and, so long, health!
But when contention and occasion meet,
By Jove, I'll play the hunter for thy life
With all my force, pursuit and policy.

speeches.speech: And thou shalt hunt a lion, that will fly With his face backward. In humane gentleness, Welcome to Troy! now, by Anchises' life, Welcome, indeed! By Venus' hand I swear, No man alive can love in such a sort The thing he means to kill more excellently.
lines: And thou shalt hunt a lion, that will fly
With his face backward. In humane gentleness,
Welcome to Troy! now, by Anchises' life,
Welcome, indeed! By Venus' hand I swear,
No man alive can love in such a sort
The thing he means to kill more excellently.

speeches.speech: We sympathize: Jove, let AEneas live, If to my sword his fate be not the glory, A thousand complete courses of the sun! But, in mine emulous honour, let him die, With every joint a wound, and that to-morrow!
lines: We sympathize: Jove, let AEneas live,
If to my sword his fate be not the glory,
A thousand complete courses of the sun!
But, in mine emulous honour, let him die,
With every joint a wound, and that to-morrow!

speeches.speech: We know each other well.
lines: We know each other well.

speeches.speech: We do; and long to know each other worse.
lines: We do; and long to know each other worse.

speeches.speech: This is the most despiteful gentle greeting, The noblest hateful love, that e'er I heard of. What business, lord, so early?
lines: This is the most despiteful gentle greeting,
The noblest hateful love, that e'er I heard of.
What business, lord, so early?

speeches.speech: I was sent for to the king; but why, I know not.
lines: I was sent for to the king; but why, I know not.

speeches.speech: His purpose meets you: 'twas to bring this Greek To Calchas' house, and there to render him, For the enfreed Antenor, the fair Cressid: Let's have your company, or, if you please, Haste there before us: I constantly do think-- Or rather, call my thought a certain knowledge-- My brother Troilus lodges there to-night: Rouse him and give him note of our approach. With the whole quality wherefore: I fear We shall be much unwelcome.
lines: His purpose meets you: 'twas to bring this Greek
To Calchas' house, and there to render him,
For the enfreed Antenor, the fair Cressid:
Let's have your company, or, if you please,
Haste there before us: I constantly do think--
Or rather, call my thought a certain knowledge--
My brother Troilus lodges there to-night:
Rouse him and give him note of our approach.
With the whole quality wherefore: I fear
We shall be much unwelcome.

speeches.speech: That I assure you: Troilus had rather Troy were borne to Greece Than Cressid borne from Troy.
lines: That I assure you:
Troilus had rather Troy were borne to Greece
Than Cressid borne from Troy.

speeches.speech: There is no help; The bitter disposition of the time Will have it so. On, lord; we'll follow you.
lines: There is no help;
The bitter disposition of the time
Will have it so. On, lord; we'll follow you.

speeches.speech: Good morrow, all.
lines: Good morrow, all.

speeches.speech: And tell me, noble Diomed, faith, tell me true, Even in the soul of sound good-fellowship, Who, in your thoughts, merits fair Helen best, Myself or Menelaus?
lines: And tell me, noble Diomed, faith, tell me true,
Even in the soul of sound good-fellowship,
Who, in your thoughts, merits fair Helen best,
Myself or Menelaus?

speeches.speech: Both alike: He merits well to have her, that doth seek her, Not making any scruple of her soilure, With such a hell of pain and world of charge, And you as well to keep her, that defend her, Not palating the taste of her dishonour, With such a costly loss of wealth and friends: He, like a puling cuckold, would drink up The lees and dregs of a flat tamed piece; You, like a lecher, out of whorish loins Are pleased to breed out your inheritors: Both merits poised, each weighs nor less nor more; But he as he, the heavier for a whore.
lines: Both alike:
He merits well to have her, that doth seek her,
Not making any scruple of her soilure,
With such a hell of pain and world of charge,
And you as well to keep her, that defend her,
Not palating the taste of her dishonour,
With such a costly loss of wealth and friends:
He, like a puling cuckold, would drink up
The lees and dregs of a flat tamed piece;
You, like a lecher, out of whorish loins
Are pleased to breed out your inheritors:
Both merits poised, each weighs nor less nor more;
But he as he, the heavier for a whore.

speeches.speech: You are too bitter to your countrywoman.
lines: You are too bitter to your countrywoman.

speeches.speech: She's bitter to her country: hear me, Paris: For every false drop in her bawdy veins A Grecian's life hath sunk; for every scruple Of her contaminated carrion weight, A Trojan hath been slain: since she could speak, She hath not given so many good words breath As for her Greeks and Trojans suffer'd death.
lines: She's bitter to her country: hear me, Paris:
For every false drop in her bawdy veins
A Grecian's life hath sunk; for every scruple
Of her contaminated carrion weight,
A Trojan hath been slain: since she could speak,
She hath not given so many good words breath
As for her Greeks and Trojans suffer'd death.

speeches.speech: Fair Diomed, you do as chapmen do, Dispraise the thing that you desire to buy: But we in silence hold this virtue well, We'll but commend what we intend to sell. Here lies our way.
lines: Fair Diomed, you do as chapmen do,
Dispraise the thing that you desire to buy:
But we in silence hold this virtue well,
We'll but commend what we intend to sell.
Here lies our way.

speeches.speech: Dear, trouble not yourself: the morn is cold.
lines: Dear, trouble not yourself: the morn is cold.

speeches.speech: Then, sweet my lord, I'll call mine uncle down; He shall unbolt the gates.
lines: Then, sweet my lord, I'll call mine uncle down;
He shall unbolt the gates.

speeches.speech: Trouble him not; To bed, to bed: sleep kill those pretty eyes, And give as soft attachment to thy senses As infants' empty of all thought!
lines: Trouble him not;
To bed, to bed: sleep kill those pretty eyes,
And give as soft attachment to thy senses
As infants' empty of all thought!

speeches.speech: Good morrow, then.
lines: Good morrow, then.

speeches.speech: I prithee now, to bed.
lines: I prithee now, to bed.

speeches.speech: Are you a-weary of me?
lines: Are you a-weary of me?

speeches.speech: O Cressida! but that the busy day, Waked by the lark, hath roused the ribald crows, And dreaming night will hide our joys no longer, I would not from thee.
lines: O Cressida! but that the busy day,
Waked by the lark, hath roused the ribald crows,
And dreaming night will hide our joys no longer,
I would not from thee.

speeches.speech: Night hath been too brief.
lines: Night hath been too brief.

speeches.speech: Beshrew the witch! with venomous wights she stays As tediously as hell, but flies the grasps of love With wings more momentary-swift than thought. You will catch cold, and curse me.
lines: Beshrew the witch! with venomous wights she stays
As tediously as hell, but flies the grasps of love
With wings more momentary-swift than thought.
You will catch cold, and curse me.

speeches.speech: Prithee, tarry: You men will never tarry. O foolish Cressid! I might have still held off, And then you would have tarried. Hark! there's one up.
lines: Prithee, tarry:
You men will never tarry.
O foolish Cressid! I might have still held off,
And then you would have tarried. Hark!
there's one up.

speeches.speech: [Within] What, 's all the doors open here?
lines: [Within] What, 's all the doors open here?

speeches.speech: It is your uncle.
lines: It is your uncle.

speeches.speech: A pestilence on him! now will he be mocking: I shall have such a life!
lines: A pestilence on him! now will he be mocking:
I shall have such a life!

speeches.speech: How now, how now! how go maidenheads? Here, you maid! where's my cousin Cressid?
lines: How now, how now! how go maidenheads? Here, you
maid! where's my cousin Cressid?

speeches.speech: Go hang yourself, you naughty mocking uncle! You bring me to do, and then you flout me too.
lines: Go hang yourself, you naughty mocking uncle!
You bring me to do, and then you flout me too.

speeches.speech: To do what? to do what? let her say what: what have I brought you to do?
lines: To do what? to do what? let her say
what: what have I brought you to do?

speeches.speech: Come, come, beshrew your heart! you'll ne'er be good, Nor suffer others.
lines: Come, come, beshrew your heart! you'll ne'er be good,
Nor suffer others.

speeches.speech: Ha! ha! Alas, poor wretch! ah, poor capocchia! hast not slept to-night? would he not, a naughty man, let it sleep? a bugbear take him!
lines: Ha! ha! Alas, poor wretch! ah, poor capocchia!
hast not slept to-night? would he not, a naughty
man, let it sleep? a bugbear take him!

speeches.speech: Did not I tell you? Would he were knock'd i' the head! [Knocking within] Who's that at door? good uncle, go and see. My lord, come you again into my chamber: You smile and mock me, as if I meant naughtily.
lines: Did not I tell you? Would he were knock'd i' the head!
[Knocking within]
Who's that at door? good uncle, go and see.
My lord, come you again into my chamber:
You smile and mock me, as if I meant naughtily.

speeches.speech: Ha, ha!
lines: Ha, ha!

speeches.speech: Come, you are deceived, I think of no such thing. [Knocking within] How earnestly they knock! Pray you, come in: I would not for half Troy have you seen here.
lines: Come, you are deceived, I think of no such thing.
[Knocking within]
How earnestly they knock! Pray you, come in:
I would not for half Troy have you seen here.

speeches.speech: Who's there? what's the matter? will you beat down the door? How now! what's the matter?
lines: Who's there? what's the matter? will you beat
down the door? How now! what's the matter?

speeches.speech: Good morrow, lord, good morrow.
lines: Good morrow, lord, good morrow.

speeches.speech: Who's there? my Lord AEneas! By my troth, I knew you not: what news with you so early?
lines: Who's there? my Lord AEneas! By my troth,
I knew you not: what news with you so early?

speeches.speech: Is not Prince Troilus here?
lines: Is not Prince Troilus here?

speeches.speech: Here! what should he do here?
lines: Here! what should he do here?

speeches.speech: Come, he is here, my lord; do not deny him: It doth import him much to speak with me.
lines: Come, he is here, my lord; do not deny him:
It doth import him much to speak with me.

speeches.speech: Is he here, say you? 'tis more than I know, I'll be sworn: for my own part, I came in late. What should he do here?
lines: Is he here, say you? 'tis more than I know, I'll
be sworn: for my own part, I came in late. What
should he do here?

speeches.speech: Who!--nay, then: come, come, you'll do him wrong ere you're ware: you'll be so true to him, to be false to him: do not you know of him, but yet go fetch him hither; go.
lines: Who!--nay, then: come, come, you'll do him wrong
ere you're ware: you'll be so true to him, to be
false to him: do not you know of him, but yet go
fetch him hither; go.

speeches.speech: How now! what's the matter?
lines: How now! what's the matter?

speeches.speech: My lord, I scarce have leisure to salute you, My matter is so rash: there is at hand Paris your brother, and Deiphobus, The Grecian Diomed, and our Antenor Deliver'd to us; and for him forthwith, Ere the first sacrifice, within this hour, We must give up to Diomedes' hand The Lady Cressida.
lines: My lord, I scarce have leisure to salute you,
My matter is so rash: there is at hand
Paris your brother, and Deiphobus,
The Grecian Diomed, and our Antenor
Deliver'd to us; and for him forthwith,
Ere the first sacrifice, within this hour,
We must give up to Diomedes' hand
The Lady Cressida.

speeches.speech: Is it so concluded?
lines: Is it so concluded?

speeches.speech: By Priam and the general state of Troy: They are at hand and ready to effect it.
lines: By Priam and the general state of Troy:
They are at hand and ready to effect it.

speeches.speech: How my achievements mock me! I will go meet them: and, my Lord AEneas, We met by chance; you did not find me here.
lines: How my achievements mock me!
I will go meet them: and, my Lord AEneas,
We met by chance; you did not find me here.

speeches.speech: Good, good, my lord; the secrets of nature Have not more gift in taciturnity.
lines: Good, good, my lord; the secrets of nature
Have not more gift in taciturnity.

speeches.speech: Is't possible? no sooner got but lost? The devil take Antenor! the young prince will go mad: a plague upon Antenor! I would they had broke 's neck!
lines: Is't possible? no sooner got but lost? The devil
take Antenor! the young prince will go mad: a
plague upon Antenor! I would they had broke 's neck!

speeches.speech: How now! what's the matter? who was here?
lines: How now! what's the matter? who was here?

speeches.speech: Ah, ah!
lines: Ah, ah!

speeches.speech: Why sigh you so profoundly? where's my lord? gone! Tell me, sweet uncle, what's the matter?
lines: Why sigh you so profoundly? where's my lord? gone!
Tell me, sweet uncle, what's the matter?

speeches.speech: Would I were as deep under the earth as I am above!
lines: Would I were as deep under the earth as I am above!

speeches.speech: O the gods! what's the matter?
lines: O the gods! what's the matter?

speeches.speech: Prithee, get thee in: would thou hadst ne'er been born! I knew thou wouldst be his death. O, poor gentleman! A plague upon Antenor!
lines: Prithee, get thee in: would thou hadst ne'er been
born! I knew thou wouldst be his death. O, poor
gentleman! A plague upon Antenor!

speeches.speech: Good uncle, I beseech you, on my knees! beseech you, what's the matter?
lines: Good uncle, I beseech you, on my knees! beseech you,
what's the matter?

speeches.speech: Thou must be gone, wench, thou must be gone; thou art changed for Antenor: thou must to thy father, and be gone from Troilus: 'twill be his death; 'twill be his bane; he cannot bear it.
lines: Thou must be gone, wench, thou must be gone; thou
art changed for Antenor: thou must to thy father,
and be gone from Troilus: 'twill be his death;
'twill be his bane; he cannot bear it.

speeches.speech: O you immortal gods! I will not go.
lines: O you immortal gods! I will not go.

speeches.speech: Thou must.
lines: Thou must.

speeches.speech: I will not, uncle: I have forgot my father; I know no touch of consanguinity; No kin no love, no blood, no soul so near me As the sweet Troilus. O you gods divine! Make Cressid's name the very crown of falsehood, If ever she leave Troilus! Time, force, and death, Do to this body what extremes you can; But the strong base and building of my love Is as the very centre of the earth, Drawing all things to it. I'll go in and weep,--
lines: I will not, uncle: I have forgot my father;
I know no touch of consanguinity;
No kin no love, no blood, no soul so near me
As the sweet Troilus. O you gods divine!
Make Cressid's name the very crown of falsehood,
If ever she leave Troilus! Time, force, and death,
Do to this body what extremes you can;
But the strong base and building of my love
Is as the very centre of the earth,
Drawing all things to it. I'll go in and weep,--

speeches.speech: Do, do.
lines: Do, do.

speeches.speech: Tear my bright hair and scratch my praised cheeks, Crack my clear voice with sobs and break my heart With sounding Troilus. I will not go from Troy.
lines: Tear my bright hair and scratch my praised cheeks,
Crack my clear voice with sobs and break my heart
With sounding Troilus. I will not go from Troy.

speeches.speech: It is great morning, and the hour prefix'd Of her delivery to this valiant Greek Comes fast upon. Good my brother Troilus, Tell you the lady what she is to do, And haste her to the purpose.
lines: It is great morning, and the hour prefix'd
Of her delivery to this valiant Greek
Comes fast upon. Good my brother Troilus,
Tell you the lady what she is to do,
And haste her to the purpose.

speeches.speech: Walk into her house; I'll bring her to the Grecian presently: And to his hand when I deliver her, Think it an altar, and thy brother Troilus A priest there offering to it his own heart.
lines: Walk into her house;
I'll bring her to the Grecian presently:
And to his hand when I deliver her,
Think it an altar, and thy brother Troilus
A priest there offering to it his own heart.

speeches.speech: I know what 'tis to love; And would, as I shall pity, I could help! Please you walk in, my lords.
lines: I know what 'tis to love;
And would, as I shall pity, I could help!
Please you walk in, my lords.

speeches.speech: Be moderate, be moderate.
lines: Be moderate, be moderate.

speeches.speech: Why tell you me of moderation? The grief is fine, full, perfect, that I taste, And violenteth in a sense as strong As that which causeth it: how can I moderate it? If I could temporize with my affection, Or brew it to a weak and colder palate, The like allayment could I give my grief. My love admits no qualifying dross; No more my grief, in such a precious loss.
lines: Why tell you me of moderation?
The grief is fine, full, perfect, that I taste,
And violenteth in a sense as strong
As that which causeth it: how can I moderate it?
If I could temporize with my affection,
Or brew it to a weak and colder palate,
The like allayment could I give my grief.
My love admits no qualifying dross;
No more my grief, in such a precious loss.

speeches.speech: Here, here, here he comes. [Enter TROILUS] Ah, sweet ducks!
lines: Here, here, here he comes.
[Enter TROILUS]
Ah, sweet ducks!

speeches.speech: O Troilus! Troilus!
lines: O Troilus! Troilus!

speeches.speech: What a pair of spectacles is here! Let me embrace too. 'O heart,' as the goodly saying is, '--O heart, heavy heart, Why sigh'st thou without breaking? where he answers again, 'Because thou canst not ease thy smart By friendship nor by speaking.' There was never a truer rhyme. Let us cast away nothing, for we may live to have need of such a verse: we see it, we see it. How now, lambs?
lines: What a pair of spectacles is here!
Let me embrace too. 'O heart,' as the goodly saying is,
'--O heart, heavy heart,
Why sigh'st thou without breaking?
where he answers again,
'Because thou canst not ease thy smart
By friendship nor by speaking.'
There was never a truer rhyme. Let us cast away
nothing, for we may live to have need of such a
verse: we see it, we see it. How now, lambs?

speeches.speech: Cressid, I love thee in so strain'd a purity, That the bless'd gods, as angry with my fancy, More bright in zeal than the devotion which Cold lips blow to their deities, take thee from me.
lines: Cressid, I love thee in so strain'd a purity,
That the bless'd gods, as angry with my fancy,
More bright in zeal than the devotion which
Cold lips blow to their deities, take thee from me.

speeches.speech: Have the gods envy?
lines: Have the gods envy?

speeches.speech: Ay, ay, ay, ay; 'tis too plain a case.
lines: Ay, ay, ay, ay; 'tis too plain a case.

speeches.speech: And is it true that I must go from Troy?
lines: And is it true that I must go from Troy?

speeches.speech: A hateful truth.
lines: A hateful truth.

speeches.speech: What, and from Troilus too?
lines: What, and from Troilus too?

speeches.speech: From Troy and Troilus.
lines: From Troy and Troilus.

speeches.speech: Is it possible?
lines: Is it possible?

speeches.speech: And suddenly; where injury of chance Puts back leave-taking, justles roughly by All time of pause, rudely beguiles our lips Of all rejoindure, forcibly prevents Our lock'd embrasures, strangles our dear vows Even in the birth of our own labouring breath: We two, that with so many thousand sighs Did buy each other, must poorly sell ourselves With the rude brevity and discharge of one. Injurious time now with a robber's haste Crams his rich thievery up, he knows not how: As many farewells as be stars in heaven, With distinct breath and consign'd kisses to them, He fumbles up into a lose adieu, And scants us with a single famish'd kiss, Distasted with the salt of broken tears.
lines: And suddenly; where injury of chance
Puts back leave-taking, justles roughly by
All time of pause, rudely beguiles our lips
Of all rejoindure, forcibly prevents
Our lock'd embrasures, strangles our dear vows
Even in the birth of our own labouring breath:
We two, that with so many thousand sighs
Did buy each other, must poorly sell ourselves
With the rude brevity and discharge of one.
Injurious time now with a robber's haste
Crams his rich thievery up, he knows not how:
As many farewells as be stars in heaven,
With distinct breath and consign'd kisses to them,
He fumbles up into a lose adieu,
And scants us with a single famish'd kiss,
Distasted with the salt of broken tears.

speeches.speech: [Within] My lord, is the lady ready?
lines: [Within] My lord, is the lady ready?

speeches.speech: Hark! you are call'd: some say the Genius so Cries 'come' to him that instantly must die. Bid them have patience; she shall come anon.
lines: Hark! you are call'd: some say the Genius so
Cries 'come' to him that instantly must die.
Bid them have patience; she shall come anon.

speeches.speech: Where are my tears? rain, to lay this wind, or my heart will be blown up by the root.
lines: Where are my tears? rain, to lay this wind, or
my heart will be blown up by the root.

speeches.speech: I must then to the Grecians?
lines: I must then to the Grecians?

speeches.speech: No remedy.
lines: No remedy.

speeches.speech: A woful Cressid 'mongst the merry Greeks! When shall we see again?
lines: A woful Cressid 'mongst the merry Greeks!
When shall we see again?

speeches.speech: Hear me, my love: be thou but true of heart,--
lines: Hear me, my love: be thou but true of heart,--

speeches.speech: I true! how now! what wicked deem is this?
lines: I true! how now! what wicked deem is this?

speeches.speech: Nay, we must use expostulation kindly, For it is parting from us: I speak not 'be thou true,' as fearing thee, For I will throw my glove to Death himself, That there's no maculation in thy heart: But 'be thou true,' say I, to fashion in My sequent protestation; be thou true, And I will see thee.
lines: Nay, we must use expostulation kindly,
For it is parting from us:
I speak not 'be thou true,' as fearing thee,
For I will throw my glove to Death himself,
That there's no maculation in thy heart:
But 'be thou true,' say I, to fashion in
My sequent protestation; be thou true,
And I will see thee.

speeches.speech: O, you shall be exposed, my lord, to dangers As infinite as imminent! but I'll be true.
lines: O, you shall be exposed, my lord, to dangers
As infinite as imminent! but I'll be true.

speeches.speech: And I'll grow friend with danger. Wear this sleeve.
lines: And I'll grow friend with danger. Wear this sleeve.

speeches.speech: And you this glove. When shall I see you?
lines: And you this glove. When shall I see you?

speeches.speech: I will corrupt the Grecian sentinels, To give thee nightly visitation. But yet be true.
lines: I will corrupt the Grecian sentinels,
To give thee nightly visitation.
But yet be true.

speeches.speech: O heavens! 'be true' again!
lines: O heavens! 'be true' again!

speeches.speech: Hear while I speak it, love: The Grecian youths are full of quality; They're loving, well composed with gifts of nature, Flowing and swelling o'er with arts and exercise: How novelty may move, and parts with person, Alas, a kind of godly jealousy-- Which, I beseech you, call a virtuous sin-- Makes me afeard.
lines: Hear while I speak it, love:
The Grecian youths are full of quality;
They're loving, well composed with gifts of nature,
Flowing and swelling o'er with arts and exercise:
How novelty may move, and parts with person,
Alas, a kind of godly jealousy--
Which, I beseech you, call a virtuous sin--
Makes me afeard.

speeches.speech: O heavens! you love me not.
lines: O heavens! you love me not.

speeches.speech: Die I a villain, then! In this I do not call your faith in question So mainly as my merit: I cannot sing, Nor heel the high lavolt, nor sweeten talk, Nor play at subtle games; fair virtues all, To which the Grecians are most prompt and pregnant: But I can tell that in each grace of these There lurks a still and dumb-discoursive devil That tempts most cunningly: but be not tempted.
lines: Die I a villain, then!
In this I do not call your faith in question
So mainly as my merit: I cannot sing,
Nor heel the high lavolt, nor sweeten talk,
Nor play at subtle games; fair virtues all,
To which the Grecians are most prompt and pregnant:
But I can tell that in each grace of these
There lurks a still and dumb-discoursive devil
That tempts most cunningly: but be not tempted.

speeches.speech: Do you think I will?
lines: Do you think I will?

speeches.speech: No. But something may be done that we will not: And sometimes we are devils to ourselves, When we will tempt the frailty of our powers, Presuming on their changeful potency.
lines: No.
But something may be done that we will not:
And sometimes we are devils to ourselves,
When we will tempt the frailty of our powers,
Presuming on their changeful potency.

speeches.speech: [Within] Nay, good my lord,--
lines: [Within] Nay, good my lord,--

speeches.speech: Come, kiss; and let us part.
lines: Come, kiss; and let us part.

speeches.speech: [Within] Brother Troilus!
lines: [Within] Brother Troilus!

speeches.speech: Good brother, come you hither; And bring AEneas and the Grecian with you.
lines: Good brother, come you hither;
And bring AEneas and the Grecian with you.

speeches.speech: My lord, will you be true?
lines: My lord, will you be true?

speeches.speech: Who, I? alas, it is my vice, my fault: Whiles others fish with craft for great opinion, I with great truth catch mere simplicity; Whilst some with cunning gild their copper crowns, With truth and plainness I do wear mine bare. Fear not my truth: the moral of my wit Is 'plain and true;' there's all the reach of it. [Enter AENEAS, PARIS, ANTENOR, DEIPHOBUS,] and DIOMEDES] Welcome, Sir Diomed! here is the lady Which for Antenor we deliver you: At the port, lord, I'll give her to thy hand, And by the way possess thee what she is. Entreat her fair; and, by my soul, fair Greek, If e'er thou stand at mercy of my sword, Name Cressida and thy life shall be as safe As Priam is in Ilion.
lines: Who, I? alas, it is my vice, my fault:
Whiles others fish with craft for great opinion,
I with great truth catch mere simplicity;
Whilst some with cunning gild their copper crowns,
With truth and plainness I do wear mine bare.
Fear not my truth: the moral of my wit
Is 'plain and true;' there's all the reach of it.
[Enter AENEAS, PARIS, ANTENOR, DEIPHOBUS,]
and DIOMEDES]
Welcome, Sir Diomed! here is the lady
Which for Antenor we deliver you:
At the port, lord, I'll give her to thy hand,
And by the way possess thee what she is.
Entreat her fair; and, by my soul, fair Greek,
If e'er thou stand at mercy of my sword,
Name Cressida and thy life shall be as safe
As Priam is in Ilion.

speeches.speech: Fair Lady Cressid, So please you, save the thanks this prince expects: The lustre in your eye, heaven in your cheek, Pleads your fair usage; and to Diomed You shall be mistress, and command him wholly.
lines: Fair Lady Cressid,
So please you, save the thanks this prince expects:
The lustre in your eye, heaven in your cheek,
Pleads your fair usage; and to Diomed
You shall be mistress, and command him wholly.

speeches.speech: Grecian, thou dost not use me courteously, To shame the zeal of my petition to thee In praising her: I tell thee, lord of Greece, She is as far high-soaring o'er thy praises As thou unworthy to be call'd her servant. I charge thee use her well, even for my charge; For, by the dreadful Pluto, if thou dost not, Though the great bulk Achilles be thy guard, I'll cut thy throat.
lines: Grecian, thou dost not use me courteously,
To shame the zeal of my petition to thee
In praising her: I tell thee, lord of Greece,
She is as far high-soaring o'er thy praises
As thou unworthy to be call'd her servant.
I charge thee use her well, even for my charge;
For, by the dreadful Pluto, if thou dost not,
Though the great bulk Achilles be thy guard,
I'll cut thy throat.

speeches.speech: O, be not moved, Prince Troilus: Let me be privileged by my place and message, To be a speaker free; when I am hence I'll answer to my lust: and know you, lord, I'll nothing do on charge: to her own worth She shall be prized; but that you say 'be't so,' I'll speak it in my spirit and honour, 'no.'
lines: O, be not moved, Prince Troilus:
Let me be privileged by my place and message,
To be a speaker free; when I am hence
I'll answer to my lust: and know you, lord,
I'll nothing do on charge: to her own worth
She shall be prized; but that you say 'be't so,'
I'll speak it in my spirit and honour, 'no.'

speeches.speech: Come, to the port. I'll tell thee, Diomed, This brave shall oft make thee to hide thy head. Lady, give me your hand, and, as we walk, To our own selves bend we our needful talk.
lines: Come, to the port. I'll tell thee, Diomed,
This brave shall oft make thee to hide thy head.
Lady, give me your hand, and, as we walk,
To our own selves bend we our needful talk.

speeches.speech: Hark! Hector's trumpet.
lines: Hark! Hector's trumpet.

speeches.speech: How have we spent this morning! The prince must think me tardy and remiss, That sore to ride before him to the field.
lines: How have we spent this morning!
The prince must think me tardy and remiss,
That sore to ride before him to the field.

speeches.speech: 'Tis Troilus' fault: come, come, to field with him.
lines: 'Tis Troilus' fault: come, come, to field with him.

speeches.speech: Let us make ready straight.
lines: Let us make ready straight.

speeches.speech: Yea, with a bridegroom's fresh alacrity, Let us address to tend on Hector's heels: The glory of our Troy doth this day lie On his fair worth and single chivalry.
lines: Yea, with a bridegroom's fresh alacrity,
Let us address to tend on Hector's heels:
The glory of our Troy doth this day lie
On his fair worth and single chivalry.

speeches.speech: Here art thou in appointment fresh and fair, Anticipating time with starting courage. Give with thy trumpet a loud note to Troy, Thou dreadful Ajax; that the appalled air May pierce the head of the great combatant And hale him hither.
lines: Here art thou in appointment fresh and fair,
Anticipating time with starting courage.
Give with thy trumpet a loud note to Troy,
Thou dreadful Ajax; that the appalled air
May pierce the head of the great combatant
And hale him hither.

speeches.speech: Thou, trumpet, there's my purse. Now crack thy lungs, and split thy brazen pipe: Blow, villain, till thy sphered bias cheek Outswell the colic of puff'd Aquilon: Come, stretch thy chest and let thy eyes spout blood; Thou blow'st for Hector.
lines: Thou, trumpet, there's my purse.
Now crack thy lungs, and split thy brazen pipe:
Blow, villain, till thy sphered bias cheek
Outswell the colic of puff'd Aquilon:
Come, stretch thy chest and let thy eyes spout blood;
Thou blow'st for Hector.

speeches.speech: No trumpet answers.
lines: No trumpet answers.

speeches.speech: 'Tis but early days.
lines: 'Tis but early days.

speeches.speech: Is not yond Diomed, with Calchas' daughter?
lines: Is not yond Diomed, with Calchas' daughter?

speeches.speech: 'Tis he, I ken the manner of his gait; He rises on the toe: that spirit of his In aspiration lifts him from the earth.
lines: 'Tis he, I ken the manner of his gait;
He rises on the toe: that spirit of his
In aspiration lifts him from the earth.

speeches.speech: Is this the Lady Cressid?
lines: Is this the Lady Cressid?

speeches.speech: Even she.
lines: Even she.

speeches.speech: Most dearly welcome to the Greeks, sweet lady.
lines: Most dearly welcome to the Greeks, sweet lady.

speeches.speech: Our general doth salute you with a kiss.
lines: Our general doth salute you with a kiss.

speeches.speech: Yet is the kindness but particular; 'Twere better she were kiss'd in general.
lines: Yet is the kindness but particular;
'Twere better she were kiss'd in general.

speeches.speech: And very courtly counsel: I'll begin. So much for Nestor.
lines: And very courtly counsel: I'll begin.
So much for Nestor.

speeches.speech: I'll take what winter from your lips, fair lady: Achilles bids you welcome.
lines: I'll take what winter from your lips, fair lady:
Achilles bids you welcome.

speeches.speech: I had good argument for kissing once.
lines: I had good argument for kissing once.

speeches.speech: But that's no argument for kissing now; For this popp'd Paris in his hardiment, And parted thus you and your argument.
lines: But that's no argument for kissing now;
For this popp'd Paris in his hardiment,
And parted thus you and your argument.

speeches.speech: O deadly gall, and theme of all our scorns! For which we lose our heads to gild his horns.
lines: O deadly gall, and theme of all our scorns!
For which we lose our heads to gild his horns.

speeches.speech: The first was Menelaus' kiss; this, mine: Patroclus kisses you.
lines: The first was Menelaus' kiss; this, mine:
Patroclus kisses you.

speeches.speech: O, this is trim!
lines: O, this is trim!

speeches.speech: Paris and I kiss evermore for him.
lines: Paris and I kiss evermore for him.

speeches.speech: I'll have my kiss, sir. Lady, by your leave.
lines: I'll have my kiss, sir. Lady, by your leave.

speeches.speech: In kissing, do you render or receive?
lines: In kissing, do you render or receive?

speeches.speech: Both take and give.
lines: Both take and give.

speeches.speech: I'll make my match to live, The kiss you take is better than you give; Therefore no kiss.
lines: I'll make my match to live,
The kiss you take is better than you give;
Therefore no kiss.

speeches.speech: I'll give you boot, I'll give you three for one.
lines: I'll give you boot, I'll give you three for one.

speeches.speech: You're an odd man; give even or give none.
lines: You're an odd man; give even or give none.

speeches.speech: An odd man, lady! every man is odd.
lines: An odd man, lady! every man is odd.

speeches.speech: No, Paris is not; for you know 'tis true, That you are odd, and he is even with you.
lines: No, Paris is not; for you know 'tis true,
That you are odd, and he is even with you.

speeches.speech: You fillip me o' the head.
lines: You fillip me o' the head.

speeches.speech: No, I'll be sworn.
lines: No, I'll be sworn.

speeches.speech: It were no match, your nail against his horn. May I, sweet lady, beg a kiss of you?
lines: It were no match, your nail against his horn.
May I, sweet lady, beg a kiss of you?

speeches.speech: You may.
lines: You may.

speeches.speech: I do desire it.
lines: I do desire it.

speeches.speech: Why, beg, then.
lines: Why, beg, then.

speeches.speech: Why then for Venus' sake, give me a kiss, When Helen is a maid again, and his.
lines: Why then for Venus' sake, give me a kiss,
When Helen is a maid again, and his.

speeches.speech: I am your debtor, claim it when 'tis due.
lines: I am your debtor, claim it when 'tis due.

speeches.speech: Never's my day, and then a kiss of you.
lines: Never's my day, and then a kiss of you.

speeches.speech: Lady, a word: I'll bring you to your father.
lines: Lady, a word: I'll bring you to your father.

speeches.speech: A woman of quick sense.
lines: A woman of quick sense.

speeches.speech: Fie, fie upon her! There's language in her eye, her cheek, her lip, Nay, her foot speaks; her wanton spirits look out At every joint and motive of her body. O, these encounterers, so glib of tongue, That give accosting welcome ere it comes, And wide unclasp the tables of their thoughts To every ticklish reader! set them down For sluttish spoils of opportunity And daughters of the game.
lines: Fie, fie upon her!
There's language in her eye, her cheek, her lip,
Nay, her foot speaks; her wanton spirits look out
At every joint and motive of her body.
O, these encounterers, so glib of tongue,
That give accosting welcome ere it comes,
And wide unclasp the tables of their thoughts
To every ticklish reader! set them down
For sluttish spoils of opportunity
And daughters of the game.

speeches.speech: The Trojans' trumpet.
lines: The Trojans' trumpet.

speeches.speech: Yonder comes the troop. [Enter HECTOR, armed; AENEAS, TROILUS, and other] Trojans, with Attendants]
lines: Yonder comes the troop.
[Enter HECTOR, armed; AENEAS, TROILUS, and other]
Trojans, with Attendants]

speeches.speech: Hail, all you state of Greece! what shall be done To him that victory commands? or do you purpose A victor shall be known? will you the knights Shall to the edge of all extremity Pursue each other, or shall be divided By any voice or order of the field? Hector bade ask.
lines: Hail, all you state of Greece! what shall be done
To him that victory commands? or do you purpose
A victor shall be known? will you the knights
Shall to the edge of all extremity
Pursue each other, or shall be divided
By any voice or order of the field?
Hector bade ask.

speeches.speech: Which way would Hector have it?
lines: Which way would Hector have it?

speeches.speech: He cares not; he'll obey conditions.
lines: He cares not; he'll obey conditions.

speeches.speech: 'Tis done like Hector; but securely done, A little proudly, and great deal misprizing The knight opposed.
lines: 'Tis done like Hector; but securely done,
A little proudly, and great deal misprizing
The knight opposed.

speeches.speech: If not Achilles, sir, What is your name?
lines: If not Achilles, sir,
What is your name?

speeches.speech: If not Achilles, nothing.
lines: If not Achilles, nothing.

speeches.speech: Therefore Achilles: but, whate'er, know this: In the extremity of great and little, Valour and pride excel themselves in Hector; The one almost as infinite as all, The other blank as nothing. Weigh him well, And that which looks like pride is courtesy. This Ajax is half made of Hector's blood: In love whereof, half Hector stays at home; Half heart, half hand, half Hector comes to seek This blended knight, half Trojan and half Greek.
lines: Therefore Achilles: but, whate'er, know this:
In the extremity of great and little,
Valour and pride excel themselves in Hector;
The one almost as infinite as all,
The other blank as nothing. Weigh him well,
And that which looks like pride is courtesy.
This Ajax is half made of Hector's blood:
In love whereof, half Hector stays at home;
Half heart, half hand, half Hector comes to seek
This blended knight, half Trojan and half Greek.

speeches.speech: A maiden battle, then? O, I perceive you.
lines: A maiden battle, then? O, I perceive you.

speeches.speech: Here is Sir Diomed. Go, gentle knight, Stand by our Ajax: as you and Lord AEneas Consent upon the order of their fight, So be it; either to the uttermost, Or else a breath: the combatants being kin Half stints their strife before their strokes begin.
lines: Here is Sir Diomed. Go, gentle knight,
Stand by our Ajax: as you and Lord AEneas
Consent upon the order of their fight,
So be it; either to the uttermost,
Or else a breath: the combatants being kin
Half stints their strife before their strokes begin.

speeches.speech: They are opposed already.
lines: They are opposed already.

speeches.speech: What Trojan is that same that looks so heavy?
lines: What Trojan is that same that looks so heavy?

speeches.speech: The youngest son of Priam, a true knight, Not yet mature, yet matchless, firm of word, Speaking in deeds and deedless in his tongue; Not soon provoked nor being provoked soon calm'd: His heart and hand both open and both free; For what he has he gives, what thinks he shows; Yet gives he not till judgment guide his bounty, Nor dignifies an impure thought with breath; Manly as Hector, but more dangerous; For Hector in his blaze of wrath subscribes To tender objects, but he in heat of action Is more vindicative than jealous love: They call him Troilus, and on him erect A second hope, as fairly built as Hector. Thus says AEneas; one that knows the youth Even to his inches, and with private soul Did in great Ilion thus translate him to me.
lines: The youngest son of Priam, a true knight,
Not yet mature, yet matchless, firm of word,
Speaking in deeds and deedless in his tongue;
Not soon provoked nor being provoked soon calm'd:
His heart and hand both open and both free;
For what he has he gives, what thinks he shows;
Yet gives he not till judgment guide his bounty,
Nor dignifies an impure thought with breath;
Manly as Hector, but more dangerous;
For Hector in his blaze of wrath subscribes
To tender objects, but he in heat of action
Is more vindicative than jealous love:
They call him Troilus, and on him erect
A second hope, as fairly built as Hector.
Thus says AEneas; one that knows the youth
Even to his inches, and with private soul
Did in great Ilion thus translate him to me.

speeches.speech: They are in action.
lines: They are in action.

speeches.speech: Now, Ajax, hold thine own!
lines: Now, Ajax, hold thine own!

speeches.speech: Hector, thou sleep'st; Awake thee!
lines: Hector, thou sleep'st;
Awake thee!

speeches.speech: His blows are well disposed: there, Ajax!
lines: His blows are well disposed: there, Ajax!

speeches.speech: You must no more.
lines: You must no more.

speeches.speech: Princes, enough, so please you.
lines: Princes, enough, so please you.

speeches.speech: I am not warm yet; let us fight again.
lines: I am not warm yet; let us fight again.

speeches.speech: As Hector pleases.
lines: As Hector pleases.

speeches.speech: Why, then will I no more: Thou art, great lord, my father's sister's son, A cousin-german to great Priam's seed; The obligation of our blood forbids A gory emulation 'twixt us twain: Were thy commixtion Greek and Trojan so That thou couldst say 'This hand is Grecian all, And this is Trojan; the sinews of this leg All Greek, and this all Troy; my mother's blood Runs on the dexter cheek, and this sinister Bounds in my father's;' by Jove multipotent, Thou shouldst not bear from me a Greekish member Wherein my sword had not impressure made Of our rank feud: but the just gods gainsay That any drop thou borrow'dst from thy mother, My sacred aunt, should by my mortal sword Be drain'd! Let me embrace thee, Ajax: By him that thunders, thou hast lusty arms; Hector would have them fall upon him thus: Cousin, all honour to thee!
lines: Why, then will I no more:
Thou art, great lord, my father's sister's son,
A cousin-german to great Priam's seed;
The obligation of our blood forbids
A gory emulation 'twixt us twain:
Were thy commixtion Greek and Trojan so
That thou couldst say 'This hand is Grecian all,
And this is Trojan; the sinews of this leg
All Greek, and this all Troy; my mother's blood
Runs on the dexter cheek, and this sinister
Bounds in my father's;' by Jove multipotent,
Thou shouldst not bear from me a Greekish member
Wherein my sword had not impressure made
Of our rank feud: but the just gods gainsay
That any drop thou borrow'dst from thy mother,
My sacred aunt, should by my mortal sword
Be drain'd! Let me embrace thee, Ajax:
By him that thunders, thou hast lusty arms;
Hector would have them fall upon him thus:
Cousin, all honour to thee!

speeches.speech: I thank thee, Hector Thou art too gentle and too free a man: I came to kill thee, cousin, and bear hence A great addition earned in thy death.
lines: I thank thee, Hector
Thou art too gentle and too free a man:
I came to kill thee, cousin, and bear hence
A great addition earned in thy death.

speeches.speech: Not Neoptolemus so mirable, On whose bright crest Fame with her loud'st Oyes Cries 'This is he,' could promise to himself A thought of added honour torn from Hector.
lines: Not Neoptolemus so mirable,
On whose bright crest Fame with her loud'st Oyes
Cries 'This is he,' could promise to himself
A thought of added honour torn from Hector.

speeches.speech: There is expectance here from both the sides, What further you will do.
lines: There is expectance here from both the sides,
What further you will do.

speeches.speech: We'll answer it; The issue is embracement: Ajax, farewell.
lines: We'll answer it;
The issue is embracement: Ajax, farewell.

speeches.speech: If I might in entreaties find success-- As seld I have the chance--I would desire My famous cousin to our Grecian tents.
lines: If I might in entreaties find success--
As seld I have the chance--I would desire
My famous cousin to our Grecian tents.

speeches.speech: 'Tis Agamemnon's wish, and great Achilles Doth long to see unarm'd the valiant Hector.
lines: 'Tis Agamemnon's wish, and great Achilles
Doth long to see unarm'd the valiant Hector.

speeches.speech: AEneas, call my brother Troilus to me, And signify this loving interview To the expecters of our Trojan part; Desire them home. Give me thy hand, my cousin; I will go eat with thee and see your knights.
lines: AEneas, call my brother Troilus to me,
And signify this loving interview
To the expecters of our Trojan part;
Desire them home. Give me thy hand, my cousin;
I will go eat with thee and see your knights.

speeches.speech: Great Agamemnon comes to meet us here.
lines: Great Agamemnon comes to meet us here.

speeches.speech: The worthiest of them tell me name by name; But for Achilles, mine own searching eyes Shall find him by his large and portly size.
lines: The worthiest of them tell me name by name;
But for Achilles, mine own searching eyes
Shall find him by his large and portly size.

speeches.speech: Worthy of arms! as welcome as to one That would be rid of such an enemy; But that's no welcome: understand more clear, What's past and what's to come is strew'd with husks And formless ruin of oblivion; But in this extant moment, faith and troth, Strain'd purely from all hollow bias-drawing, Bids thee, with most divine integrity, From heart of very heart, great Hector, welcome.
lines: Worthy of arms! as welcome as to one
That would be rid of such an enemy;
But that's no welcome: understand more clear,
What's past and what's to come is strew'd with husks
And formless ruin of oblivion;
But in this extant moment, faith and troth,
Strain'd purely from all hollow bias-drawing,
Bids thee, with most divine integrity,
From heart of very heart, great Hector, welcome.

speeches.speech: I thank thee, most imperious Agamemnon.
lines: I thank thee, most imperious Agamemnon.

speeches.speech: [To TROILUS] My well-famed lord of Troy, no less to you.
lines: [To TROILUS] My well-famed lord of Troy, no
less to you.

speeches.speech: Let me confirm my princely brother's greeting: You brace of warlike brothers, welcome hither.
lines: Let me confirm my princely brother's greeting:
You brace of warlike brothers, welcome hither.

speeches.speech: Who must we answer?
lines: Who must we answer?

speeches.speech: The noble Menelaus.
lines: The noble Menelaus.

speeches.speech: O, you, my lord? by Mars his gauntlet, thanks! Mock not, that I affect the untraded oath; Your quondam wife swears still by Venus' glove: She's well, but bade me not commend her to you.
lines: O, you, my lord? by Mars his gauntlet, thanks!
Mock not, that I affect the untraded oath;
Your quondam wife swears still by Venus' glove:
She's well, but bade me not commend her to you.

speeches.speech: Name her not now, sir; she's a deadly theme.
lines: Name her not now, sir; she's a deadly theme.

speeches.speech: O, pardon; I offend.
lines: O, pardon; I offend.

speeches.speech: I have, thou gallant Trojan, seen thee oft Labouring for destiny make cruel way Through ranks of Greekish youth, and I have seen thee, As hot as Perseus, spur thy Phrygian steed, Despising many forfeits and subduements, When thou hast hung thy advanced sword i' the air, Not letting it decline on the declined, That I have said to some my standers by 'Lo, Jupiter is yonder, dealing life!' And I have seen thee pause and take thy breath, When that a ring of Greeks have hemm'd thee in, Like an Olympian wrestling: this have I seen; But this thy countenance, still lock'd in steel, I never saw till now. I knew thy grandsire, And once fought with him: he was a soldier good; But, by great Mars, the captain of us all, Never saw like thee. Let an old man embrace thee; And, worthy warrior, welcome to our tents.
lines: I have, thou gallant Trojan, seen thee oft
Labouring for destiny make cruel way
Through ranks of Greekish youth, and I have seen thee,
As hot as Perseus, spur thy Phrygian steed,
Despising many forfeits and subduements,
When thou hast hung thy advanced sword i' the air,
Not letting it decline on the declined,
That I have said to some my standers by
'Lo, Jupiter is yonder, dealing life!'
And I have seen thee pause and take thy breath,
When that a ring of Greeks have hemm'd thee in,
Like an Olympian wrestling: this have I seen;
But this thy countenance, still lock'd in steel,
I never saw till now. I knew thy grandsire,
And once fought with him: he was a soldier good;
But, by great Mars, the captain of us all,
Never saw like thee. Let an old man embrace thee;
And, worthy warrior, welcome to our tents.

speeches.speech: 'Tis the old Nestor.
lines: 'Tis the old Nestor.

speeches.speech: Let me embrace thee, good old chronicle, That hast so long walk'd hand in hand with time: Most reverend Nestor, I am glad to clasp thee.
lines: Let me embrace thee, good old chronicle,
That hast so long walk'd hand in hand with time:
Most reverend Nestor, I am glad to clasp thee.

speeches.speech: I would my arms could match thee in contention, As they contend with thee in courtesy.
lines: I would my arms could match thee in contention,
As they contend with thee in courtesy.

speeches.speech: I would they could.
lines: I would they could.

speeches.speech: Ha! By this white beard, I'ld fight with thee to-morrow. Well, welcome, welcome! I have seen the time.
lines: Ha!
By this white beard, I'ld fight with thee to-morrow.
Well, welcome, welcome! I have seen the time.

speeches.speech: I wonder now how yonder city stands When we have here her base and pillar by us.
lines: I wonder now how yonder city stands
When we have here her base and pillar by us.

speeches.speech: I know your favour, Lord Ulysses, well. Ah, sir, there's many a Greek and Trojan dead, Since first I saw yourself and Diomed In Ilion, on your Greekish embassy.
lines: I know your favour, Lord Ulysses, well.
Ah, sir, there's many a Greek and Trojan dead,
Since first I saw yourself and Diomed
In Ilion, on your Greekish embassy.

speeches.speech: Sir, I foretold you then what would ensue: My prophecy is but half his journey yet; For yonder walls, that pertly front your town, Yond towers, whose wanton tops do buss the clouds, Must kiss their own feet.
lines: Sir, I foretold you then what would ensue:
My prophecy is but half his journey yet;
For yonder walls, that pertly front your town,
Yond towers, whose wanton tops do buss the clouds,
Must kiss their own feet.

speeches.speech: I must not believe you: There they stand yet, and modestly I think, The fall of every Phrygian stone will cost A drop of Grecian blood: the end crowns all, And that old common arbitrator, Time, Will one day end it.
lines: I must not believe you:
There they stand yet, and modestly I think,
The fall of every Phrygian stone will cost
A drop of Grecian blood: the end crowns all,
And that old common arbitrator, Time,
Will one day end it.

speeches.speech: So to him we leave it. Most gentle and most valiant Hector, welcome: After the general, I beseech you next To feast with me and see me at my tent.
lines: So to him we leave it.
Most gentle and most valiant Hector, welcome:
After the general, I beseech you next
To feast with me and see me at my tent.

speeches.speech: I shall forestall thee, Lord Ulysses, thou! Now, Hector, I have fed mine eyes on thee; I have with exact view perused thee, Hector, And quoted joint by joint.
lines: I shall forestall thee, Lord Ulysses, thou!
Now, Hector, I have fed mine eyes on thee;
I have with exact view perused thee, Hector,
And quoted joint by joint.

speeches.speech: Is this Achilles?
lines: Is this Achilles?

speeches.speech: I am Achilles.
lines: I am Achilles.

speeches.speech: Stand fair, I pray thee: let me look on thee.
lines: Stand fair, I pray thee: let me look on thee.

speeches.speech: Behold thy fill.
lines: Behold thy fill.

speeches.speech: Nay, I have done already.
lines: Nay, I have done already.

speeches.speech: Thou art too brief: I will the second time, As I would buy thee, view thee limb by limb.
